title: A.R.S. 32-1132.01: The $30,000 Recovery Fund Cap Per Claim
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1132.01 caps what Arizona's residential contractors' recovery fund pays at $30,000 per claim. The fund covers your actual damages only, and money you collect from other sources, like a bond or the contractor, is subtracted first. It does not pay for emotional distress or attorney fees.
bodyHtml:
Arizona's recovery fund will not write a blank check. A.R.S. 32-1132.01 sets a hard ceiling: $30,000 for any one claim. It also limits what the money can be used for. The fund pays your actual damages, meaning real out-of-pocket losses, and it subtracts anything you already collected from another source. This page explains the cap, how the math works, and the costs the fund will not touch, so your expectations match the law before you file.

The $30,000 per-claim ceiling

The headline number in this section is fixed and easy to miss in the legalese. The statute states it plainly:

The maximum individual award from the residential contractors' recovery fund is $30,000.

That is per claim, against the contractor's bad work on your home. Two things shape what you actually receive. First, the fund pays only actual damages you suffered as a direct result of the contractor's violation, not punishment money. Second, the statute requires the ROC to subtract what you recover elsewhere:

...not to exceed $30,000.

So if the contractor's bond or a court order already paid you part of your loss, that amount comes off the top, and the fund pays the difference up to the $30,000 limit.

What "actual damages" leaves out

Actual damages means the concrete cost to fix or finish the work, plus direct losses tied to the violation. It does not cover everything a lawsuit might. The recovery fund generally will not pay for emotional distress, pain and suffering, lost time, interest, or your attorney fees. Those may be available in a separate civil case, but the fund itself is limited to real, provable repair and completion costs.

What this means for you

Picture a botched kitchen and bath remodel in Scottsdale. A second contractor quotes $52,000 to tear out and redo the failed work. You win an order against the original builder. The recovery fund reimburses your actual damages up to a hard cap of $30,000 per contractor, minus anything you have already recovered. Here the $8,000 the contractor's bond paid comes off first, leaving $44,000 in unpaid damages. Since that is still more than the cap, the fund pays you its $30,000 maximum, and $14,000 of the loss stays uncovered.

The takeaway: treat the recovery fund as a backstop, not full insurance on a large custom project. On a $1.5 million Rio Verde build, $30,000 is a small fraction. The real protection is hiring a licensed, financially sound builder up front so you never need the fund. Jematell Homes builds with that in mind, but knowing the cap helps you size the risk on any contractor you consider.

How this connects to the rest of the fund rules

This per-claim cap sits inside a larger limit. Even if several homeowners file against the same contractor, A.R.S. 32-1139 caps the fund's total payout per license at $200,000. To claim at all you must be eligible under A.R.S. 32-1132 and file in time under A.R.S. 32-1133 . For the steps, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim .

Full text and source

Read the current version, including how recoveries from other sources are applied, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1132.01 on azleg.gov .

How the cap applies to your loss depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1133: The 2-Year Deadline to Sue for a Recovery Fund Claim
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1133 gives you two years to start the lawsuit that can later tap Arizona's recovery fund. The clock runs from the contractor's harmful act or from the date you occupied the home. You must also notify the ROC in writing within 30 days of filing that lawsuit.
bodyHtml:
A recovery fund claim has a deadline, and missing it can sink an otherwise strong case. A.R.S. 32-1133 gives you two years to start the underlying lawsuit, and a separate 30-day window to tell the ROC about it. The two-year clock runs from the contractor's harmful act or from the date you moved in, whichever is the right marker for your situation. This page explains both deadlines and how they fit together, so you do not lose the fund on a technicality.

The two-year clock

The recovery fund pays out only after a court order in a lawsuit against the contractor. That lawsuit has to be filed in time. Here is the operative rule:

An action for a judgment that may subsequently result in an order for collection from the residential contractors' recovery fund may not be commenced later than two years after the date of the commission of the act by the contractor that is the cause of the injury or from the date of occupancy.

Read it carefully: the limit applies to commencing the action, the lawsuit itself, not the fund claim that follows. You measure the two years from the contractor's harmful act, or from the date of occupancy of the home. For new-home defects, occupancy is often the marker that matters, because that is when you took the house and the clock can start.

The 30-day ROC notice

Filing the lawsuit is not enough on its own. You also have to alert the Registrar of Contractors quickly:

...notify the registrar in writing to this effect within thirty calendar days after the commencement of the action.

So once you file suit, you have 30 calendar days to put the ROC on notice in writing. Skip that step and you can win your case yet still be blocked from the fund. Calendar this the day you file, not the day you remember.

What this means for you

Say you take occupancy of a custom home in Apache Junction, and within the first year the roof framing proves defective. To keep the recovery fund alive, you must start your lawsuit against the builder within two years of that occupancy date (or of the defective act), then notify the ROC within 30 days of filing. Wait three years to sue and the door is likely shut, even with clear proof of bad work.

Two practical moves. First, do not sit on a known defect. Two years passes faster than it sounds when you are getting repair bids and trying to work it out with the builder. Second, treat the 30-day ROC notice as a hard checklist item the moment you file. For the related construction-defect timelines that can run alongside this, see our new home builder warranty in Arizona page.

How this connects to the rest of the fund rules

This deadline only matters if you are an eligible claimant under A.R.S. 32-1132 , and what you can collect is capped by A.R.S. 32-1132.01 and A.R.S. 32-1139 . A separate, shorter deadline applies if you instead file a complaint with the ROC under A.R.S. 32-1162 . For the full path, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim and the ROC glossary entry .

Full text and source

Read the current version on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1133 on azleg.gov .

How these deadlines apply to a specific claim depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1139: The $200,000 Recovery Fund Cap Per License
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1139 caps the total Arizona recovery fund can pay out for any single contractor's license at $200,000. When several homeowners file against the same bad contractor and their claims add up past that limit, they split the money pro rata. The fund pays nothing more once $200,000 is reached.
bodyHtml:
There is a second ceiling on Arizona's recovery fund that homeowners often miss. A.R.S. 32-1139 caps the fund's total liability at $200,000 for any one contractor's license. The per-claim limit of $30,000 protects you individually. This per-license limit protects the fund when one bad contractor harms many homeowners at once. Once the combined payouts hit $200,000, the fund stops, and claimants share what is left. This page explains the cap and how the math hits real claimants.

The $200,000 per-license ceiling

This number is fixed in the statute and sits on top of the individual cap. The operative language:

The liability of the fund shall not exceed two hundred thousand dollars for any one residential contractor's license.

That is the total the fund will ever pay for a single license, no matter how many homeowners were harmed. When the claims add up to more than $200,000, the law does not pay them in full. It splits the pool:

...the claims shall be paid based on a pro rata share of the common liability.

Pro rata means proportional. If valid claims total $400,000 against one license, every claimant collects about half of their otherwise eligible amount, because the $200,000 has to stretch across all of them. After the cap is reached, no further fund recovery is allowed against that license.

What this means for you

Picture a developer-builder in Casa Grande who takes deposits on ten homes, does failing work on all of them, then folds. Each family has a $30,000 fund-eligible claim, so the claims total $300,000. The fund caps out at $200,000 for that one license. The ten families do not each get $30,000. They split $200,000 pro rata, which works out to about $20,000 each instead. The math is real, and it punishes the families who file last as much as the rest.

Two takeaways. First, the recovery fund is strongest when only one or two homeowners are harmed by a contractor, and weakest in a mass failure, which is exactly when people lean on it most. Second, do not assume a contractor with the right license is safe just because the fund exists. The cap means a large or repeat failure can blow past the fund entirely. Vetting the builder's track record and finances up front, which is how Jematell Homes operates, beats relying on a shared pool of money.

How this connects to the rest of the fund rules

This per-license cap works together with the per-claim cap in A.R.S. 32-1132.01 : your claim is limited to $30,000, and the license total is limited to $200,000, whichever bites first. To recover at all you must be eligible under A.R.S. 32-1132 , define your claim under A.R.S. 32-1131 , and file in time under A.R.S. 32-1133 . For the process, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim and the ROC glossary entry .

Full text and source

Read the current version on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1139 on azleg.gov .

How the cap applies when multiple claims compete depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1151: Contracting Without a License Is Illegal in Arizona
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1151 makes it illegal to act as a contractor in Arizona, or to offer to, without a contractor's license in good standing from the Registrar of Contractors. Pulling a permit or hiring workers for a job counts as proof you were acting as a contractor.
bodyHtml:
If someone builds, remodels, or repairs a home in Arizona for pay, they need a contractor's license. A.R.S. 32-1151 is the rule that makes working without one against the law. It applies to people and to companies, and it covers offering to do the work, not just doing it. This page explains who the rule binds, how the state proves a violation, and why it protects you as a homeowner.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1151 makes it unlawful to act as a contractor, or to offer to act as one, without a license in good standing from the Arizona Registrar of Contractors (the ROC, the state agency that licenses and disciplines contractors). A license is "in good standing" when it is active, not suspended, and not revoked. The ban reaches any person, partnership, corporation, or other organization. Here is the core of the rule:

It is unlawful for any person... to engage in the business of, submit a bid or respond to a request for proposal or qualifications for, act or offer to act in the capacity of or purport to have the capacity of a contractor without having a contractor's license in good standing.

The statute also makes it easy for the state to prove someone was acting as a contractor. Pulling a building permit, or putting workers on a job, counts as evidence that a contract existed:

Evidence of securing a permit from a governmental agency or the employment of any person on a construction project shall be accepted in any court as prima facie evidence of existence of a contract.

"Prima facie" means it is treated as proof unless the person shows otherwise.

What it means for your home build

The practical takeaway is simple: before you sign with anyone to build or remodel in Scottsdale, Cave Creek, or anywhere in Arizona, confirm they hold an active ROC license for the work. You can check any license free at the Registrar's site. An unlicensed person who quotes you a job is already breaking this law by offering to do it.

For example, suppose a handyman offers to frame and finish a 600-square-foot casita addition for $40,000. Framing a habitable structure needs a license. If that handyman has none, the offer alone violates 32-1151, and you take on real risk by hiring them: no Recovery Fund coverage, no bond, and weak recourse if the work fails. There is a narrow owner-builder path where you build your own home without a license, but that is a separate exemption with its own limits, not a way to hire an unlicensed crew.

Penalties land on the unlicensed contractor, not the homeowner. A first offense is a class 1 misdemeanor, and repeat unlicensed contracting can rise to a class 6 felony. The ROC also publishes names of cited unlicensed contractors. That is why a licensed builder is the floor, not a bonus.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the front door to Arizona's contractor licensing system. It pairs directly with A.R.S. 32-1153, which says an unlicensed contractor cannot even sue you to collect payment. It also sets up the contract rules in A.R.S. 32-1158, which spell out what a licensed contractor must put in writing. When you build, the same license that satisfies 32-1151 is what your city checks before issuing a permit. For what belongs in the agreement once you hire a licensed builder, see our guide on what to include in a custom home contract , and to compare pricing structures, see cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including any amendments, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1151 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific job depends on the facts, so verify any license at roc.az.gov and confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors — License Lookup and Verification

title: A.R.S. 32-1152: The License Bond Every Arizona Contractor Must Carry
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1152 requires every Arizona contractor to file a license bond, or a cash deposit, with the Registrar of Contractors before the license is issued or renewed. The bond amount scales with license class and work volume, from $1,000 for small specialty work up to six figures for large general contractors.
bodyHtml:
Every licensed contractor in Arizona has to post a license bond before the Registrar of Contractors (ROC) will issue or renew the license. A.R.S. 32-1152 sets this rule. A license bond is a financial guarantee a surety company sells to the contractor; if the contractor breaks the rules or leaves a job unfinished, an owner can make a claim against it. This page explains who must bond, how big the bond is, and what it actually protects.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1152 says the ROC cannot grant an original license, and cannot renew one, unless the contractor has a bond (or an approved cash deposit) in full force. The bond runs to the State of Arizona for the benefit of anyone the contractor harms. Here is the core rule:

Before granting an original contractor's license, the registrar shall require... a surety bond... or a cash deposit.

The dollar amount is not one flat number. It scales with the license class (residential vs. commercial, general vs. specialty) and with the contractor's annual work volume. The figures the ROC uses by rule run roughly like this:

  • Residential general (B class): $5,000 to $15,000.
  • Residential specialty: $1,000 to $7,500.
  • Commercial general: $5,000 up to $100,000 for the largest firms.
  • Commercial specialty: $2,500 up to $50,000.

The bigger the company's yearly volume, the bigger the bond. A contractor who also wants to qualify for the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund posts an additional bond tied to that fund.

What the bond pays for

The bond is not insurance for the contractor. It protects you and the people the contractor hires. If a residential contractor takes your deposit and walks, does defective work, or fails to pay a supplier, a homeowner, subcontractor, or material supplier can file a claim against the bond. For example, on a $40,000 kitchen remodel in Mesa, if your contractor abandons the job after collecting $18,000, you can claim against the bond up to its face amount. A $7,500 residential specialty bond will not cover a $40,000 loss in full, which is exactly why the bond is a floor, not a ceiling, and why the separate Recovery Fund exists.

The statute also controls how a bond can shrink or end. A surety that wants to cancel must give the ROC written notice not less than thirty days before the cancellation takes effect, and if a cash deposit is drawn down the contractor has thirty days to top it back up. If the bond lapses, the license cannot renew.

How this connects to other rules

The bond is one of three layers of financial protection in Title 32, Chapter 10. The bond here is the first claim source. A.R.S. 32-1132 opens the Residential Contractors' Recovery Fund for losses a residential contractor's bond cannot cover, and A.R.S. 32-1132.01 caps a Recovery Fund claim at $30,000. The license the bond protects is issued under A.R.S. 32-1124 and kept current under the renewal rules in A.R.S. 32-1125. Before you sign, confirm your builder is bonded and licensed by checking the Registrar of Contractors license lookup, walked through in how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC . If a job goes wrong, the bond and the fund are both in play, as covered in how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact bond schedule and amendments, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1152 on azleg.gov .

The right bond amount and how a claim is decided depend on the facts, so confirm details with the ROC or a qualified professional before relying on it.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors — Bonds and License Requirements

title: A.R.S. 32-1153: An Unlicensed Contractor Cannot Sue to Get Paid
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1153 bars a contractor from suing in Arizona to collect payment unless they prove they held the proper license both when the contract was signed and when the claim arose. No valid license means no right to recover compensation in court.
bodyHtml:
A contractor who works without a license in Arizona loses the right to sue you for the money. A.R.S. 32-1153 says a contractor cannot win a collection case in court unless they prove they were properly licensed when the contract was signed and when the claim came up. This page explains the rule, what "prove" means, and why it gives homeowners a strong hand.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1153 turns a contractor's license into a gate they must pass before any court will hear a payment claim. The contractor has to both allege (claim in the lawsuit) and prove they held a valid license at two specific moments: when the contract was entered into, and when the cause of action arose. Here is the operative rule:

No contractor... shall act as agent or commence or maintain any action in any court of the state for collection of compensation for the performance of any act for which a license is required by this chapter without alleging and proving that the contracting party whose contract gives rise to the claim was a duly licensed contractor when the contract sued upon was entered into and when the alleged cause of action arose.

In plain terms, an unlicensed contractor, or one whose license had lapsed at the wrong moment, cannot use the courts to force payment. The license must have been valid both when the deal was struck and when the dispute matured.

What it means for your home build

This statute is one of the strongest protections a homeowner has. If a contractor was not licensed for the work, they cannot sue you to collect, full stop. That changes the balance of power if a dispute starts.

For example, say a tile setter contracts to finish a $25,000 bathroom and pool-deck job in Fountain Hills but never held a license for that scope. If they walk off and then sue you for the unpaid balance, 32-1153 blocks the case: they cannot prove the license element, so the court will not award them the money. Arizona courts read this rule strictly. Even a license that lapsed for a short window at the wrong time can sink a contractor's claim.

There is a flip side worth knowing. Hiring an unlicensed person is still a bad deal for you. You give up the ROC complaint process and Recovery Fund coverage, and a mechanics lien fight can still get messy. The smart move is to verify the license before you sign, not to rely on this statute as a rescue later.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the enforcement teeth behind A.R.S. 32-1151, which makes unlicensed contracting illegal in the first place. Together they say: working without a license is unlawful, and you cannot sue to get paid for it. It also pairs with the contract rules in A.R.S. 32-1158, since a clean, written, signed contract with a licensed builder is what keeps both sides out of court. To get that agreement right from the start, see our guide on what to include in a custom home contract and the breakdown of cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including any amendments, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1153 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific dispute depends on the facts, so verify any license at roc.az.gov and confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors — License Lookup and Verification

title: A.R.S. 32-1154: Grounds to Suspend or Revoke a Contractor's License
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1154 lists the acts that let Arizona's ROC suspend or revoke a contractor's license. The list covers abandoning a job, ignoring the plans, poor workmanship, building code violations, fraud, and not paying for materials over $750. The ROC can also fine a contractor up to $500 per violation.
bodyHtml:
If a contractor does something wrong on your Arizona home, A.R.S. 32-1154 is the list that lets the Registrar of Contractors (the ROC ) act against their license. It names the specific acts and omissions that are grounds for discipline, from walking off the job to violating the building code to not paying suppliers. This page translates the key grounds into plain terms and shows which ones a homeowner most often relies on when filing a complaint.

The acts that can cost a contractor their license

The statute opens by telling licensees what they may not do:

The holder of a license or any person named on a license pursuant to this chapter may not commit any of the following acts or omissions...

The grounds most relevant to homeowners include:

  • Abandonment. Walking off or refusing to perform a job without a legal excuse. The glossary covers this at abandonment .
  • Departure from the plans. Disregarding the plans or specifications in any material way that harms someone.
  • Building code violations. Violating a state or local building code in any material respect that is prejudicial to another.
  • Not finishing for the contract price. Failing in a material respect to complete the project for the price stated in the contract.
  • Fraud. A fraudulent act by the contractor that substantially injures another person.
  • Unpaid bills. Failing to pay money over $750 when due for materials or services. The statute puts it directly:

Failure ... to pay monies in excess of $750 when due for materials or services rendered...

These are the workhorse grounds. Most homeowner complaints land on poor workmanship that violates the code, abandonment, or a job left unfinished for the agreed price.

The fines that come with it

Discipline is not only suspension or revocation. The ROC can also fine the contractor. The statute sets the amounts:

...not to exceed $500 on a contractor for each violation.

Contracting while suspended carries a higher fine, up to $1,000 per violation. These civil penalties are separate from any money you recover for your own losses through a lawsuit or the recovery fund.

What this means for you

Say a contractor in Carefree pours your foundation off the approved plans, fails inspection, and then stops returning calls. That is potentially three grounds at once under this section: departure from the plans, a code violation, and abandonment. You do not have to prove all three. Any single ground can support ROC discipline. When you file your complaint, point to the specific acts here so the ROC can match your facts to the statute.

The practical move is to document everything: the signed contract and plans, the failed inspection notice, photos, and dates of missed work. That record is what turns a frustration into a provable A.R.S. 32-1154 violation. A licensed, careful builder like Jematell Homes avoids these grounds by building to the approved plans and passing inspections the first time.

How this connects to the rest of contractors law

These grounds are what you allege when you file a complaint with the ROC under A.R.S. 32-1155 . That complaint has its own deadline, set by A.R.S. 32-1162 . If the contractor's bad work meets these grounds and you cannot collect, the recovery fund may apply. For the full path, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim .

Full text and source

Read the complete list of grounds on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1154 on azleg.gov .

Whether specific conduct meets a ground for discipline depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1154 (Grounds for suspension or revocation of license; civil penalty)
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (AZ ROC)

title: A.R.S. 32-1155: How a Contractor Complaint Gets Filed and Resolved
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1155 sets how a contractor complaint moves through Arizona's ROC. You file a written complaint charging a licensee with an act that is grounds for discipline. The ROC investigates and may issue a citation, and the contractor then has 10 days to file a written answer.
bodyHtml:
When a contractor wrongs you in Arizona, A.R.S. 32-1155 is the section that starts the formal process at the Registrar of Contractors (the ROC ). It says your complaint must be in writing and must charge the licensee with an act that is grounds for discipline. From there the ROC investigates and can issue a citation, and the contractor gets a short window to respond. This page explains each step so you know what happens after you hit submit.

What the complaint has to be

The process begins with a written complaint, not a phone call. The statute describes it:

A written complaint with the registrar charging a licensee with the commission of an act that is cause for suspension or revocation of a license...

Two things stand out. It must be written, and it must charge a specific act that is a ground for discipline. That ties directly to the list in A.R.S. 32-1154 . A vague "I'm unhappy" does not move the case. Naming the act, like abandonment or a code violation, does.

What the ROC does next

After you file, the ROC investigates and decides whether to act. The statute gives it discretion:

After investigation, in its sole discretion, [the registrar] may issue a citation directing the licensee, within ten days after service of the citation on the licensee, to appear by filing with the registrar the licensee's written answer...

So the ROC reviews your complaint, and if it sees cause, it issues a citation to the contractor. The contractor then has 10 days after being served to file a written answer. This is the contractor's chance to respond, deny, or explain. The case proceeds from there, often toward a fix, a settlement, or a hearing.

What this means for you

Imagine a contractor in Phoenix leaves your remodel unfinished and ignores your calls. You file a written complaint with the ROC that charges abandonment and points to the unfinished work for the contracted price. The ROC investigates. If it agrees there is cause, it cites the contractor, who must answer within 10 days. Many contractors choose to fix the work rather than risk discipline, which is often the homeowner's fastest path to a repair.

Three practical moves. First, put it in writing and be specific about the act. Second, attach your evidence: the contract, photos, inspection notices, and a timeline. Third, file through the ROC's online complaint portal, which structures the information the way the statute expects. The clearer your filing, the more likely the ROC acts on it.

How this connects to the rest of contractors law

The complaint here charges grounds defined in A.R.S. 32-1154 , and it must be filed within the deadline set by A.R.S. 32-1162 . If discipline alone does not make you whole, the recovery fund may follow. For the complete walkthrough, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim and the new home builder warranty in Arizona .

Full text and source

Read the current version, including the citation and answer procedure, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1155 on azleg.gov .

How the complaint process applies to your dispute depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1155 (Complaint; citation; answer)
  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (AZ ROC)

title: A.R.S. 32-1158: What an Arizona Home Construction Contract Must Include
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1158 lists what a contractor must put in any Arizona construction contract over $1,000: names, addresses, license number, dates, scope, total price, deposit, and progress payments, plus a bold complaint-rights notice. The contractor must hand you signed copies at signing.
bodyHtml:
Any Arizona construction contract over $1,000 must contain a specific set of terms, set by A.R.S. 32-1158. The contractor has to put your names, the price, the scope, the payment schedule, and a bold notice about your complaint rights in writing, and hand you signed copies when you sign. This page lists every required term and explains what each one protects.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1158 applies to contracts that exceed $1,000. It sets a floor for what the written agreement must contain, requires the contractor to give you signed copies, and treats cash payments specially. Here is the heart of the requirement:

A written contract entered into between an owner and a contractor that exceeds one thousand dollars shall contain the following...

The statute then lists the mandatory terms. Below is the full checklist, in plain language.

The required contract terms

  1. The contractor's identity and license. Name, business address, and ROC license number.
  2. The owner and the jobsite. Your name and mailing address, plus the jobsite address or legal description.
  3. The contract date. The date you and the contractor entered into the agreement.
  4. The estimated completion date. The estimated date all work under the contract will be finished.
  5. The scope of work. A description of the work to be performed.
  6. The total price. The total dollar amount you will pay the contractor for all the work, including all applicable taxes.
  7. Any deposit. The dollar amount of any advance deposit paid or scheduled to be paid.
  8. The progress payments. The dollar amount of each progress payment and the stage of construction at which the contractor may collect it.
  9. A complaint-rights notice. A statement of your right to file a complaint with the Registrar of Contractors, including how to contact the ROC, set in at least ten-point bold type and signed by both parties.

The statute also requires the contractor to give you a signed copy of every document you sign, and to provide a receipt for any cash payment. One more point matters: if a contractor leaves something out, the contract is still valid. The law states the contract "is not invalid solely because" it fails to meet these requirements, but the omission can be grounds for ROC discipline against the contractor.

What it means for your home build

Treat this list as your minimum checklist before signing. For example, on a $650,000 custom home in Cave Creek, the contract must name your builder's ROC license number, give a total price with tax, set an estimated completion date, and tie each payment to a construction stage (foundation, framing dry-in, drywall, final). If a builder hands you a one-page quote with a price and a start date but no progress-payment stages and no complaint notice, it does not meet 32-1158.

Two of the required items deserve extra attention. The progress payment schedule (item 8) is your protection against paying ahead of completed work; it should read like a draw schedule tied to real milestones. And while 32-1158 does not require a change-order clause, every term here can shift mid-build, so pair it with a written change order process so price and scope changes are documented, not verbal.

How this connects to other rules

This section defines the paperwork; the licensing sections decide who is even allowed to sign it. A.R.S. 32-1151 makes contracting without a license illegal, and A.R.S. 32-1153 stops an unlicensed contractor from suing for payment, so the license number in item 1 is doing real work. The payment-stage rule in item 8 connects to the prompt-payment timing in A.R.S. 32-1182. For a fuller walkthrough of building a strong agreement, see what to include in a custom home contract , how pricing is structured in cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts , and how payments line up with financing in construction loan draw schedules explained .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including any amendments and the exact statutory wording of each required term, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1158 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific contract depends on the facts, so confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1158.01: Arizona's Rules for Swimming Pool and Spa Contracts
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1158.01 caps how a pool or spa contractor can collect payment in Arizona: no more than 15 percent up front, then staged payments tied to excavation, structure and plumbing, and decking, each capped at 25 percent. It also requires written change orders and an offer of completion and payment bonds.
bodyHtml:
Building a pool in Arizona comes with its own payment-protection statute. A.R.S. 32-1158.01 limits how much a pool or spa contractor can collect up front and ties every later payment to a stage of construction you can see in the ground. It exists because a pool is a high-deposit job, and the law does not want you paying for a pool you do not yet have. This page lays out the exact payment caps and the other protections the section builds in.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1158.01 sets a maximum payment schedule for any contract to build a swimming pool or spa. The contractor can ask for less, but not more, at each stage. The staged caps are:

not more than fifteen percent of the original contract price

up front before work starts, then:

  • Up to 25 percent after excavation is complete.
  • Up to 25 percent after the structural concrete (gunite or shotcrete) and rough plumbing are installed.
  • Up to 25 percent after the decking is installed.
  • The remaining balance only as the finishing work (plaster, tile, equipment, water) is completed.

For example, on a $90,000 backyard pool in Scottsdale, the most your contractor can collect before a shovel hits dirt is $13,500. After excavation, another $22,500 at most, and so on down the schedule. Each draw lines up with completed, visible work.

The other protections built in

The payment caps are the headline, but the statute does three more things. First, it requires the contractor to offer you the option to request payment and performance bonds in a form the ROC approves, which secure completion of the work and payment of subcontractors and suppliers. You pay for the bonds, and the contractor cannot add a surcharge on top.

Second, any change to the work has to be a written change order: "any changes, additions or deletions to the work specified in the original contract shall be included in a written change order." A verbal "let's also add a baja shelf" does not bind anyone until it is written down with a price.

Third, the contractor must give you statutory disclosure notices in at least ten-point type about your rights and dispute-resolution options. A contractor who ignores these rules commits a violation under A.R.S. 32-1154(A), which is grounds for ROC discipline. The parties can adjust the payment terms only if a performance bond is furnished.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the pool-specific cousin of the general contract rules. A.R.S. 32-1158 lists what any construction contract over $1,000 must contain, and 32-1158.01 layers the pool payment caps on top. The staged-draw idea here works like the draw schedule on a home build, and the written-change rule matches a standard change order process. Because the contractor must be licensed by the Registrar of Contractors , a violation of these caps can support a complaint under A.R.S. 32-1155. For pricing context, see the cost to add a pool to a new home in Arizona , and for general contract terms see what to include in a custom home contract .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact payment schedule and notice wording, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1158.01 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific pool contract depends on the facts, so confirm details with the ROC or a qualified professional before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1158.02: Arizona Storm-Damage Repair Contract Rules
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1158.02 governs Arizona home repair contracts paid by storm-damage insurance. It gives you the right to cancel within four business days for any reason, or within 72 hours after your insurer denies the claim, and caps any down payment at 50 percent of the contract.
bodyHtml:
After a monsoon or hailstorm, A.R.S. 32-1158.02 protects you when a contractor offers to repair your home and the work will be paid by your property insurance. It gives you a right to cancel, limits how much you can be asked to pay up front, and bars high-pressure tactics. This page explains those protections and when they apply.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1158.02 covers contracts for the residential repair or replacement of damage from a catastrophic storm, where the work falls under contractor licensing and the homeowner is filing a property-insurance claim. It is built for the storm-chaser scenario: a roofer or repair crew knocks on doors after a storm and offers to "handle your insurance." Two cancellation rights anchor the section.

You can walk away for any reason in the first four business days:

You may cancel this contract at any time, for any reason, within four business days after signing this contract.

And you can cancel after the insurer says no:

You may cancel this contract at any time within seventy-two hours after you have been notified that your insurer has denied your claim to pay for the goods and services to be provided under this contract.

The statute also caps the up-front payment. The contract may not require more than half down:

The down payment section on the execution of a contract entered into pursuant to this section shall not require more than fifty per cent of the total contract.

On top of that, the contractor cannot promise that your insurer will approve the claim, must use written change orders for any change, and generally cannot negotiate the claim for you unless authorized and uncompensated.

What it means for your home build

These rules matter most after Arizona's monsoon season, when hail and wind hit roofs, stucco, and windows across Phoenix, Apache Junction, and the East Valley. For example, say a hailstorm dents your roof and a crew offers a $28,000 replacement "at no cost once insurance pays." Under 32-1158.02, you have four business days to cancel for any reason, no penalty. If your insurer later denies the claim, a fresh 72-hour cancellation window opens once you are notified. And no one can require more than $14,000 (50 percent) down on that $28,000 job.

The practical takeaway: never let a storm-chaser rush you into signing the day they show up. Get the ROC license number, keep the down payment within the cap, and insist that any change to scope or price come as a written change order . A promise that "insurance will definitely cover it" is exactly the assurance this statute forbids.

How this connects to other rules

This section is a specialized companion to A.R.S. 32-1158, the general rule on what every Arizona construction contract over $1,000 must include. Where 32-1158 sets the baseline terms, 32-1158.02 adds storm-and-insurance protections on top. The licensing backbone still applies: under A.R.S. 32-1151 the repair crew must hold a contractor's license , and under A.R.S. 32-1153 an unlicensed one cannot sue you for payment. To build a solid agreement in any situation, see what to include in a custom home contract and the comparison of cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including any amendments and the full disclosure language, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1158.02 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific repair contract depends on the facts, so confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1159.01: Void Indemnity Clauses in Arizona Home Contracts
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1159.01 makes a clause in an Arizona home construction or design contract void when it tries to force one party to cover the other party's own negligence. You cannot be required to pay for damage your builder or architect caused through their own fault.
bodyHtml:
One common contract trick shows up in Arizona home construction and design contracts: a clause that makes you pay for damage someone else caused through their own carelessness. A.R.S. 32-1159.01 shuts that trick down. Any term that shifts a party's own negligence onto the other side is void. This page explains what the rule blocks and why it protects everyone on a build.

What this statute says

An indemnity clause is a contract term where one party agrees to cover, or "hold harmless," another party for certain losses. A.R.S. 32-1159.01 says that in a construction contract or an architect-engineer professional services contract involving a dwelling (a home), an indemnity clause cannot force a party to pay for losses caused by the other party's own negligence. The statute treats that kind of clause as against public policy. Here is the operative language:

is against the public policy of this state and is void only to the extent that it purports to insure, to indemnify or to hold harmless the promisee from or against liability for loss or damage resulting from the negligence of the promisee.

Two details matter. First, the clause is void "only to the extent" it shifts the promisee's own negligence; the rest of the indemnity term can still stand. Second, "dwelling" takes its meaning from A.R.S. 12-1361, the Purchaser Dwelling Act, so this covers homes and their improvements, not commercial towers.

What it means for your home build

This rule keeps responsibility where it belongs: with the party whose mistake caused the harm. For example, suppose a contract says you, the homeowner, must indemnify your architect for any claim arising on the project. The architect then makes a design error that cracks a foundation slab on Arizona's expansive clay soil. Under 32-1159.01, the architect cannot use that clause to make you pay for their own negligent design. The void rule strikes that shift.

It cuts both ways and protects subcontractors too. A general contractor cannot write a subcontract that forces a framer to indemnify the GC for the GC's own negligence on a home. The legal name for the loss this section deals with is often tied to a construction defect claim, where the fight is over whose fault the failure was. Because indemnity language is dense, read it before signing, and treat any "you cover all claims" clause as a red flag worth a second look.

How this connects to other rules

This section sits alongside the other contract rules in Article 5. A.R.S. 32-1158 lists what your home contract must include, and 32-1159.01 polices what it cannot enforce. The negligence question it turns on is the same one behind many construction defect disputes, which Arizona handles through the Purchaser Dwelling Act in Title 12. Licensing still frames it all: under A.R.S. 32-1151 the builder must be licensed. For drafting a fair agreement, see what to include in a custom home contract and cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the full list of exceptions and definitions, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1159.01 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific clause depends on the facts, so confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

Sources

title: A.R.S. 32-1160: The Notice You Must Send Before Suing Over Termite Damage
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1160 makes you give a termite-control contractor written notice at least 30 days before you sue over termite damage. The contractor then has 10 days to inspect or offer a settlement, and any agreed repairs must be finished within 45 days of your written acceptance.
bodyHtml:
If a termite-control contractor treated your home and termites still caused damage, A.R.S. 32-1160 says you cannot go straight to court. You first have to send the contractor written notice and give them a chance to fix it. This pre-suit notice rule exists to settle termite disputes through repair instead of litigation. This page walks through every deadline in the process and what happens if you skip a step.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1160 requires a claimant to deliver written notice to the contractor at least 30 days before filing a lawsuit for termite damage. The notice has to spell out the problem in reasonable detail. Here is the core timing rule:

the claimant shall... give written notice... at least thirty days before filing an action

Once the contractor receives that notice, a clock starts on their side too. The contractor has 10 days to either ask for access to inspect the property or send you a written settlement offer describing the repair and treatment work they propose. If you accept a settlement offer in writing, the contractor must complete the agreed repairs within 45 days of receiving your acceptance, unless you cause the delay or something outside the contractor's control gets in the way.

What the deadlines mean for you

Walk it through with real dates. For example, suppose you discover termite damage in your Cave Creek home on June 1 and the company had treated the house under a warranty. You send written notice on June 2 describing the damaged areas. The contractor has until roughly June 12 to ask to inspect or to offer a settlement. If they offer to treat and repair the framing at their expense and you accept in writing on June 20, the repairs must be done by about August 4 (45 days later).

Two consequences make following the steps important. First, the notice and inspection are not optional courtesies. If you fail to give written notice, or you refuse to let the contractor inspect, the statute creates a rebuttable presumption that the damage could have been mitigated, which can shrink what you recover. Second, the 30-day notice is not required if you would otherwise blow past the statute of limitations, or if you are raising the termite claim as a counterclaim. In those situations you can file first.

How this connects to other rules

This section is a homeowner-protection mirror of the termite contractor's licensing duties under the Registrar of Contractors. Termite and pest control on structures is licensed work, so the same complaint and discipline tools apply. If a contractor refuses to honor the process, you can also file a complaint with the Registrar of Contractors under A.R.S. 32-1155, and a pattern of bad work is grounds for discipline under A.R.S. 32-1154. Because this is a structural construction defect question, the same evidence (inspection reports, photos, the original treatment contract) drives both the repair offer and any ROC complaint. For how the complaint side works, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim , and to confirm the company is licensed, see how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact notice and offer wording, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1160 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific termite claim depends on the facts, so confirm your situation with a qualified attorney before relying on it.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors — Homeowner Resources

title: A.R.S. 32-1161: What a Contractor Can Do After a License Suspension
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1161 covers a contractor whose Arizona license was suspended. The ROC must reinstate the license once the contractor proves the loss behind the suspension is fully paid or the judgment's renewal terms are met, and the suspended contractor may still do warranty or corrective work, but only for free.
bodyHtml:
When the Arizona Registrar of Contractors suspends a license, the contractor is not in permanent limbo. A.R.S. 32-1161 sets out two things: exactly how a suspended contractor earns the license back, and the one kind of work they are still allowed to do while suspended. The short version is that the path back runs through making the homeowner whole, and the only work permitted in the meantime is free warranty or corrective work. This page explains both halves and why they protect the homeowner on the other side of the dispute.

What this statute does

A license suspension under A.R.S. 32-1154 usually follows a finding that a contractor harmed a customer, such as abandoning a job or doing defective work. Section 32-1161 has two subsections that pick up from there.

Subsection A handles reinstatement. The Registrar must renew the suspended license once the contractor either complies with the renewal terms in a court judgment, or, if there is no judgment, makes a proper showing that the full loss behind the suspension has been satisfied.

After suspending the license... the registrar shall renew it... on proper showing that all loss caused by the act or omission for which the license was suspended has been fully satisfied.

Subsection B addresses what the contractor can still do while suspended. The answer is narrow: warranty work or other corrective work, and only without compensation.

After suspending the license pursuant to section 32-1154, the licensee may perform, without compensation, warranty work or other corrective work.

What it means for your home build

Say a builder who worked on homes in Casa Grande gets a license suspension after the ROC finds defective work on your house. Under 32-1161, that builder can come back and fix the warranty or corrective items on your home even while suspended, but they cannot charge you for it. That keeps the door open for the original contractor to cure their own defect instead of forcing you to hire a replacement just to handle warranty repairs.

The reinstatement rule cuts in your favor too. The suspended contractor cannot simply wait out the clock; to get the license back, they have to satisfy the loss they caused, which often means paying for or completing the repair that triggered the suspension. So a suspension is not just a punishment, it is pressure that pushes the contractor to make things right. If your builder is suspended, keep clear records of the defective work and any repairs, since "all loss... fully satisfied" is the standard they must meet.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the back end of the discipline process that starts with the grounds for suspension in A.R.S. 32-1154. The free corrective work it allows lines up with the protections a new-home buyer gets under the implied warranty of workmanship and habitability . For the agency running the process, see ROC . Suspension is also distinct from the unlicensed-contractor enforcement in A.R.S. 32-1166 ; that one targets people who never had a license, while 32-1161 deals with a licensed contractor who lost it.

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact reinstatement conditions, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1161 on azleg.gov .

Whether a specific repair counts as the "warranty or corrective work" allowed during suspension can be a judgment call, so coordinate any post-suspension work through the ROC complaint record rather than an informal handshake.

Sources

  • A.R.S. 32-1161 (Rights of contractor after suspension of license)

title: A.R.S. 32-1162: The 2-Year Deadline to File an ROC Complaint
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1162 gives you two years to file a complaint with Arizona's ROC against a contractor. For a new home, the two years runs from the earlier of close of escrow or actual occupancy. For other projects, it runs from the date the specific project was completed. Miss it and the ROC will not act.
bodyHtml:
The ROC will not chase an old complaint. A.R.S. 32-1162 puts a two-year deadline on filing a complaint against a contractor, and the date the clock starts depends on the kind of project. For a new home it is close of escrow or move-in, whichever comes first. For everything else it is the day the project was finished. This page explains both triggers so you file in time and keep the Registrar of Contractors (the ROC ) able to help.

The two-year limit and when it starts

The deadline is two years, but the trigger date is not the same for every job. The statute splits it. For new construction:

...within two years after the earlier of the close of escrow or actual occupancy.

For all other projects, like a remodel or repair:

...within two years after the completion of the specific project.

So for a brand-new home, the clock starts at the earlier of close of escrow or the day you actually move in. For a remodel, addition, or pool, it starts when that specific project is complete. Either way, you have two years from that point to file your complaint with the ROC.

What this means for you

Say you close escrow on a new home in Scottsdale on March 1 and move in April 15. The trigger is the earlier date, close of escrow, so your two years runs from March 1. A defect you discover 25 months later is past the ROC deadline, even if the work was clearly bad. Now take a kitchen remodel in Mesa finished on June 1. Your two years runs from that completion date.

Two practical moves. First, write down your trigger date the day it happens: the close-of-escrow date or the project completion date. That single date controls everything. Second, do not let an attempt to work it out with the contractor eat your two years. Negotiations can drag, and the deadline keeps running. If you are getting close to two years with no fix, file the complaint to protect your rights, then keep talking.

How this connects to the rest of contractors law

This deadline governs the ROC complaint you file under A.R.S. 32-1155 , charging grounds listed in A.R.S. 32-1154 . It is a different clock from the recovery fund lawsuit deadline in A.R.S. 32-1133 , so do not assume meeting one covers the other. For the full path and how these deadlines stack, see how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim and the new home builder warranty in Arizona .

Full text and source

Read the current version on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1162 on azleg.gov .

How the deadline applies to your project depends on the facts, so confirm with the ROC or a qualified attorney before relying on it.

Sources

title: A.R.S. 32-1164: The Criminal Penalty for Unlicensed Contracting in Arizona
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1164 makes acting as a contractor without a license a class 1 misdemeanor in Arizona, with a fine of at least $1,000 for a first offense and at least $2,000 for repeats. If the court grants probation, it must order the violator to pay all state, county, and city taxes owed from the job.
bodyHtml:
Contracting without a license in Arizona is not just a regulatory problem, it is a crime. A.R.S. 32-1164 sets the criminal penalty: a class 1 misdemeanor with mandatory minimum fines that climb for repeat offenders. This page explains exactly which acts are charged, what the fines are, and the tax-payment condition the court must attach to probation. It tells you what is at stake for anyone who builds without the proper license.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1164 names the conduct that crosses from license discipline into criminal liability. Two acts are class 1 misdemeanors:

Acting in the capacity of a contractor within the meaning of this chapter without a license.

The other charged act is committing certain serious violations listed in A.R.S. 32-1154 (the discipline statute), specifically paragraphs 6 and 9 of subsection A. A class 1 misdemeanor is the most serious misdemeanor class in Arizona. The fines are set as floors: a first offense draws a fine of "not less than one thousand dollars," and a subsequent offense draws "not less than two thousand dollars." Those are minimums, so a court can impose more.

The statute also controls probation. If the court grants probation after a conviction, it must require the violator to pay all state transaction privilege or use tax, all county tax, and all municipal tax owed and "arising from the acts or omissions constituting the offense." In other words, an unlicensed operator cannot dodge the sales and use taxes a licensed contractor would have collected.

What this means for homeowners and owner-builders

This section is the enforcement edge behind every licensing rule on the site. For you as a homeowner, it is a reason to never hire an unlicensed contractor for real construction. Someone willing to break the licensing law is someone the criminal courts treat as a class 1 misdemeanant, and they carry none of the bond, Recovery Fund, or discipline protections a licensed contractor brings.

It also draws a line for owner-builders. For example, if you legitimately build your own home under the owner-builder exemption in A.R.S. 32-1121, you are not "acting in the capacity of a contractor" for hire and you are not exposed here. But if you take the exemption and then act as a paid contractor on someone else's project, or hire out as a builder without a license, you can be charged under 32-1164. The first-offense minimum is $1,000, the second is $2,000, and the tax-payment probation condition follows. The practical takeaway is simple: confirm the license before the work, and stay inside the owner-builder lane if that is the path you chose.

How this connects to other rules

A.R.S. 32-1164 is the criminal partner to the civil and administrative rules. A.R.S. 32-1151 makes unlicensed contracting unlawful in the first place, A.R.S. 32-1153 stops an unlicensed contractor from suing for payment, and A.R.S. 32-1154 lists the discipline grounds, two of which 32-1164 turns into crimes. The owner-builder exemption that keeps homeowners out of this section is A.R.S. 32-1121. To verify a license before you hire, see how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC , and for the owner-builder path, see can I be my own general contractor in Arizona . Complaints about unlicensed work go to the Registrar of Contractors , covered in how to file an ROC complaint and recovery fund claim .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact classification and probation wording, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1164 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific situation depends on the facts, so confirm your situation with a qualified attorney before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1166: When the ROC Can Order an Unlicensed Contractor to Stop
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1166 lets the Arizona Registrar of Contractors issue a cease and desist order to anyone who is doing contracting work that requires a license but does not hold one. The order forces the unlicensed person to stop the prohibited activity the moment they receive it.
bodyHtml:
The Arizona Registrar of Contractors has a fast way to shut down unlicensed building work, and it lives in A.R.S. 32-1166. The statute gives the ROC the power to issue a cease and desist order to any person who is doing work that requires a contractor license but who is not licensed. The order tells that person to stop the moment they get it. For a homeowner, this is one of the tools that gives a license complaint real teeth.

What this statute does

The ROC is the state agency that licenses and disciplines residential and commercial contractors in Arizona. Section 32-1166 lets the Registrar act against people who skip licensing entirely, not licensed contractors who broke a rule. The target is the unlicensed person who is engaging in contracting (an act, a practice, or a transaction) that violates the contractor statutes, a Registrar rule, or a prior Registrar order.

The registrar may issue a cease and desist order to any person who is required to be licensed by this chapter but who is not licensed and who engaged in an act of contracting, a practice or a transaction that violates this chapter, a rule adopted by the registrar or an order issued by the registrar.

The order takes effect on receipt and requires the person to stop the prohibited activity right away. The Registrar can deliver it by personal service or by certified mail to the person's last known business or home address, so there is a clear record of when the clock started.

What it means for your home build

Picture an unlicensed handyman in Apache Junction taking a $40,000 job to remodel a kitchen and add a bedroom, work that is well past the small-job exemption and clearly requires a license. If a homeowner or a competitor reports him, the ROC can issue a cease and desist order under 32-1166 that legally compels him to stop the project on the spot. Ignoring the order is itself a violation that can lead to further enforcement.

For you as the homeowner, the lesson is to confirm a license before anyone starts. You can verify any contractor's license number for free on the ROC website. Hiring licensed protects you in two ways: the contractor is subject to ROC discipline like this order, and only a properly licensed contractor gives you access to the state recovery fund. An unlicensed contractor who gets a cease and desist order may walk off mid-job, leaving you to find a licensed builder to finish and correct the work.

How this connects to other rules

This is the enforcement counterpart to the basic ban on unlicensed contracting in A.R.S. 32-1151, which makes the conduct illegal in the first place. A cease and desist order under 32-1166 is also one of the two triggers that lets a city or county order work on a project to stop until everyone on the job is properly licensed, under A.R.S. 32-1168. For the agency itself, see ROC in the glossary. The quality protections you get from hiring licensed connect to the implied warranty of workmanship and habitability .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the service rules and the exact triggering conduct, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1166 on azleg.gov .

Enforcement steps and timing are handled by the Registrar case by case, so report suspected unlicensed work directly to the ROC rather than relying on a stop order happening on its own.

Sources

title: A.R.S. 32-1168: Stopping Work Until Contractors on a Project Are Licensed
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1168 lets a city, county, or state authority halt or suspend work on a construction project, at the Registrar of Contractors' request, until the contractors on that job meet Arizona's licensing requirements. It turns a license problem into a job-site shutdown.
bodyHtml:
A license problem in Arizona does not just sit on paper; it can freeze a whole job site. A.R.S. 32-1168, titled "Proof of valid license," gives local and state government the power to make work on a construction project stop until the contractors on that project are properly licensed. The Registrar of Contractors flags the problem, and the city, county, or other authority pulls the plug on the work. This page explains how that chain works and why it matters to a homeowner whose project could get caught in it.

What this statute does

The section ties together a few other parts of the contractor law into one enforcement result. After the Registrar issues either a citation under A.R.S. 32-1104 or a cease and desist order under A.R.S. 32-1166, and at the Registrar's request, a city, county, or state authority may order work on the project to cease or be suspended. The shutdown lasts until the contractors working on that project come into compliance with the licensing requirement in A.R.S. 32-1151.

At the request of the registrar, and after issuing a citation pursuant to section 32-1104, subsection A, paragraph 4, or a cease and desist order pursuant to section 32-1166, the county, city or authority of the state may cause work on a construction project to cease or be suspended on that project until there is compliance with the licensing requirements of section 32-1151 by those contractors employed on that project.

So the title "Proof of valid license" describes the cure: the way to lift the stop is to show the contractors on the job are validly licensed. The local government is the muscle, but the ROC is the trigger.

What it means for your home build

Suppose you are building a custom home in Cave Creek and one of the trades your general contractor brought on, say the framer, turns out to be unlicensed. If the ROC issues a cease and desist order and asks the town to act, the town can suspend work on your entire project, not just that one trade's portion, until the licensing issue is fixed. That can mean lost weeks, a stalled construction loan draw, and schedule pressure that ripples through every other trade.

The takeaway is to push licensing diligence onto your builder and confirm it yourself. Ask your general contractor to use only licensed subcontractors, and spot-check the key trades' license numbers on the free ROC lookup. A clean, fully licensed crew is the cheapest insurance against a 32-1168 shutdown, because the way to avoid the stop entirely is to never have an unlicensed contractor on the job.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the local-government enforcement arm built on top of two ROC actions: the citation power tied to A.R.S. 32-1104 and the cease and desist order in A.R.S. 32-1166 . The underlying requirement it enforces is the basic licensing mandate of A.R.S. 32-1151. Because the stop happens during active construction and can hold up inspections and draws, it sits close to the permitting process that drives your building permit cost in Arizona . For the agency at the center of it, see ROC .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact references to the citation and cease and desist authorities, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1168 on azleg.gov .

Whether a city actually suspends a project, and how fast the stop can be lifted, depends on the Registrar's request and local procedure, so verify every contractor's license before work starts rather than counting on a quick fix afterward.

Sources

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors — official site

title: A.R.S. 32-1169: Proof of a Valid License for Arizona Building Permits
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1169 makes Arizona cities and counties check licensing before they issue a building permit. The permit applicant must file a signed statement giving their contractor license number, or, if exempt, state the exemption and name the licensed trades doing the work.
bodyHtml:
Licensing is enforced not only by the state but at the local permit counter. A.R.S. 32-1169 puts cities, counties, and other permit-issuing agencies in the loop: before they hand out a building permit, they have to make the applicant prove proper licensing or claim a valid exemption. This is the checkpoint that ties Arizona's contractor law to the permit your home build actually needs.

What this statute says

The rule requires a signed statement of licensing as part of the permit application. The applicant has to give a license number, or explain the exemption if they are not licensed. Here is the operative language:

each applicant for a building permit file a signed statement that the applicant is properly licensed to perform the work described in the permit under this chapter, with the applicant's license number.

That signed statement makes the contractor put licensing in writing to a local government, under their own signature, before any permit issues. It turns the permit office into a second line of verification behind the state Registrar.

What this means for you

This section is the reason your permit and your licensing line up. A real example: your builder applies for a permit to construct your 3,000 square foot home in Apache Junction. As part of that application, they file a signed statement listing their ROC license number for the work. The city checks it before issuing the permit, so an unlicensed operator cannot quietly pull a permit on a job they are not allowed to do.

The section also handles the owner-builder path. If you apply as an exempt owner under A.R.S. 32-1121 , you state the basis for your exemption and provide the license numbers of any general, mechanical, electrical, or plumbing contractors you hire for the project. The permit authority may also ask the state Registrar to verify a claimed exemption. So even as an owner-builder, the licensed trades you bring in still have to be named and verified at the counter.

How this connects to the rest of the law

This section is where the licensing scheme meets local permitting. It rests on the contractor definition in A.R.S. 32-1101 , the classifications in A.R.S. 32-1102 , and the exemptions in A.R.S. 32-1121 . The license number it asks for is issued and posted under A.R.S. 32-1124 , and verification runs through the Arizona Registrar of Contractors . If you plan to act as your own builder, start with can I build my own house in Arizona , then use how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC for the trades you hire.

Full text and source

Read the current version, including the exemption and verification details and any amendments, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1169 on azleg.gov .

How a specific permit or exemption is handled depends on your local jurisdiction, so confirm with your city or county permit office before relying on it.

  • Arizona Registrar of Contractors (ROC)

title: A.R.S. 32-1170: What Counts as a Solar Contractor in Arizona
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1170 defines a solar contractor as a licensed contractor who installs, alters, or repairs solar devices, and defines a solar device as equipment that applies solar energy to plumbing, heating and cooling, pool heating, hot water, or daylighting. These definitions set who needs a solar qualification to do the work.
bodyHtml:
Arizona treats solar work as its own licensed specialty, and A.R.S. 32-1170 is the section that defines the terms. It tells you who counts as a solar contractor and what equipment counts as a solar device. That matters because the definition decides who is allowed to install, alter, or repair the solar systems on your roof, your pool, and your water heater. This page breaks down both definitions in plain language and shows why they control who can legally do the work.

What this statute says

A.R.S. 32-1170 supplies two definitions used across the solar provisions of the contractor law. First, the person:

"Solar contractor" means a contractor, as defined in section 32-1101, who installs, alters or repairs solar devices.

So a solar contractor is not a new kind of company; it is a licensed contractor (under the broad 32-1101 meaning) whose work is solar devices. Second, the equipment. A solar device is gear that uses and applies solar energy as part of one of these systems:

  • Boilers, steamfitting, and process piping.
  • Plumbing systems.
  • Air conditioning, comfort heating, and cooling systems.
  • Hot water systems for homes and swimming pools.
  • Solar daylighting that meets the standards in A.R.S. 44-1761.

The common thread is that the device puts the sun to work in a building system. Rooftop solar water heating, a solar pool heater, and solar-assisted HVAC all fall inside the definition.

Why the definition controls the work

A definition section looks dry, but it is load-bearing. By naming what a solar device is and who a solar contractor is, A.R.S. 32-1170 sets the line that the next sections enforce. Installing solar equipment is licensed contracting, so the installer needs the right license classification and, for many solar systems, a solar qualification. For example, if you add a solar water heating system to a new build in Fountain Hills, the company tying it into your plumbing and hot water system is doing solar device work and must be a properly licensed contractor, not a handyman. The same is true for a solar pool heater on a Scottsdale backyard pool.

This protects you in a concrete way. If something fails (a leaking solar collector, a botched roof penetration, an HVAC tie-in that voids your equipment warranty), the work was licensed contracting, which means the Registrar of Contractors complaint and discipline tools are available. Hire someone outside the definition and you lose that backstop.

How this connects to other rules

These definitions feed the rest of the solar licensing scheme. A.R.S. 32-1170.01 prohibits installing a solar device without the required qualification, and A.R.S. 32-1170.02 governs the solar qualification exam. The "contractor" half of the definition points back to A.R.S. 32-1101, the master definition of contractor, and to the license classifications under A.R.S. 32-1102. Because solar work is licensed, you verify the installer with the Registrar of Contractors just like any other contractor, as shown in how to verify an Arizona contractor license with the ROC . For whether solar pencils out on a new home, see is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona and should I prewire a new home for solar and automation .

Full text and source

Read the current section, including the exact list of covered systems, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1170 on azleg.gov .

How the definitions apply to a specific solar job depends on the facts, so confirm details with the ROC or a qualified professional before relying on it.

title: A.R.S. 32-1170.01: Who Can Install Solar in Arizona
shortSummary: A.R.S. 32-1170.01 makes it unlawful for a contractor to install, alter, or repair a solar device in Arizona without first qualifying as a solar contractor under the licensing rules. A general license alone does not cover solar work; the contractor must hold the solar qualification.
bodyHtml:
In Arizona, installing solar takes more than a general contractor's license. A.R.S. 32-1170.01 makes it unlawful to install, alter, or repair a solar device unless the contractor has qualified specifically as a solar contractor. This page explains the rule and why it matters for a state where rooftop solar is everywhere.

What this statute says

A "solar device" means equipment that collects and uses sunlight for energy, such as a rooftop photovoltaic (PV) array or a solar water heater. A.R.S. 32-1170.01 says a contractor cannot install, alter, or repair one of these systems without first qualifying as a solar contractor under the Registrar of Contractors' rules. The qualification is a specific credential, not something a generic license carries by default. Here is the full rule:

After June 30, 1980, it is unlawful for a contractor to install, alter or repair a solar device without qualifying as a solar contractor pursuant to this article.

In plain terms, holding any contractor's license is not enough. To touch solar, the contractor must have passed the solar qualification and carry the right license scope for it. This sits on top of the basic licensing requirement, so the contractor needs both a license in good standing and the solar qualification.

What it means for your home build

Arizona leads the country in rooftop solar, so this rule comes up on a lot of custom homes and retrofits. For example, if you add a 9 kW PV system to a new build in Scottsdale, the company that mounts the panels and wires the array must hold the proper solar qualification, not just a general residential license. Hiring a crew that lacks it means the work is being done unlawfully, and you lose the protections that come with a properly licensed installer.

Before you sign a solar contract, verify the license and its scope free at the ROC site. Confirm the qualification covers PV or solar thermal, whichever you are installing. Then make sure the agreement spells out the system size, equipment brands, and price, and that any change comes as a written change order . A solar installer who cannot show the right qualification is a clear reason to walk away.

How this connects to other rules

This section is the solar-specific version of the general ban in A.R.S. 32-1151, which makes contracting without a license illegal. Where 32-1151 sets the baseline, 32-1170.01 adds a second gate for solar work. The same enforcement applies: under A.R.S. 32-1153, a contractor who lacked the proper contractor's license or qualification cannot sue you to collect. And your solar agreement still must meet the contract rules in A.R.S. 32-1158. For building a strong contract, see what to include in a custom home contract and cost-plus vs fixed-price home contracts .

Full text and source

Read the current section, along with the related solar definitions and exam rules, on the legislature's site: View A.R.S. 32-1170.01 on azleg.gov .

How the rule applies to a specific job depends on the facts, so verify any license at roc.az.gov and confirm details with a qualified professional before relying on it.

Sources
