title: Apache Junction Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Apache Junction inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered. You schedule inspections by calling 480-474-5101 or through the MyGovernmentOnline portal. After the final passes, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy that lets you move in.
bodyHtml:
When you build in Apache Junction, the city inspects the work at fixed stages, and each stage has to pass before a crew covers it with concrete, drywall, or stucco. The last stage is the final inspection, and clearing it is what lets the city issue your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. This page covers the order, how Apache Junction wants inspections requested, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence

The inspector follows the build from the dirt up and catches each stage while the work is still in the open. A new home runs through this order:

  1. Foundation. After the footing trench and steel are set, before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical under the floor are placed, before the slab covers them.
  3. Roof and framing. When sheathing, tie-downs, walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are all in, before any cover-up.
  4. Insulation. Before drywall.
  5. Lath. Before stucco or plaster.
  6. Final. When the home is complete and all fixtures are set, before occupancy.

The order holds because an inspector cannot approve work that is already hidden. For example, suppose a framer finishes a 2,400 square foot Apache Junction home and the drywall crew boards the walls before the framing inspection is called. The board now hides the wiring, plumbing rough-in, and fire blocking, leaving the inspector nothing to check, so the inspection fails. The crew opens the new drywall back up, re-inspects, then re-patches, costing a week and labor. The City of Apache Junction is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , and its Division of Building Safety and Inspection coordinates the permit, plan review, and these inspections. Apache Junction is in Pinal County, but inside city limits the city runs the inspections, and its adopted codes are the 2018 I-codes.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

Apache Junction is the one city in this group that still leans on the phone. You schedule a building inspection by calling 480-474-5101, and the city asks you to leave a callback number and your permit number when you reach the line. You can also request inspections through the MyGovernmentOnline (MGO) portal, which the city describes this way:

24 hour access to permit and inspection information, scheduling, etc.

For questions about a permit or an inspection, the Building Division answers at 480-474-5156.

Whichever method you use, request the inspection that matches the stage your work is at, and do not let a crew conceal wiring, plumbing, or framing before that inspection passes. A failed inspection means cutting the cover back open.

Move-in waits on one last sign-off. A passing final on a new home is what lets the city issue the Certificate of Occupancy, the record that the house meets code and may be lived in. The city's inspection authority comes from City Code Section 7-1-7, which is the local version of the model code's inspection rules.

When this comes into play

This runs across the entire build, not only the finish:

  • Pour days. A footing or slab cannot be poured until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the time you call in the request shapes the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical are inspected before insulation and drywall. A failure here stops the cover-up.
  • Move-in. The final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy. A sale or mortgage often needs the CO first.

What this means for you

Build the habit of calling 480-474-5101 (or requesting in MGO) the day before you need an inspection, with your permit number and a callback number ready, because a voicemail without those slows the schedule. Never bury wiring, plumbing, or framing before the inspector signs off. Do not move family in before the Certificate of Occupancy issues, because occupying without it is a code violation. If your end-of-build timeline is tight, ask the Building Division early what a final needs, so nothing on the punch list holds up the CO.

This entry closes the Apache Junction module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Apache Junction Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Apache Junction Residential Building Permit Requirements . To see how inspections fit the rest of the build, read the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The inspection scheduling phone line and the MyGovernmentOnline portal are published by the City of Apache Junction: Division of Building Safety and Inspection on apachejunctionaz.gov . For permit steps and the portal link, see Permits .

Take this as general guidance, not an official code interpretation. The city can change its phone line, MGO portal, or CO steps, so confirm the current process with the Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection before you call in a request.

Sources

  • City of Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection
  • City of Apache Junction Permits (Building Safety and Inspections)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Apache Junction Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: The $6,736 Development Fee
shortSummary: Apache Junction charges a new single-family home a development impact fee of $6,736, made of a $3,250 streets fee, $1,707 parks fee, $1,229 police fee, and $550 library fee, collected at the first building permit. The building permit fee itself runs off the ICC Building Valuation Data table. Confirm the live numbers with the city.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Apache Junction, in Pinal County, carries two separate buckets of city money. First, a building permit and plan review fee, priced off the value of your construction. Second, a development impact fee, the larger charge that pays for the streets, parks, police, and library service your home will use. This page gives you the real impact-fee numbers from the city's own ordinance, plus the basis for the permit fee, so you can put accurate line items in your budget.

The development impact fee is $6,736 for a single-family home

Impact fees are charges a city collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth requires. Arizona authorizes them under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Apache Junction adopted its current schedule by City Council Ordinance 1521, effective October 31, 2022, and codified it in Volume II, Chapter 7 of the City Code. The city assesses four categories on a new home, and the Appendix 7-A schedule sets each one per housing unit:

Single Family, Per Housing Unit: Library $550, Parks and Recreation $1,707, Police $1,229, Streets $3,250. Total Development Fee $6,736.

So a single detached home pays $6,736 in combined development impact fees. The streets fee, at $3,250, is the largest single piece, because it is built from the average vehicle-miles a new home generates. Parks ($1,707), police ($1,229), and library ($550) follow. Apache Junction has no city water or sewer impact fee in this schedule, since those four categories are what the city adopted. The development fee is collected up front, as the city code makes plain:

No building permit or certificate of occupancy shall be issued if a development fee is not paid.

So the $6,736 is part of the cash you bring to pull the permit, not a later bill.

How the building permit fee is calculated

Separate from the impact fee, Apache Junction charges a building permit and plan review fee. For a house, the city does not use the ICC valuation table it applies to commercial projects. It sets the fee from a flat square-foot rate in its adopted fee schedule (Ordinance 1469): $1.20 per square foot for the first story with an $850 minimum, $1.05 per square foot for each additional story, and $0.80 per square foot for occupied roof or covered area. Plan review is charged alongside the permit.

Because the rate is per square foot, you can estimate the permit fee before you apply. A single-story 3,000 square foot home comes to about $3,600 in permit fee (3,000 square feet at $1.20), comfortably above the $850 minimum, with plan review added on top. The City Council adjusts the fee schedule over time, so confirm the current rate with Apache Junction Development Services at 480-474-5090. The development impact fee is separate and fully published at $6,736 for a single-family home.

When these fees come into play

  • At first permit. The development fee is calculated and collected with the application for the first building or electrical permit. The city will not release the permit until the fee is paid in full.
  • The 24-month freeze. Under City Code 7-1-13(D)(1) and A.R.S. 9-463.05, once the first building permit issues for a single-unit residential development, the city locks that development's fee schedule for 24 months at the permittee's request, so a later fee increase does not catch your project mid-build.
  • Valuation drives the permit fee. Because the permit fee runs off the ICC valuation table, a larger or higher-value home pays a larger permit fee, on top of the flat $6,736 impact fee.

What this means for you

Budget two separate numbers. The first is firm: the $6,736 single-family development impact fee, due up front at permit, split as $3,250 streets, $1,707 parks, $1,229 police, and $550 library. The second is the building permit and plan review fee, which scales with your home's ICC-table valuation and which you should confirm with the city for your specific valuation. If you have a single-unit residential lot, ask the city to lock your fee schedule for 24 months when your first permit issues. Apache Junction collects no separate city water or sewer impact fee in this chapter, so if your lot is on septic or a private well, budget those through Pinal County and the state instead.

This entry sits in the Apache Junction building-codes module alongside Apache Junction Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Apache Junction Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans are checked against. For how the permit basis differs across the metro, see valuation versus per-square-foot permit pricing in Arizona and how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The $6,736 single-family total and the four category amounts are reproduced from the City of Apache Junction's Volume II, Chapter 7 development-fee schedule (Appendix 7-A, Ordinance 1521), a public record published on the city's Development Services Fees page. The collection-at-permit and 24-month-freeze rules are codified in City Code Section 7-1-13 . The permit office is the Division of Building Safety and Inspection , and the statute behind impact fees is A.R.S. 9-463.05 .

These fees change by city action, and the per-valuation permit-fee figures are not posted in a machine-readable form. Confirm the current impact fee and the permit fee for your valuation with Apache Junction Development Services before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Apache Junction Development Services Fees (Appendix 7-A Development Fee Schedule)
  • Apache Junction City Code Volume II, Chapter 7, Section 7-1-13 (Collection of Development Fees)
  • City of Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Apache Junction Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and Where
shortSummary: A new home in Apache Junction needs a building permit before any work starts. You apply online through MyGovernmentOnline with a site plan, floor plans, and engineered construction documents including truss plans. Review for codes, zoning, and site conditions currently runs about four to six weeks before the permit issues.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Apache Junction home until the city issues a building permit. The permit is the city's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted 2018 I-codes and your lot's zoning. Apache Junction takes permit applications online and runs them through its Division of Building Safety and Inspection. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how it is reviewed, and how long it takes.

What a residential permit requires

Apache Junction accepts residential building-permit applications online at MyGovernmentOnline.org, and a new single-family home, duplex, townhouse, accessory dwelling unit, garage, or addition all go through the same digital intake. A complete submittal for a new home generally includes:

  • A site plan showing the house on the lot, with setbacks (the required distance from each property line), drainage, and access called out.
  • Floor plans and elevations.
  • Structural and construction documents engineered for Arizona loads, with the foundation designed for local soils.
  • Engineered truss plans for floors and roofs.
  • Energy compliance paperwork showing the design meets the 2018 IECC as amended.

The city is specific about a few of these items up front:

Engineered truss plans for floors and/or roofs shall be submitted at the time of application for the first review. The construction documents shall contain a title sheet or title sheets indicating the name, address, and phone numbers of project owner(s), design professionals, and contractors.

Leaving the truss plans or the title-sheet contacts out of the first submittal is a common reason a package stalls before review even begins, which costs you days.

For example, say you apply online for a 2,200 square foot home and upload the architecturals but not the engineered roof trusses. Intake holds the file as incomplete, so the four-to-six-week review clock does not even start until you add the truss plans. A second applicant who uploaded a complete set the same day is already weeks ahead of you.

How review and timeline work

Apache Junction reviews a residential plan for building-code compliance, zoning, and site conditions. Current review times run about four to six weeks for a new residential project. If the reviewers find corrections, you fix the sheets and resubmit through the same portal, and each round adds calendar time, so a clean first submittal is the cheapest path.

The flow is: assemble the site plan and engineered set, apply through MyGovernmentOnline, let the Division of Building Safety and Inspection complete its review, clear any corrections, then pay fees and receive the permit. Because Apache Junction is in Pinal County, a lot outside the city sewer system also needs a separate septic permit. The Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division issues septic permits within the city limits under authority delegated by the state, so onsite wastewater is a county track, not a city one, and a private well is registered with the Arizona Department of Water Resources.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Apache Junction build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the digital package, the Division of Building Safety and Inspection runs its four-to-six-week review, and the permit issues only after corrections clear.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121 only covers a home you intend to occupy, not one you build to sell or rent within a year. That legal limit decides whether you can sign as the permit holder.
  • Septic and well lots. On unsewered land, the Pinal County septic permit and the state well registration have to be lined up with the city build, so plan those timelines in parallel.

What this means for you

Budget the permit as a real line item and submit a complete first package, since missing truss plans or contact title sheets stall the file before review starts. Get the site plan setbacks right the first time, because a setback violation is one of the most common residential corrections. Build the four-to-six-week review, plus any correction cycles, into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant. If your lot is unsewered, open the Pinal County septic file at the same time, not after the house permit, so the two tracks finish together.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Apache Junction module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Apache Junction Adopted Building Codes . The permit ends, much later, with a certificate of occupancy once final inspections pass. For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The permit process, online intake, and submittal items are published by the City of Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection . For permit types and contacts, see Apache Junction Building Permits . Arizona's owner-builder limit is set in state law at A.R.S. 32-1121 .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the city's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with the Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection before you file.

Sources

  • City of Apache Junction Division of Building Safety and Inspection
  • City of Apache Junction Building Permits
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Apache Junction Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Where Your New Home Can Sit on the Lot
shortSummary: Apache Junction single-residence zoning runs from RS-GR on 1.25 acre lots to RS-5 on 5,000 square foot lots, and Table 5-2 sets each yard. RS-GR needs a 40 foot front, 20 foot sides, and 40 foot rear. RS-7 needs a 20 foot front, 7 foot sides, and a 20 foot rear.
bodyHtml:
Before you draw a floor plan in Apache Junction, find your zoning district, because the district sets how far the house must sit from each property line. That open distance is called a setback, and it is the rule that decides where the house can go on the lot. Apache Junction, in Pinal County, names its single-residence districts RS, from the large-lot RS-GR (general rural, 1.25 acres) down to RS-5 (5,000 square feet). One published table, Table 5-2, carries the numbers for all of them.

What the districts require

The Land Development Code, Section 1-5-2, sets the residential bulk regulations. The required lot, yards, height, and coverage change by district. Here are the numbers for the single-residence districts:

  • RS-GR: 1.25 acre min lot, front 40 ft, interior side 20 ft, street side 20 ft, rear 40 ft, height 35 ft, lot coverage 30%.
  • RS-54 / RS-54M: 1.25 acre min lot, front 30 ft, interior side 10 ft, street side 20 ft, rear 30 ft, height 35 ft, lot coverage 20%.
  • RS-20 / RS-20M: 20,000 sq ft min lot, front 25 ft, interior side 10 ft, street side 10 ft, rear 20 ft, height 35 ft, lot coverage 30%.
  • RS-10 / RS-10M: 10,000 sq ft min lot, front 20 ft, interior side 10 ft, street side 10 ft, rear 20 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 40%.
  • RS-7 / RS-7M: 7,000 sq ft min lot, front 20 ft, interior side 7 ft, street side 10 ft, rear 20 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 50%.
  • RS-5: 5,000 sq ft min lot, front 20 ft, interior side 7 ft, street side 10 ft, rear 15 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 60%.

The code defines lot coverage so you count every roofed structure, not just the house:

The footprints of all principal and accessory structures, including garages, carports, covered patios, and covered porches, shall be added together in order to calculate lot coverage.

So a covered patio and a detached garage both eat into the coverage cap, which matters most on the smaller RS-7 and RS-5 lots where the cap is already a tight share of the land.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks and coverage drive the site plan before you draw the house:

  • Build-on-your-lot. For example, on an RS-GR 1.25 acre lot, the 40 foot front and rear plus 20 foot sides carve a deep border out of the parcel before the house even starts. Your designer subtracts those yards to find the buildable area, the footprint left for the home.
  • Corner lots. A corner lot carries a street-side setback on the second frontage, so the buildable pad is smaller than on an interior lot.
  • Accessory structures. Sheds, casitas, and detached garages have their own setbacks in Table 5-2, and a covered front porch may project up to 10 feet into the front yard under set conditions.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason an Apache Junction plan gets kicked back, so the city confirms each yard distance before the permit moves forward.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact RS district before you buy or design, because the jump from RS-7 to RS-GR changes the front yard from 20 feet to 40 feet and the sides from 7 feet to 20 feet. Watch lot coverage alongside the yards, since RS-5 allows up to 60% coverage while RS-54 allows only 20%, which caps the house size on a rural lot. Note the height too: the rural RS-GR and RS-54 districts allow 35 feet, while the smaller RS-10 through RS-5 lots cap at 30 feet. We run the setbacks, coverage cap, and buildable area on the first day of an Apache Junction project, because they decide whether a floor plan fits or has to change.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Apache Junction module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Apache Junction Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Apache Junction Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The per-district lot sizes, setbacks, heights, and coverage limits are published by the City of Apache Junction in Table 5-2 of the Land Development Code, Section 1-5-2: view Section 1-5-2 on amlegal.com . The city's zoning hub is the Apache Junction Zoning Ordinance , and parcels are mapped at the Pinal County Assessor .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks and coverage change with overlays, modular (M) sub-districts, and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Apache Junction Building Safety before you design.

Sources

  • Apache Junction Land Development Code, Section 1-5-2 (Residential Bulk Regulations, Table 5-2)
  • City of Apache Junction Zoning Ordinance
  • Pinal County Assessor parcel and zoning lookup

title: Arizona Building Code Editions by City: A Phoenix-Metro Comparison
shortSummary: Arizona has no statewide building code, so each city and county adopts its own I-code edition. Across the Phoenix metro the spread is wide: Phoenix is on the 2024 codes, Scottsdale on 2021, and Maricopa County, Carefree, Casa Grande, and Apache Junction on 2018. This page compares the adopted edition, electrical code, effective date, and adopting ordinance for each.
bodyHtml:
The exact code edition your home is built to depends entirely on which side of a jurisdiction line your lot sits, because the state leaves code adoption to each city and county rather than setting one rule for everyone. The code authors at the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) put it plainly: codes are adopted locally in Arizona and are predominantly the International Codes (I-codes). In the Phoenix metro that produces a striking spread of nearly a decade, from the 2024 codes in Phoenix down to the 2015 codes still in force in Paradise Valley. This page lines up the ten jurisdictions Jematell Homes builds in so you can see the differences at a glance, then explains why they exist and what they cost you.

The comparison table

The "edition" column is the residential and building code cycle (IRC and IBC). The "NEC" column is the electrical code, which often runs a cycle behind the I-codes because the National Electrical Code publishes on a different schedule. Every edition listed below is confirmed against each jurisdiction's own published adoption.

JurisdictionI-code editionNECEffectiveAdopting ordinance
Phoenix20242023Aug 1, 2025Ord. G-7397
Scottsdale20212020Jan 1, 2023City Council adoption
Cave Creek2024 (some 2025 codes)2023Jan 1, 2026Res. R2025-01
Fountain Hills20242023Sept 1, 2025Ord. 25-06
Maricopa County (unincorporated)20182017Aug 17, 2022Local Additions and Addenda
Carefree20182017Jan 24, 2023Ord. 2022-02
Casa Grande20182017Title 15 adoptionRes. 5170
Apache Junction20182017July 18, 2019City Code Chapter 7
Mesa20182017Mesa City Code Title 4Mesa Residential Code (2019 amendments)
Paradise Valley2015 (+2021 IFC)2014May 26, 2016Ord. 2016-02

Two extra notes belong with the table. Cave Creek keeps the 2018 residential energy code for houses even though it moved to the 2024 codes for everything else, so a single-family build there follows a lighter energy path than the 2024 cycle would suggest. Maricopa County adopts the 2018 energy code as voluntary, not mandatory, so a rural county build is not forced into the energy paperwork a city requires. Those two wrinkles change what a homeowner actually pays for insulation and duct testing, so they are worth catching early.

Why the editions differ

The reason is structural, not an oversight. The I-codes are written by the International Code Council on a three-year cycle (2018, 2021, 2024), but adoption is a local political act. A city council or board of supervisors has to vote to adopt the new cycle, edit it for local desert conditions, and set an effective date. The code authors confirm this:

Codes are adopted locally in Arizona and are predominantly the I-codes.

Some jurisdictions move fast and some move slowly. Phoenix and Fountain Hills jumped to the brand-new 2024 cycle in 2025. Cave Creek followed on January 1, 2026. Scottsdale sits a cycle back on 2021. Maricopa County, Carefree, Casa Grande, and Apache Junction are all still on 2018. A town that updates every cycle stays current; a town that updates every second or third cycle falls a few years behind by design. None of them are out of compliance, because there is no state floor forcing a single edition. The local AHJ has the final word, and its adopted edition is the law inside its lines.

The electrical code drifts its own way. The NEC is on a three-year cycle too (2017, 2020, 2023), but a jurisdiction often adopts a slightly older NEC than its I-codes because the books pair up by what was current at adoption time. That is why Phoenix runs the 2024 I-codes with the 2023 NEC, and Scottsdale runs the 2021 I-codes with the 2020 NEC.

What it means for a builder or homeowner

The edition gap is not academic. It changes framing details, wiring rules, and energy specs, and those changes hit your plans and your budget.

  • A plan set is not portable across the line. A custom-home plan drawn to Phoenix's 2024 codes is not automatically correct for a lot in Carefree or Casa Grande on the 2018 codes, and the reverse is also true. Plan reviewers check against the edition their jurisdiction adopted, so a reused set from the wrong city is the most common and most expensive plan-review correction.
  • Energy paperwork varies the most. Phoenix and Fountain Hills enforce the 2024 energy code, Scottsdale the 2021, and Cave Creek keeps the 2018 energy code for houses. Maricopa County treats energy as voluntary. The insulation R-values, duct-leakage testing, and window U-factors you have to prove change with that edition, and so does the cost.
  • Electrical follows a different book per city. An electrician quoting a Phoenix job works from the 2023 NEC. The same electrician on a Maricopa County lot a few miles away works from the 2017 NEC, two cycles back. Arc-fault and ground-fault protection requirements differ between them.
  • Effective dates create transition windows. Edition is tied to when your permit was issued, not today's calendar. A permit pulled in Phoenix before August 1, 2025 may still run under the prior 2018 code. A Fountain Hills application received before September 1, 2025 may still run under the prior cycle. If your project straddles an effective date, confirm which edition applies before your designer finalizes the set.

For example, say you own two lots, one in Phoenix and one in Casa Grande, and you want the same floor plan on both. You cannot submit one identical set to both offices. The Phoenix reviewer checks the energy sheet against the 2024 IECC and the wiring against the 2023 NEC. The Casa Grande reviewer checks the same home against the 2018 IECC and the 2017 NEC. The framing, the duct sealing, and the panel can all come back with different corrections. Each lot needs its plans confirmed to its own jurisdiction's edition before a permit issues.

How this connects to the rest of the module

This hub is the map; each spoke has the full detail. For the exact code list, amendments, and effective dates in your jurisdiction, see the per-city entries: Scottsdale , Phoenix , Maricopa County , Cave Creek , Fountain Hills , Carefree , Casa Grande , and Apache Junction . For the bigger picture of where code sits in the build, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and what building codes apply in Scottsdale .

Source

Arizona's local-adoption structure is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona . Each edition above is confirmed against the jurisdiction's own published adoption, including the City of Scottsdale Building Code Information and the City of Phoenix Building Construction Codes . The remaining cities and towns each publish their own adoption, linked from the per-city entries above.

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and effective dates change on each jurisdiction's own schedule, so confirm the current adopted code with the local building department before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona
  • City of Scottsdale Building Code Information
  • City of Phoenix Building Construction Codes

title: Arizona Building Permit Fees: Construction Valuation vs Per-Square-Foot Pricing
shortSummary: Arizona cities price a new-home building permit one of two ways. Most (Cave Creek, Casa Grande, Mesa, Maricopa County) charge by construction valuation, run through a tiered table. A few (Scottsdale, Fountain Hills) charge by square foot. The same home can land at very different permit fees depending on the method.
bodyHtml:
Arizona has no statewide building permit fee, so each city and county writes its own. When you price a new-home permit across the Phoenix metro, the schedules split into two camps. Most jurisdictions charge by construction valuation, the estimated dollar value of the work. A few charge by square foot instead. Knowing which method your jurisdiction uses tells you what drives your permit bill, and it explains why the same floor plan can cost more to permit on one side of a city line than the other. This page lines the two methods up and works the same home through both.

The two methods, and who uses which

A valuation-based permit fee starts with a number: the estimated cost to build your home. The city sets that valuation, usually from the ICC Building Valuation Data (national cost-per-square-foot figures) or your signed contract, then reads the permit fee off a tiered table. The fee climbs in steps as the valuation rises. A per-square-foot permit fee skips valuation and multiplies a published rate by the square footage of your home, often splitting air-conditioned area from covered non-A/C area like garages and patios.

Here is how the jurisdictions Jematell Homes builds in line up:

JurisdictionMethodBasis
Cave CreekValuationICC table via Town Code 151.14; $300k value = $2,984 permit fee; plan review 65% of permit
Casa GrandeValuationICC table; $200k+ = $1,800 + $7.00 per $1,000; plan review 65% + 5% tech fee
MesaValuationTotal valuation run through a residential rate table; deposit at submittal
Maricopa CountyValuationTable 1-A; $500k value = $3,233.75 permit fee + 65% plan review
CarefreeValuationTown Code Table 3-A; valuation from engineer's certified estimate
Apache JunctionValuationICC Building Valuation Data (Aug 2018 table)
Paradise ValleyValuationBuilding valuation data in the Town Master Fee Schedule
ScottsdalePer square foot$195 base + $0.94/sq ft A/C area + $0.54/sq ft covered non-A/C
Fountain HillsPer square footPer-unit basis (square foot, linear foot) under the Town fee schedule

So the valuation camp is the larger one. Scottsdale and Fountain Hills are the two clear per-square-foot exceptions among these jurisdictions.

Why the methods produce different bills

The two methods reward and penalize different things. A valuation fee tracks the value of your home, so high-end finishes, structural complexity, and a big contract price push the permit fee up even if the square footage is modest. A custom estate with a small footprint but a large budget pays a high valuation fee. A per-square-foot fee tracks area, so a large but plainly finished home pays more than a small luxury one, and a deep garage or covered patio adds to the bill at the covered-area rate. The same house is not priced the same way under the two systems.

There is a second, bigger distinction worth keeping straight. The permit fee (valuation or square foot) pays for plan review and inspections. It is not the same as an impact fee , the separate charge a city collects to pay for the water, sewer, roads, parks, and fire capacity new growth uses. Arizona authorizes those development fees under their own statute, A.R.S. 9-463.05:

A municipality may assess development fees to offset costs to the municipality associated with providing necessary public services to a development.

That statute runs on its own schedule, no matter how the city prices the building permit. So a full new-home budget has two layers. One is the permit fee, priced by valuation or square foot. The other is the impact or development fee, priced by meter size, per dwelling, or service area.

The same home, priced two ways

Take one custom home and run it through both methods: 3,000 square feet of air-conditioned living space, a 600 square foot garage, and a 400 square foot covered patio (1,000 square feet of covered non-A/C area), with a construction valuation of about $500,000.

Per square foot, in Scottsdale. The math is the $195 base, plus 3,000 times $0.94 ($2,820) for the A/C area, plus 1,000 times $0.54 ($540) for the covered area, plus the $379 Certificate of Occupancy and a $363 GIS fee. That is roughly $4,297 in building-permit charges, driven almost entirely by square footage.

By valuation, in Maricopa County. The same home, valued at $500,000, lands at the top of the county's Table 1-A bracket for a $3,233.75 permit fee, plus a 65 percent plan review of about $2,102, for roughly $5,336 before drainage and inspections, driven entirely by the $500,000 value.

Same house, two methods, two different totals, and the gap widens if you change the inputs. Add expensive finishes that raise the valuation to $800,000 and the county fee climbs while the Scottsdale square-foot fee does not move. Add a huge garage and the Scottsdale fee climbs while the valuation fee barely notices. That is the practical point: the method decides what raises your permit bill.

What this means for you

Find out which method your jurisdiction uses before you budget the permit, because it tells you what to watch. In a valuation city (Cave Creek, Casa Grande, Mesa, Maricopa County, Carefree, Apache Junction, Paradise Valley), build an honest cost estimate, because the city can use the ICC table value or verify your number, and the fee follows the value. In a per-square-foot city (Scottsdale, Fountain Hills), count your square footage carefully, split A/C from covered area, and remember that garages and covered patios add to the bill. In both, treat the impact or development fee as a separate, often larger line. We price the permit on the right method for the jurisdiction on day one, so the building-department line in your budget is real, not a guess.

This hub ties the per-city fee entries together. For the full numbers in your jurisdiction, see the per-city fee pages: Scottsdale , Phoenix , Mesa , Cave Creek , Fountain Hills , Casa Grande , Maricopa County , Carefree , Apache Junction , and Paradise Valley . For the separate development charge, see impact fees . For a plain-language overview, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The per-square-foot rates are from the City of Scottsdale FY2025-26 Single Family Residential permit fee schedule . The valuation brackets and 65 percent plan review are from the Maricopa County Summary of the Fee Schedule (Table 1-A) and the City of Casa Grande Fee Schedule . The development-fee statute is A.R.S. 9-463.05 . Each jurisdiction's full figures are confirmed in its per-city fee entry above.

This page compares methods for general planning. Every jurisdiction resets its fee schedule on its own cycle, so confirm the current permit fee and impact fee with the local building department before you rely on a number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Permit Fee Schedule, Single Family Residential (FY2025-26)
  • Maricopa County Planning and Development Summary of the Fee Schedule (Table 1-A)
  • City of Casa Grande City Fee Schedule (FY2025-26 Consolidated Fee Schedule)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Carefree Adopted Building Codes: The 2018 I-Codes That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Effective January 24, 2023, the Town of Carefree builds to the 2018 International Residential, Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fuel Gas, Energy, and Fire codes, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code. The Town Council adopted them by reference in Ordinance 2022-02, with local amendments in Town Code Section 10-1-2.
bodyHtml:
Your home in the Town of Carefree is checked against a stack of national model codes that the Town Council voted to adopt by reference, then edited for local desert conditions. Carefree runs the 2018 editions, which are a cycle or two behind newer neighbors like Cave Creek and Fountain Hills, so the exact rules differ depending on which town line your lot sits inside. This page lists the codes Carefree actually enforces today, when they took effect, and the ordinance that adopted them.

Which codes Carefree enforces

Carefree adopted the 2018 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for most construction, plus the 2017 electrical code. The editions took effect January 24, 2023. Here is the current list the Town's Building Department enforces, as written into Town Code Section 10-1-2:

  • 2018 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2018 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2018 International Plumbing Code (IPC), with Appendix C: water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2018 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2018 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2018 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2018 International Fire Code (IFC): fire and life-safety provisions.
  • 2017 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the code a single-family builder lives in day to day. The Town of Carefree is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its Building Official, not the county and not the state, has the final word inside town limits.

One point matters for plumbing. The Town's older handout still listed the 1994 Uniform Plumbing Code, but the codified ordinance in Section 10-1-2 adopts the 2018 International Plumbing Code. The codified Town Code controls, so build to the 2018 IPC and confirm the live plumbing code with the Building Department before you draw fixtures and drains.

What the Town says

The codified ordinance states each adoption plainly. Section 10-1-2 opens its residential provision this way:

Effective January 24, 2023, there is herewith adopted, by reference, the International Residential Code, 2018 Edition, published by the International Code Council.

The same section repeats that pattern for the IBC, IPC, IMC, IFGC, IECC, IFC, and the 2017 NEC, each adopted by reference and then changed by Carefree's local amendments. So the rule you build to is the published 2018 edition as modified by the Town's amendments for that code. The adopting ordinance is Ordinance 2022-02, which amended Carefree's building code to the 2018 editions and replaced the Town's prior 2006-era adoptions.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your framing, bracing, and foundation details follow the 2018 IRC as amended. A plan drawn to a newer 2021 or 2024 set may carry details Carefree's older code does not require or match, and the reviewer checks against the 2018 text.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2017 NEC. An electrician working from a 2020 or 2023 code book is ahead of what Carefree adopted, which is usually fine but should be confirmed against the 2017 base.
  • Energy compliance runs on the 2018 IECC as amended, a lighter path than towns on the 2024 energy code, but you still have to hit the 2018 numbers.

For example, suppose you submit a 3,000 square foot custom home plan in Carefree in 2026. The plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2018 IRC, your wiring against the 2017 NEC, your gas piping against the 2018 IFGC, and your energy sheet against the 2018 IECC. If your designer reused a set drawn to Fountain Hills' or Cave Creek's 2024 codes, several editions will not line up, and the plan can come back with corrections before a permit issues. Edition is tied to the code in effect for your permit, not the newest book on the shelf.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction. Build to Carefree's 2018 I-codes and the 2017 NEC, with the Town's amendments applied, and use the 2018 IPC for plumbing. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition or to a neighboring town's newer code. Carefree sits behind Cave Creek and Fountain Hills on the code cycle, so a set drawn for one of those towns is not automatically right for a Carefree lot, and the reverse is also true.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Carefree building-codes module. From here, see Carefree Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review and what you file to get a permit. If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county and state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, the editions, and the effective date are written into the codified Town Code: Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 on American Legal Publishing . The Town's plain-language building information is published by the Town of Carefree Building Department . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with the Town of Carefree Building Department before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 (Building Code Amendments)
  • Town of Carefree Building Department
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Carefree Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Carefree inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. You request inspections through the Town's Permit and License Portal, and a request must be in by 4 p.m. for the next available day. The final inspection leads to your Certificate of Occupancy.
bodyHtml:
In Carefree, your new home is checked at fixed milestones as it goes up, and each one has to clear before the next trade seals its work behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The last milestone is the final inspection, and passing it is what releases your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. Below is the order, Carefree's own scheduling rule, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence

The inspector follows the build upward and catches each stage while it is still visible. A new home runs through this order:

  1. Foundation. After the footing trench and steel are set, before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical under the floor are placed, before the slab covers them.
  3. Roof and framing. When sheathing, tie-downs, walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are in, before any cover-up.
  4. Insulation. Before drywall.
  5. Lath. Before stucco or plaster.
  6. Final. When the home is complete and all fixtures are set, before occupancy.

The order holds because an inspector cannot approve hidden work. Suppose a framer finishes a 2,700 square foot Carefree home and the drywallers board the walls before the framing inspection is called. The inspector cannot see the wiring, plumbing rough-in, or fire blocking, so the inspection fails. The crew cuts the fresh drywall back open, re-inspects, then re-patches, costing a week and labor. The Town of Carefree is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , and its Building Department sets and enforces this sequence. Because Carefree's adopted codes are the 2018 I-codes, these inspection points follow the 2018 IRC, a cycle behind newer neighbors like Cave Creek.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You request inspections through the Town's online Permit and License Portal, which runs on SmartGov (twn-carefree-az.smartgovcommunity.com). Carefree sets a clear daily cutoff:

Inspection requests must be submitted by 4PM in the Permit & License Portal in order to be requested for the next available day.

The Town also notes your preferred date is not guaranteed, and you get a notice once the inspection is officially scheduled with a confirmed date and time. If you have a question about the portal, Town Hall answers at 480-488-3686. Keep your permit number ready when you request.

Nothing clears you to move in until the final passes. A passing final on a new home is what lets the Town issue the Certificate of Occupancy, the record that the house meets code and may be lived in. Carefree's portal also handles a separate Building Safety Inspection used when a business needs a Certificate of Occupancy, but for a new house the construction final is your path to the CO. Do not let any crew conceal wiring, plumbing, or framing before its inspection passes.

When this comes into play

This runs across the whole build, not only at the finish:

  • Pour days. A footing or slab cannot be poured until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the 4 p.m. request deadline shapes your concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical are inspected before insulation and drywall. A failure here halts the cover-up.
  • Move-in. The final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy. A sale or mortgage often needs the CO first.

What this means for you

Plan your trades around the 4 p.m. portal cutoff, because a request that misses it costs you a full day on the next pour or close-in. Set up your Permit and License Portal account early, request inspections there, and remember the confirmed date comes back from the Town, not from you. Never bury wiring, plumbing, or framing before it is signed off. Wait for the Certificate of Occupancy before moving family in, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Carefree module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Carefree Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Carefree Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the build start to finish, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The 4 p.m. inspection request cutoff and the Permit and License Portal are published by the Town of Carefree: Building Department on carefree.org . For the portal itself and permit steps, see Permits and Licenses .

This is a plain-language summary, not an official interpretation of the code. Carefree may change its cutoff, portal, or CO steps over time, so confirm today's process with the Town's Building Department before you submit a request.

Sources

  • Town of Carefree Building Department
  • Town of Carefree Permits and Licenses
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Carefree Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: The Valuation Basis in Table 3-A
shortSummary: Carefree prices a new-home building permit by construction valuation, set from an engineer's certified cost estimate and run through Table 3-A in Town Code Section 10-1-2(G), under Chapter One of the adopted building code. Carefree charges no city water or sewer impact fees, since most lots use a private well and septic.
bodyHtml:
A new home in the Town of Carefree carries a building permit fee driven by one number: the construction valuation of your house. Carefree does not price the permit by square foot the way Scottsdale does. It runs the fee off a valuation table written into the Town Code, the same approach most Arizona towns use. This page gives you the real basis from Carefree's own ordinance, so you can budget the permit line instead of guessing, and it flags where the live dollar figure has to come from the Town.

How Carefree sets the permit fee

Carefree builds the permit fee from the value of the work, and the Town Code is specific about where that value comes from. The building permit fee provisions live in Section 10-1-2(G) of the Town Code, and they point to two things: a fee table and the adopted building code. The Town's posted resource states it directly:

Building permit fees are found in Table 3-A of Section 10-1-2(G) of the Town Code, and valuation is based on an engineer's certified estimate of the cost of the proposed improvements. All fees shall be in accordance with Chapter One of the International Building Code as adopted by the Town of Carefree.

So the process runs in two steps. First, the Town sets your home's valuation, the estimated construction cost. Carefree ties that figure to an engineer's certified estimate of the cost of the work, not a number you pick yourself, which is stricter than the ICC-table default some towns use. Second, the fee comes off Table 3-A in the Town Code, the tiered schedule that follows Chapter One of the adopted 2018 International Building Code. The permit fee climbs as the valuation climbs, so a larger or more expensive home pays a larger permit fee. Plan review and inspection charges follow the same Chapter One structure, which in the I-code model sets plan review as a share of the permit fee.

The exact dollar figures in Table 3-A are set by the Town and updated through its fee schedule, which Carefree posted as effective June 6, 2025. The Town's codified building-permit table is the controlling source, but the live per-tier dollar amounts are not reliably machine-readable from the Town site. So treat the basis here (valuation from an engineer's estimate, fee from Table 3-A under IBC Chapter One) as the verified part, and pull the current dollar figure for your valuation from the Carefree Building Department or the posted fee schedule.

For a sense of scale, take a custom home with an engineer's certified construction cost of $600,000. The Town sets the valuation at that $600,000 figure, then reads the permit fee off the matching Table 3-A tier, and adds the Chapter One plan review charge on top, which in the I-code model is a percentage of the permit fee. The number you owe rises step by step with the valuation, so a $1 million home pays more than a $600,000 one. Because the per-tier dollars are set by the Town, ask for the Table 3-A figure at your valuation rather than working from a rule of thumb.

Why there are no city water or sewer impact fees

Impact fees are charges a city collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth uses, authorized in Arizona under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Cities like Phoenix and Fountain Hills charge them for municipal water, sewer, parks, and fire. Carefree generally does not, because Carefree runs no town water or town sewer utility. A typical Carefree home sits on a private well or a water-hauling arrangement and an on-site septic system, which you permit and pay for separately.

That changes the budget math. Inside a fee-charging city, impact fees can be the single largest line on a new home. On a Carefree lot, your big separate costs are the septic system permit and install and the well drilling, which run through Maricopa County and the state, not a town impact-fee menu. So the permit fee plus its plan review, and then your well and septic, are the real numbers, not a stack of municipal impact fees.

When these fees come into play

  • Build-on-your-lot. You apply through Carefree's permit portal or the Building Department, supply an engineer's certified cost estimate, and the Town sets your valuation and invoices the Table 3-A permit fee plus plan review.
  • Valuation is verified, not self-declared. Because Carefree ties valuation to a certified estimate, a low number does not lower your fee the way it might where the town just takes the contract price. Build the estimate honestly.
  • Well and septic, not city utilities. Plan the well and septic as their own permits and real install costs. On a rural Carefree lot those usually dwarf the building permit fee.

What this means for you

Budget the Carefree permit as a valuation-based fee, the same family as Cave Creek and Casa Grande, not a per-square-foot fee like Scottsdale. Get an engineer's certified cost estimate early, since that figure drives the permit fee directly. Then budget your well and septic separately, because Carefree has no town water or sewer impact fee to fold in. The dollar amounts in Table 3-A change when the Town updates its fee schedule, and the live figures are hard to pull from the Town site, so confirm the current per-valuation fee with the Carefree Building Department at 480-488-3686 before you rely on a number here.

This entry sits in the Carefree building-codes module alongside Carefree Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Carefree Adopted Building Codes , which covers the 2018 codes your plans are checked against. For how the basis differs across the metro, see valuation versus per-square-foot permit pricing in Arizona and how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The valuation basis, the Table 3-A reference, the engineer's-estimate rule, and the IBC Chapter One basis are written into the Town of Carefree's codified ordinance, a public record: Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 on American Legal Publishing . The current dollar figures live in the Town's Comprehensive Fee Schedule , and the permit process is described by the Town of Carefree Building Department . The statute behind impact fees is A.R.S. 9-463.05 .

These fees change when the Town updates its fee schedule, and the live Table 3-A figures are not easily pulled from the Town site. Confirm the current permit fee for your valuation, and your well and septic costs, with the Carefree Building Department before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • Town of Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 (Building Code Amendments and Permit Fees)
  • Town of Carefree Notice of New Fees (Comprehensive Fee Schedule)
  • Town of Carefree Building Department
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Carefree Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and the Steps
shortSummary: A new home in the Town of Carefree needs a Town building permit before construction starts. You apply through the Town's online Permit and License Portal with a scaled site plan, construction drawings, and your parcel information. The Building Department, Town Engineer, and Fire Marshal review the plans, and the permit issues after fees are paid.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new home in the Town of Carefree until the Town issues a building permit. The permit is the Town's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes, your zoning, and your site conditions. The Carefree Building Department handles this for any lot inside town limits, working alongside the Town Engineer, the Planning and Zoning Director, and the Fire Marshal. This page walks the real steps: what you gather, how you submit it, how the Town reviews it, and what it takes to get the permit in hand.

What a residential permit requires

Carefree runs new-home permits through its online Permit and License Portal, the Town's SmartGov system, not over the counter. A complete residential package comes together from these pieces:

  • A site plan, drawn to scale and legible, showing the house on the lot with setbacks (the required distance from each property line), grading, and drainage called out.
  • Your construction plans, drawn to the Town's adopted 2018 I-codes, covering framing, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and energy.
  • An engineer's certified cost estimate for the work, because Carefree bases its permit valuation and fees on that estimate, not on a generic per-foot table.
  • Your parcel number, owner and property information, and contact details.
  • Your project's zoning and lot-coverage information for your district, since the Planning and Zoning Director confirms the build fits the zoning.

The Town's codified permit rule sets a real deadline you should know up front:

Every remodel and addition shall be completed within 180 days from the date the permit is issued, or the permit will expire.

A package that is incomplete or a site plan that is not to scale gets handed back before review, which costs you days. Getting the site plan to scale, the setbacks right, and the engineer's estimate attached the first time is the cheapest way to keep the review moving.

How review, fees, and issuance work

After you submit through the Permit and License Portal, the Building Department coordinates the review with the Town Engineer, the Planning and Zoning Director, and the Fire Marshal, checking your plans against the adopted Town codes and your zoning. If corrections are required, you fix the sheets and resubmit through the portal. When the plans clear and the permit fees are paid, the permit issues, and you schedule inspections through the same system.

Two practical details matter. First, fees are set by Table 3-A in Town Code Section 10-1-2, and the permit valuation is based on your engineer's certified estimate of the cost of the work, so a higher-cost custom home carries a higher permit fee. Second, inspection requests must be submitted in the portal by 4 p.m. to be scheduled for the next available day, so you plan field inspections a day ahead rather than same-day.

Here is a real example. Suppose you submit a 2,800 square foot custom home in Carefree with a site plan that shows the house too close to the front property line for your zoning district. The Planning and Zoning Director flags the setback in review. You shift the footprint or seek relief, then resubmit through the portal. That single correction round can add a week or two before the permit issues, which is why the site plan setbacks are worth getting right the first time.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Carefree build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the site plan, the construction set, and the engineer's cost estimate, you submit through the portal, and the review clock runs through any correction rounds before the permit issues.
  • Septic and well first. Many Carefree lots have no Town sewer or water main. Your septic approval and your private well run on a separate track through Maricopa County and the state, not the Town, and the two have to line up on the same site plan before you build.
  • Zoning fit. The permit confirms your home meets Carefree's zoning for your district, including setbacks and lot coverage. A design that overbuilds the lot or crowds a property line gets caught here, before construction.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item, and have your engineer's certified cost estimate ready, because Carefree prices the permit off that number. Get the site plan to scale and the setbacks right the first time, because a setback problem is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Draw the set to Carefree's current 2018 I-codes, not a neighboring town's newer edition, so review does not stall on a wrong-code sheet. Request field inspections through the portal by 4 p.m. the day before you need them. On a rural lot, line up your septic and well work in parallel, since the Town building permit alone does not give you water or wastewater. When the home passes its final inspections, the Town issues the certificate of occupancy that lets you legally move in.

This entry sits next to the codes in the Carefree module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Carefree Adopted Building Codes . If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county and state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the full picture of a custom build, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The Building Department's role, the online Permit and License Portal, and the inspection cutoff are published by the Town of Carefree: Building Department on carefree.org and Permits & Licenses . The permit rules, fee table, and valuation method are written into Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the Town's current applications and checklists. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with the Town of Carefree Building Department before you file.

Sources

  • Town of Carefree Building Department
  • Town of Carefree Permits & Licenses
  • Carefree Town Code Section 10-1-2 (Building Code Amendments)

title: Carefree Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Big Desert Lots and Where the House Goes
shortSummary: Carefree's residential zones require deep yards and a low 24 foot height cap. Rural-70 needs a 60 foot front and rear yard and 30 foot sides at 13% coverage. Rural-43 drops to a 40 foot front and rear at 17%. The smaller R1-18 needs a 30 foot front and 10 foot sides at 25% coverage.
bodyHtml:
Carefree protects its desert and mountain character with deep yards and a low roofline, so the setback, the open distance your home must keep from each property line, is larger here than in most Phoenix-area cities. The town's main residential zones run from Rural-190 (190,000 square feet, over four acres) down to R1-10 (10,000 square feet), and every one of them caps building height at 24 feet. Those big lots come with big setbacks and tight coverage limits.

What the districts require

Carefree's Section 6.01 sets one development-standards table for every zone. The residential districts share a 24 foot height cap, but their yards and coverage step down as lots get smaller:

  • Rural-190: 190,000 sq ft min lot, front 60 ft, side 30 ft, rear 60 ft, lot coverage 6%, max disturbed area 18%.
  • Rural-70: 70,000 sq ft min lot, front 60 ft, side 30 ft, rear 60 ft, lot coverage 13%, max disturbed area 39%.
  • Rural-43: 43,560 sq ft min lot, front 40 ft, side 30 ft, rear 40 ft, lot coverage 17%, max disturbed area 51%.
  • R1-35: 35,000 sq ft min lot, front 40 ft, side 20 ft, rear 40 ft, lot coverage 20%, max disturbed area 60%.
  • R1-18: 18,000 sq ft min lot, front 30 ft, side 10 ft, rear 30 ft, lot coverage 25%, max disturbed area 75%.
  • R1-10: 10,000 sq ft min lot, front 20 ft, side 7 ft, rear 25 ft, lot coverage 30%.

The town states its height rule from the natural ground, not an average:

No part of any structure may rise more than twenty-four (24) feet above natural and finished grade directly below.

That wording matters on Carefree's rocky, sloped lots, where a tall roof over a low spot still counts against the 24 foot limit. The town also caps any single wall at 18 feet without a 6 foot break, so long flat walls have to step.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks, coverage, and disturbance drive the site plan before you draw the house:

  • Build-on-your-lot. For example, on a Rural-43 acre, the 40 foot front and rear plus 30 foot sides carve a wide border out of the parcel, and the 17% coverage cap limits the house-plus-garage footprint. The lot coverage cap and the setbacks are separate tests, and the house has to pass both.
  • Disturbed-area cap. Carefree also limits how much of the lot you may disturb at all, counting driveways, graded pads, and septic. Rural-70 allows only 39% disturbance, so even the cleared ground is capped.
  • Mountainside lots. Parcels on slopes face extra rules, including a stricter 12 foot height where a structure crosses a ridgeline, and reduced disturbance.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a Carefree plan gets kicked back, so the town confirms each yard distance before the permit moves forward.

What this means for you

Confirm your Rural or R1 district first, because a Rural-70 front yard of 60 feet pushes the house far off the road, while an R1-18 lot needs only 30 feet. Plan the footprint against the coverage cap early, since 6% on a Rural-190 lot is generous in raw square feet but still ties the house size to the parcel. Remember Carefree measures height from natural grade and caps it at 24 feet, lower than the 30 feet allowed in Phoenix or Scottsdale, so a steep lot does not buy you extra height and a two-story plan has less room. We map the setbacks, coverage cap, and disturbance limit together on day one, because in Carefree the land rules, not just the floor plan, decide what fits.

This entry connects to the rest of the Carefree module. The codes your plans are reviewed against are in Carefree Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Carefree Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The district setbacks, 24 foot height cap, coverage, and disturbed-area limits are published by the Town of Carefree in Section 6.01 of the Zoning Ordinance: view Section 6.01 on amlegal.com . The town's zoning hub is at Carefree Planning and Zoning , with the full ordinance on the Zoning Documents page .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks, coverage, and mountainside rules change with conservation-easement options and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Carefree Planning before you design.

Sources

  • Town of Carefree Zoning Ordinance, Section 6.01 (Intensity Schedule and Development Standards)
  • Town of Carefree Planning and Zoning
  • Town of Carefree Zoning Documents

title: Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes: The 2018 I-Codes That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: The City of Casa Grande builds to the 2018 International Residential, Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fuel Gas, Energy, and Fire codes, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code. The City Council adopted them by reference in Title 15 of the City Code, with local amendments made public record by Resolution No. 5170.
bodyHtml:
Your home in the City of Casa Grande is checked against a stack of national model codes that the City Council voted to adopt by reference, then edited for local desert conditions. Casa Grande sits in Pinal County, not Maricopa, so the city is its own permit office and its own code authority. This page lists the codes Casa Grande actually enforces today, where they live in the City Code, and the resolution that adopted them.

Which codes Casa Grande enforces

Casa Grande adopted the 2018 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for most construction, plus the 2017 electrical code. They are written into Title 15 of the City Code, chapter by chapter. Here is the current list the city's Building Division enforces:

  • 2018 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2018 International Building Code (IBC), with Appendices C and I: larger and commercial structures.
  • 2018 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2018 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2018 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2018 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2018 International Fire Code (IFC): fire and life-safety provisions.
  • 2017 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the code a single-family builder lives in day to day. The City of Casa Grande is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its Building Official, not Pinal County and not the state, has the final word inside city limits.

Casa Grande also runs a local administrative layer on top of the I-codes. The City of Casa Grande Building and Technical Administrative Code, 2019 Edition sets the rules for permits, fees, inspections, and the Building Official's authority. It is the procedural spine that ties the technical codes together.

What the City Code says

Each code is adopted by reference in its own chapter of Title 15. The building code chapter, Section 15.08.010, states the adoption plainly:

The International Building Code, 2018 Edition, including Appendices C and I, and the City of Casa Grande Amendments to the International Building Code, 2018 Edition, copies of which were made a public record by Resolution No. 5170 of the City of Casa Grande, Arizona, are hereby adopted by reference.

The same pattern repeats for the IRC, IPC, IMC, IFGC, IECC, IFC, and the 2017 NEC, each adopted by reference and then changed by Casa Grande's local amendments on file with the City Clerk. So the rule you build to is the published 2018 edition (2017 for electrical) as modified by the city's amendments for that code.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your framing, bracing, and foundation details follow the 2018 IRC as amended. A plan drawn to a newer 2021 or 2024 set may carry details Casa Grande's adopted code does not require or match, and the reviewer checks against the 2018 text.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2017 NEC. An electrician working from a 2020 or 2023 code book is ahead of what Casa Grande adopted, which is usually fine but should be confirmed against the 2017 base.
  • Energy compliance runs on the 2018 IECC as amended. You still have to hit the 2018 insulation, duct, and window numbers, even if newer Phoenix-metro cities are on a tighter energy code.

For example, suppose you submit a 2,400 square foot custom home plan in Casa Grande in 2026. The plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2018 IRC, your wiring against the 2017 NEC, your gas piping against the 2018 IFGC, and your energy sheet against the 2018 IECC. If your designer reused a set drawn to Scottsdale's 2021 codes, several editions will not line up, and the plan can come back with corrections before a permit issues. Edition is tied to the code in effect for your permit, not the newest book on the shelf.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction. Build to Casa Grande's 2018 I-codes and the 2017 NEC, with the city's amendments applied. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition or to a different city's newer code. Because Casa Grande is in Pinal County, do not assume a Maricopa-metro plan set carries over without a check.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Casa Grande building-codes module. From here, see Casa Grande Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review and what you file to get a permit. If your lot is on septic or a private well, that side runs through Pinal County and the state, not the city, so confirm onsite wastewater with the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division and wells with the Arizona Department of Water Resources. For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, the editions, and the adopting resolution are written into the codified City Code: Casa Grande City Code Title 15 on American Legal Publishing . The city's plain-language building information is published by the City of Casa Grande Building Division . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with the Casa Grande Building Division before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • Casa Grande City Code Title 15 (Buildings and Construction)
  • City of Casa Grande Building Division
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Casa Grande Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Casa Grande inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered. You schedule through the WebPermits portal, with an 11:59 p.m. cutoff. The Building Final, plus a Planning Final and a DC Engineering Final, generates your Certificate of Occupancy.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Casa Grande gets checked at set points while it is built, and each point has to pass before a crew seals its work behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The chain ends with a final inspection, and on a new house Casa Grande pairs that with two more finals to release your Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that legally lets you move in. This page lays out the order, the city's WebPermits scheduling rule, and how the finals generate the CO.

The inspection sequence

The inspector follows the build from the ground up and catches each stage while the work is still open. Casa Grande lists the required inspections on the record card attached to your permit, and a new home runs roughly this order:

  1. Foundation. After the footing trench and rebar are set, before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical under the floor are placed, before the slab buries them.
  3. Roof and framing. When sheathing, tie-downs, walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are all in, before any cover-up.
  4. Insulation. Before drywall.
  5. Lath. Before stucco or plaster.
  6. Final. When the home is finished and every fixture is set, before occupancy.

The order is firm because the city is explicit that "Work shall not be concealed prior to obtaining an inspection." For example, suppose a framer finishes a 2,600 square foot Casa Grande home and the drywall crew boards the walls before the framing inspection is called. Because the wiring, plumbing rough-in, and fire blocking are hidden behind that board, the inspector cannot approve them and the inspection fails. The crew cuts the new drywall back open, re-inspects, then re-patches, costing a week and labor. The City of Casa Grande is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) . Casa Grande sits in Pinal County, so the city, not the county, runs these inspections, and its adopted codes are the 2018 I-codes.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You schedule, cancel, and reschedule inspections through the city's WebPermits portal (rogue.casagrandeaz.gov/WebPermits), and you need your permit number. Casa Grande runs on a same-day cutoff:

The City does not provide same-day inspections unless the inspection is received by 11:59 pm on the inspection date. Inspection requests received after 11:59 pm will be scheduled for the following business day.

Inspectors arrive any time from 6 a.m. to 3:30 p.m., so plan the site to be ready early. To cancel a same-day inspection you call the office at 520-421-8630, and to request a time window you email insptimerequest@casagrandeaz.gov by 11:59 p.m. the morning of the inspection.

Casa Grande makes the finish line a group effort. On a new home the city ties three inspections together:

When requesting a Building Final, you will also be required to add a Planning Final and DC Engineering Final inspection to close out the permit and generate a Certificate of Occupancy.

So the Building Final alone is not enough. The Planning Final and the Development and Construction Engineering Final have to pass too, and together they generate the Certificate of Occupancy, the record that the home meets code and may be lived in.

When this comes into play

This spans the whole construction phase:

  • Pour days. A footing or slab cannot be poured until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the WebPermits schedule drives the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical are inspected before insulation and drywall. A failure here halts the cover-up.
  • Move-in. The three finals and the Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy. A closing or mortgage often needs the CO first.

What this means for you

Schedule through WebPermits and remember the 11:59 p.m. cutoff, because a late request bumps you to the next business day and can stall a pour. Have the site ready by 6 a.m. on inspection day, since the inspector can come anytime in the window. Most important on a new home, do not treat the final inspection as a single step. Add the Planning Final and DC Engineering Final with your Building Final, or the permit will not close and the CO will not generate. Never conceal wiring, plumbing, or framing before its inspection, and do not move in before the Certificate of Occupancy issues, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Casa Grande module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Casa Grande Residential Building Permit Requirements . To set inspections in the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The WebPermits scheduling rule, the 11:59 p.m. cutoff, and the three-final Certificate of Occupancy step are published by the City of Casa Grande: Building Inspections on casagrandeaz.gov . For the Building Division overview, see Building .
