title: Casa Grande Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: The ICC Valuation Table and Development Fees
shortSummary: Casa Grande prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, using the latest ICC Building Valuation Table on a tiered fee scale. Building plan review is 65 percent of the permit fee, plus a 5 percent technology recovery fee. Separate development impact fees, set in the city's Infrastructure Improvements Plan, are due before the permit issues.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Casa Grande, in Pinal County, carries two separate buckets of city money. First, a building permit and plan review fee, priced off the value of your construction. Second, development impact fees, the larger charge that pays for the roads, water, sewer, parks, and public safety your home will use. This page gives you the real basis for each from the city's official FY2025-26 schedule, so you can set a line item instead of guessing.

How Casa Grande calculates the permit fee

Casa Grande prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, and the city is specific about where that value comes from:

Building Permit fees are calculated on the values in the latest ICC Building Valuation Table, or upon the actual construction costs contained within a verifiable signed construction contract, whichever are less.

So the city sets your home's valuation from the ICC Building Valuation Table (the national cost-per-square-foot figures) or your signed contract, whichever is lower. The permit fee then comes off a tiered scale. For a home valued between $200,001 and $1,000,000, the fee is $1,800 for the first $200,000 plus $7.00 for each additional $1,000. For example, a home that values out at $450,000 pays $1,800 plus 250 times $7.00 ($1,750), so about $3,550 in building permit fee.

Plan review stacks on top. The city charges building plan review at 65 percent of the building permit fee. On that $450,000 home, the 65 percent plan review adds about $2,308, bringing the building-department total to roughly $5,858 before trade permits. Then add the technology recovery fee, which is "5% of the permit and land use application fees" with a $750 maximum, collected on all permits. Electrical, mechanical, and plumbing permits for a residence are $75 each. A Certificate of Occupancy issued with the building permit is "no charge, included in permit," while a temporary CO is $200.

The development impact fees are separate and larger

Impact fees are charges a city collects on new construction to fund the public infrastructure that growth requires. Arizona authorizes them under A.R.S. 9-463.05, which also forces a biennial audit of each city's fees. Casa Grande sets its impact fees in its Infrastructure Improvements Plan (IIP), a separate schedule from the building permit fee. The city's 2024 audit report confirms the categories in force. These include water, wastewater, and streets. Wastewater is charged by service zone, with a Zone A rate and a Zone B rate. The city's code is direct about timing:

Development impact fees shall be paid prior to issuance of a building permit.

So the impact fees are due up front, as part of the cash you bring to pull the permit, not a later bill. They are usually the largest single city charge on the whole project, well above the permit and plan review fees. The exact per-unit dollar amounts live in the adopted IIP schedule rather than in the consolidated fee schedule, so pull the current figure for your address and service zone from the city.

There is a protection built in. Under A.R.S. 9-463.05, once the first building permit issues for a platted single-family development, the city locks that development's impact fee schedule for 24 months at the permittee's request.

When these fees come into play

  • At permit application. The city sets your valuation, then invoices the permit fee, the 65 percent plan review, and the 5 percent technology recovery fee.
  • Before the permit issues. Development impact fees are collected before the permit is released, so budget them as start-of-construction cash.
  • Watch the service zone. Casa Grande's wastewater impact fee differs by zone, and the 2024 audit found a few permits charged the wrong zone, so confirm your zone in writing.

What this means for you

Budget the permit fee and the 65 percent plan review together, since the plan review tracks the permit, then add the 5 percent technology recovery fee. Get an honest valuation, because the city uses the ICC table value when it is lower than your contract, and the fee follows the valuation. Treat the development impact fees as the big, up-front number, and ask the city for the current IIP amount for your specific address and service zone, because that figure is not in the consolidated fee schedule and it resets on its own cycle. If you have a platted single-family lot, ask the city to lock your impact-fee schedule for 24 months when your first permit issues.

This entry sits in the Casa Grande building-codes module alongside Casa Grande Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans are checked against. For how these costs compare across the metro, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The ICC valuation basis, the tiered permit fee figures, the 65 percent plan review rule, the 5 percent technology recovery fee, and the CO charges above are reproduced from the City of Casa Grande's official FY2025-26 Consolidated Fee Schedule, a public record: Consolidated Fee Schedule on casagrandeaz.gov . The impact-fee categories and timing come from the city's 2024 Development Impact Fees Report and A.R.S. 9-463.05 .

These fees change by city action, and impact-fee amounts live in a separate schedule that updates on its own cycle. Confirm the current permit fee, plan review, and impact-fee figures with Casa Grande Planning and Development before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Casa Grande FY2025-26 Consolidated Fee Schedule (PDF)
  • City of Casa Grande City Fee Schedule page
  • City of Casa Grande Development Impact Fees Report (2024)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Casa Grande Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and Where
shortSummary: A new home in Casa Grande needs a building permit before any work starts. You apply through the city's Development Center with a residential site plan, floor plans, and engineered construction documents, then wait for a multi-discipline plan review. Initial review typically runs about 20 days before the permit can issue.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Casa Grande home until the city issues a building permit. The permit is the city's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted 2018 I-codes and your lot's zoning. Casa Grande runs permitting through a single Development Center, so building, engineering, fire, and planning staff review your project together. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how it gets reviewed, and what the timeline looks like.

What a residential permit requires

Casa Grande handles development through a Development Center that the city calls a "One-Stop Shop," where the public, contractors, and consultants submit plans and pull permits in one place. A new single-family home, addition, accessory structure, or pool triggers a Residential Site Plan plus a full building-permit package. A complete submittal generally includes:

  • A residential site plan showing the house on the lot, with setbacks (the required distance from each property line), drainage, access, and utilities called out.
  • Floor plans, one sheet per level.
  • Structural and construction documents engineered for Arizona loads, including the foundation designed for local soils.
  • Energy compliance paperwork showing the design meets the 2018 IECC as amended.
  • A completed building permit application.

The city is clear about what its review covers:

The city reviews submitted plans for building code compliance, structural integrity, fire safety, energy code, and ADA accessibility.

That means five separate lenses are looking at your set at once. A clean package addresses each one up front, instead of leaving a reviewer to bounce the plan for a missing energy sheet or an undersized footing detail.

For example, say you submit a 2,400 square foot custom home on a one-acre lot at the edge of the city. The Development Center routes your set to building, structural, fire, energy, and planning reviewers at the same time. If your site plan shows the house four feet from a side line where the zone requires five, the planning reviewer flags the setback, and the whole package waits on that one correction before a permit issues, even if every other discipline cleared.

How review and timeline work

Casa Grande reviews a residential plan through several disciplines before a permit issues. The initial review cycle generally takes around 20 calendar days. If the reviewers find corrections, you fix the sheets and resubmit, and each round adds time. A clean first submittal is the cheapest path, because every correction cycle pushes your start date out.

The flow is: assemble the site plan and building package, submit to the Development Center, let staff complete the multi-discipline review, clear any corrections, then pay fees and receive the permit. Because Casa Grande is in Pinal County, a project outside the city sewer system also needs a separate septic permit from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, and a private well runs through the Arizona Department of Water Resources. Those approvals sit alongside the city building permit, not inside it.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Casa Grande build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the site plan and engineered set, the Development Center runs its review, and the permit issues only after corrections clear.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121 only covers a home you intend to occupy, not one you build to sell or rent within a year. That legal limit decides whether you can sign as the permit holder.
  • Septic and well lots. On unsewered land, the county septic permit and the state well registration have to be lined up with the city build, so plan those timelines in parallel.

What this means for you

Budget the permit as a real line item and start the site plan early, because setbacks and drainage are common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder, since that decides who signs and carries responsibility. Build the roughly 20-day initial review, plus any correction cycles, into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant. If your lot is unsewered, open the Pinal County septic file at the same time, not after the house permit, so the two tracks finish together.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Casa Grande module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes . The permit ends, much later, with a certificate of occupancy once final inspections pass. For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The Development Center process and the scope of plan review are published by the City of Casa Grande Building Division . For zoning, site plans, and broader development steps, see Casa Grande Planning & Development . Arizona's owner-builder limit is set in state law at A.R.S. 32-1121 .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the city's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with the Casa Grande Development Center before you file.

Sources

  • City of Casa Grande Building Division
  • City of Casa Grande Planning & Development
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Casa Grande Residential Zoning and Setbacks: District Yards for a New Home
shortSummary: Casa Grande's R-1 single-family zone needs a 7,000 square foot lot with a 15 foot front yard, a 20 foot setback for a street-facing garage, a 5 to 10 foot side yard, and a 20 foot rear yard. Larger rural zones run deeper. Maximum building height across residential zones is 28 feet.
bodyHtml:
In Casa Grande, your zoning district sets how far the house must sit from each property line, a distance called the setback. Casa Grande is in Pinal County, so the city writes and enforces its own zoning, separate from Maricopa County. The residential zones range from compact R-1 and R-1a lots to large Rural Ranch and Suburban Homestead parcels, and the required yards change with the district.

What the districts require

Casa Grande's Title 17.20 sets a 28 foot building-height limit across its residential zones, but the lot size and yards differ by district:

  • R-1 (Single-Family Residential): 7,000 sq ft lot, front yard 15 ft, side yard 5 ft on one side and 10 ft on the other, rear yard 20 ft. A street-facing garage door must sit back at least 20 ft.
  • R-1a (Single-Family Residential): 5,000 sq ft lot, front yard 15 ft, side yard 3 ft on one side with 10 ft on the other, rear yard 20 ft.
  • UR (Urban Ranch): 54,450 sq ft lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 10 ft, rear yard 40 ft.
  • RR (Rural Ranch): 144,000 sq ft lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 10 ft, rear yard 40 ft.
  • SH (Suburban Homestead): 87,120 sq ft lot, front yard 30 ft, side yard 10 ft, rear yard 40 ft.

The R-1 zone spells out the garage rule directly:

A garage door, or the front of a carport, that faces a street shall have a minimum setback of 20'.

That extra distance keeps a car parked in the driveway from hanging over the sidewalk or street, which is why the garage face sits back farther than the rest of the house.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks shape the build before the floor plan is final:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer subtracts the front, side, and rear yards from the lot to find the buildable footprint. For example, on a standard 70 by 100 foot R-1 lot, the 15 foot front, 20 foot rear, and 5 and 10 foot sides leave a building band roughly 55 feet wide and 65 feet deep.
  • The split side yard. R-1's 5-and-10 side rule means one side stays tight and the other stays open, so the house cannot be centered without losing buildable width. R-1a's 3-and-10 rule is even tighter on the close side.
  • Garages. Because a street-facing garage needs 20 feet while the house needs only 15, a front-loaded garage sets the controlling front line on many Casa Grande lots.

A setback error on the site plan is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected, so the city checks each yard before issuing the permit. Casa Grande controls home size mainly through lot dimensions and yards rather than a single published lot coverage percentage for these zones, so the setbacks do most of the work of sizing the footprint.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact zone before you design, because R-1 and R-1a look similar but the close side yard differs by two feet, and the rural zones push the rear yard out to 40 feet. Plan the garage placement early, since the 20 foot garage setback often controls the front of the house. Remember the 28 foot height cap applies across the residential zones, so a taller roofline is not an option without relief. We map the yards and the garage line on day one of a Casa Grande project, because they decide whether a plan fits the lot.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Casa Grande module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Casa Grande Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The district lot sizes, setbacks, and the 28 foot height limit are published by the City of Casa Grande in Title 17.20 of the City Code: View Chapter 17.20, Residential Zones on amlegal.com . The city's zoning-district overview is at Casa Grande Zoning Districts .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks change with overlays, planned developments, and amendments, so confirm your lot's zone and standards with Casa Grande Planning and Development before you design.

Sources

  • Casa Grande City Code, Chapter 17.20 (Residential Zones)
  • City of Casa Grande Zoning Districts
  • City of Casa Grande Planning and Development

title: Cave Creek Adopted Building Codes: The 2024 I-Codes That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Effective January 1, 2026, the Town of Cave Creek builds to the 2024 International Residential and Building codes, the 2024 International Mechanical, Fuel Gas, and Energy codes, the 2023 National Electrical Code, and the 2025 Plumbing, Fire, and Existing Building codes. The Town Council adopted them by reference through Resolution R2025-01.
bodyHtml:
Your home in the Town of Cave Creek is checked against a stack of national model codes that the Town Council voted to adopt by reference, then edited for local desert conditions. Cave Creek runs newer editions than the unincorporated county next door, so the exact rules differ depending on which side of the town line your lot sits. This page lists the codes Cave Creek actually enforces today, when they took effect, and the resolution that adopted them.

Which codes Cave Creek enforces

Cave Creek adopted the 2024 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for most construction, with the 2023 electrical code and a few 2025-edition codes layered in. The editions changed on January 1, 2026. Here is the current list the Town's Building and Fire Code Compliance Division enforces:

  • 2024 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2024 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2024 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2024 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2024 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, and HVAC efficiency for new commercial work.
  • 2023 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.
  • 2025 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2025 International Fire Code (IFC): fire and life-safety provisions.
  • 2025 International Existing Building Code (IEBC): work on existing structures.
  • 2024 International Pool and Spa Code (ISPSC): pools and spas.

One wrinkle is worth knowing. For homes, Cave Creek keeps the 2018 residential energy code rather than the 2024 energy code. So a single-family build follows the older, lighter energy path. The IRC is the code a single-family builder lives in day to day. The Town of Cave Creek is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its Building Official, not the county and not the state, has the final word inside town limits.

What the Town says

The codified ordinance states the adoption plainly. Chapter 151 of the Town Code opens its building section with the effective date and the adopting resolution:

Effective January 1, 2026, there is herewith adopted, by reference, The International Building Code, 2024 Edition, as published by the International Code Council, adopted as a public record by Resolution No. R2025-01 of the Mayor and Council of the Town of Cave Creek.

Each code in the stack carries its own section in Chapter 151 (Building at 151.01, Residential at 151.02, Mechanical at 151.03, Plumbing at 151.04, Electrical at 151.05, Fire at 151.06), and each section then lists the Town's local amendments to that code. So the rule you build to is the published edition as changed by Cave Creek's amendments for that code. The Town's older codes came in under Resolution R2022-03 in 2022; the current 2024-cycle set replaced them on the January 1, 2026 effective date.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your framing, bracing, and foundation details follow the 2024 IRC as amended. A plan copied from an older 2018 or 2021 set may carry details the current code no longer matches, and it can get bounced in review.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2023 NEC. An electrician working from a 2017 or 2020 code book may miss newer required protections.
  • Residential energy compliance still runs on the 2018 energy code, not the 2024 edition. That keeps insulation and duct paperwork lighter than a city like Scottsdale requires, but you still have to meet the 2018 numbers.

Here is a real example. Say you submit a 3,000 square foot custom home plan in Cave Creek in early 2026. The plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2024 IRC. Your wiring is checked against the 2023 NEC. Your gas piping is checked against the 2024 IFGC. Your home energy sheet is checked against the 2018 residential energy code. If your designer reused a set drawn to Maricopa County's 2018 codes, several editions will not line up. The plan can then come back with corrections before a permit issues. A permit application started under the prior 2021 code cycle is reviewed case by case.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction. Build to Cave Creek's 2024 I-codes and the 2023 NEC, with the Town's amendments applied, and use the 2018 residential energy code for a house. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition or to a neighboring jurisdiction's code. Cave Creek sits ahead of the surrounding unincorporated county (still on the 2018 codes and 2017 NEC), so a set drawn for a rural county lot is not automatically right for a town lot, and the reverse is also true.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Cave Creek building-codes module. From here, see Cave Creek Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review and what you file to get a permit. If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, the editions, and the effective date are published by the Town of Cave Creek: Building and Fire Code Compliance on cavecreekaz.gov . The adopting ordinance text and the per-code amendments live in Cave Creek Town Code Chapter 151: Building Regulations . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with the Town of Cave Creek Building and Fire Code Compliance Division before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • Town of Cave Creek Building and Fire Code Compliance
  • Cave Creek Town Code Chapter 151: Building Regulations (American Legal Publishing)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Cave Creek Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Cave Creek inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. You request every inspection through the Town's SmartGov permitting portal. After the final inspection passes, the Town issues a Certificate of Occupancy that lets you legally move in.
bodyHtml:
Building a home in Cave Creek means the Town watches the work at fixed points, and every point has to clear before the next trade buries its work behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The chain ends with a final inspection that releases your Certificate of Occupancy, the paper that legally lets you move in. This page walks the order, how Cave Creek wants each inspection requested, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence

Inspections track the build from the dirt up, and the inspector calls each one while the work is still in plain view. For a new house the order runs:

  1. Foundation. After the footing trench is dug and the steel reinforcement is tied, before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical lines under the floor are set, before the slab covers them.
  3. Roof and framing. When sheathing, tie-downs, walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are all in, before anything gets closed up.
  4. Insulation. Before drywall goes on.
  5. Lath. Before stucco or plaster is applied.
  6. Final. When the house is finished and every fixture is set, before anyone occupies it.

This order exists because an inspector cannot sign off on what is already hidden. Say a framer wraps a 2,900 square foot Cave Creek house and the drywall crew hangs board before the framing inspection is called. The inspector cannot see the wiring, the plumbing rough-in, or the fire blocking, so the inspection fails. The crew cuts out fresh drywall to expose the framing, then re-inspects and re-patches, which can cost a week and real labor dollars. The Town of Cave Creek is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , so its Building and Fire Code Compliance Division sets and enforces this sequence. The Town's adopted codes are the 2024 I-codes, so these inspection points follow the 2024 IRC.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You request every inspection through the Town's online portal. Cave Creek moved all of this to SmartGov, and the page is plain that the portal is the only door:

All access to permits, including inspection requests, plan reviews, payments, etc., must go through the Town's permitting portal.

You log in at the SmartGov public site (twn-cavecreek-az.smartgovcommunity.com), open your permit, and request the inspection type you need. There is also a recorded inspection request line at 480-488-7092, and the Building Safety office answers at 480-488-6622 during Monday through Thursday hours. Have your permit number ready either way.

Everything funnels toward the final inspection. A passing final on a new home is what lets the Town issue the Certificate of Occupancy, the document that says the house meets code and may be lived in. Do not let a crew conceal wiring, plumbing, or framing before its inspection passes, because a failed inspection means cutting the cover back open.

When this comes into play

This runs across the whole build, not just the end:

  • Pour days. You cannot pour a footing or slab until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the inspection calendar drives the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical all get checked before insulation and drywall. A failure here halts the cover-up.
  • Move-in. The final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy. A sale closing or a mortgage often needs the CO in hand.

What this means for you

Treat the inspection calendar as the backbone of your schedule. Each phase has to pass before the next trade covers its work, so one missed or failed inspection slides everything after it. Set up your SmartGov account early, request inspections through the portal, and keep your permit number handy. Never bury wiring, plumbing, or framing before it is signed off. Do not move family or furniture in before the Certificate of Occupancy issues, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Cave Creek module loop. The codes your work is measured against are in Cave Creek Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Cave Creek Residential Building Permit Requirements . To see where inspections sit in the larger build, walk the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The inspection process and portal-only scheduling rule are published by the Town of Cave Creek: Building and Fire Code Compliance on cavecreekaz.gov . For portal access and the inspection request details, see Online Permitting and Inspection Service .

Treat this as a plain-language overview, not an official code reading. Cave Creek can revise its inspection steps and CO rules, so verify the current process with the Town's Building and Fire Code Compliance Division before you book.

Sources

  • Town of Cave Creek Building and Fire Code Compliance
  • Town of Cave Creek Online Permitting and Inspection Service
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Cave Creek Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: How the Valuation Table Works
shortSummary: Cave Creek prices a new-home permit by construction valuation, using the ICC Building Valuation Table set in Town Code Section 151.14. The permit fee climbs with valuation, and plan review is $50 or 65 percent of the permit fee, whichever is greater. The Town's posted table is dated, so confirm the live fee.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Cave Creek carries a building permit fee that is driven by one number: the construction valuation of your house. Cave Creek does not price the permit by square foot the way Scottsdale does. It runs the fee off a valuation table, the same approach most Arizona cities use. This page gives you the real fee basis from the Town's official schedule, so you can budget the building-permit line instead of guessing.

How Cave Creek calculates the permit fee

Cave Creek sets the permit fee from the value of the work, and the Town's schedule is explicit about where that value comes from:

All permit fees based on valuation shall be derived from Table 1-A, as published in Section 151.14 of the Cave Creek Town Code. Refer to the most recent publication of the ICC Building Valuation Table for all values not listed below.

So the Town first establishes your home's valuation, the estimated construction cost. For items it lists itself, it uses set per-square-foot figures: a residential garage is valued at $55.50 per square foot, a covered patio or carport at $21.60, an Arizona room at $100.00. For the house itself it points to the ICC Building Valuation Table, the national cost-per-square-foot figures the International Code Council updates. Once the valuation is set, the fee comes off the Town's tiered table. On that table, a $300,000 valuation lands a $2,984 permit fee, and a $500,000 valuation lands a $4,584 permit fee. Above $1,000,000 the fee is $8,003 for the first million plus $5.00 for each additional $1,000.

Plan review is separate and stacks on top. The Town states it plainly: plan review fees are a minimum of $50 or 65 percent of the permit fee, whichever is greater. For example, on a home that values out to $500,000, the $4,584 permit fee carries a plan review fee of about $2,980 (65 percent), for roughly $7,560 in building-department charges before trade permits. Electrical, plumbing, mechanical, pool, and wall permits are billed on their own lines.

Where impact and development fees fit

The fee above is the building-department charge. It is not the same as impact fees , which are charges a city collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth uses. Arizona lets towns levy them under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Cave Creek's posted building schedule covers permit and plan review fees, not a separate municipal impact-fee menu. The biggest infrastructure cost most Cave Creek homes face is water. Many lots here connect to the Cave Creek Water Company. Others rely on a well and septic system instead of a city sewer. So your largest single number is often the water connection or meter charge, billed by the utility, not a line on the building permit. Confirm your water and wastewater path early, because it can dwarf the permit fee.

When these fees come into play

  • Build-on-your-lot. You apply through Cave Creek Development Services, and the Town sets your valuation, then invoices the permit and the 65 percent plan review fee at application.
  • Garages and covered patios. Because the Town values a garage at $55.50 per square foot and a covered patio at $21.60, a big garage or deep covered patio raises the valuation, and therefore the fee, on a real and predictable basis.
  • Permits pulled after work starts. The Town doubles both the permit and plan review fees for work that began before a permit was obtained, so do not let a subcontractor jump the gun.

What this means for you

Budget the permit fee and the 65 percent plan review fee together, since the plan review tracks the permit. Get an honest valuation, because the Town can use the ICC table value rather than your contract if your number looks low, and the fee follows the valuation. Pin down your water and septic situation early, since that is usually the largest infrastructure cost on a Cave Creek lot. One caution on the numbers: the Town's posted valuation table carries an old effective date, and fees are reset by ordinance, so treat every dollar figure here as a planning estimate and confirm the current schedule with Cave Creek Development Services before you rely on it.

This entry sits in the Cave Creek building-codes module alongside Cave Creek Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Cave Creek Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans are checked against. For how these costs compare across the metro, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The valuation basis, the Town Code 151.14 reference, the ICC table reference, the tiered fee figures, and the 65 percent plan review rule are reproduced from the Town of Cave Creek's official permit fee schedule, a public record: Valuation Schedule and Permit Fees on cavecreekaz.gov . The governing ordinance is Cave Creek Town Code Chapter 151 .

These fees change by Town ordinance, and the posted table is dated. Confirm the current schedule and your water and septic costs with Cave Creek Development Services before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • Town of Cave Creek Valuation Schedule and Permit Fees (PDF)
  • Town of Cave Creek Development Services
  • Town of Cave Creek Permit Fees and Valuation page
  • Cave Creek Town Code, Chapter 151 (Building Regulations)

title: Cave Creek Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and the Steps
shortSummary: A new home in the Town of Cave Creek needs a Town building permit before construction starts. You submit digitally through the Town's online permitting portal with a site plan, the required documents, and your parcel information. The Building and Fire Code Compliance Division reviews the plans for code and zoning compliance, and the permit issues after fees are paid.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new home in the Town of Cave Creek until the Town issues a building permit. The permit is the Town's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes, your zoning, and your site conditions. The Town's Development Services Department, through its Building and Fire Code Compliance Division, handles this for any lot inside town limits. This page walks the real steps: what you gather, how you submit it, how the Town reviews it, and what it takes to get the permit in hand.

What a residential permit requires

Cave Creek runs new-home permits through an online portal, not over the counter. The Town moved to paperless permitting and now requires digital submittals. A complete residential package comes together from these pieces:

  • A site plan, drawn to scale and legible, showing the house on the lot with setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out. The Town publishes a residential site plan review worksheet to guide this.
  • Your construction plans, drawn to the Town's adopted 2024 I-codes, for framing, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and energy.
  • Your other required documents for the project type, submitted as digital files through the portal.
  • Your parcel number, owner and property information, and contact details.
  • Your project's lot coverage and zoning information for your district, since the permit also confirms the build fits the zoning.

The Town describes the division's job directly:

The Building and Fire Code Compliance Division ensures quality construction for the Town's residents by regulating building construction and building occupancy.

A package that is incomplete or a site plan that is not to scale gets handed back before review, which costs you days. If you cannot digitize a sheet yourself, the Town charges a per-sheet fee to scan it for you, so it is cheaper to submit clean digital files.

How review and issuance work

After you submit through the online permitting portal, the Building and Fire Code Compliance Division reviews your plans for compliance with the adopted Town codes and your zoning. A Town representative also decides during plan review whether a site inspection is needed. If corrections are required, you fix the sheets and resubmit through the portal. When the plans clear and the permit fees are paid, the permit issues, and you schedule inspections through the same system. The Town's published fee schedule sets what you owe.

Here is a real example. Suppose you submit a 2,800 square foot custom home in Cave Creek, with a site plan that shows the house too close to the front property line for your zoning district. Staff flags the setback in review. You shift the footprint or seek relief, then resubmit. That single correction round can add a week or two before the permit issues, which is why the site plan setbacks are worth getting right the first time. A plan started under the prior 2021 code cycle is reviewed case by case, so a current 2024-code set moves faster.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Cave Creek build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the site plan and engineered documents, you submit through the online portal, and the review clock runs through any correction rounds before the permit issues.
  • Septic and well first. A rural Cave Creek lot often has no Town sewer or water. Your septic approval and your private well run on a separate track through Maricopa County and the state, not the Town, and the two have to line up on the same site plan before you build.
  • Zoning fit. The permit confirms your home meets the Town zoning for your district, including setbacks and lot coverage. A design that overbuilds the lot or crowds a property line gets caught here, before construction.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item, and prepare clean digital files, because the Town no longer takes paper plans. Get the site plan to scale and the setbacks right the first time, because a setback problem is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Draw the set to Cave Creek's current 2024 I-codes, not a neighboring jurisdiction's edition, so review does not stall on a wrong-code sheet. Build review and correction time into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant. On a rural lot, line up your septic and well work in parallel, since the Town building permit alone does not give you water or wastewater. When the home passes its final inspections, the Town issues the certificate of occupancy that lets you legally move in.

This entry sits next to the codes in the Cave Creek module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Cave Creek Adopted Building Codes . If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the full picture of a custom build, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The division's role, the paperless permitting requirement, and the adopted codes are published by the Town of Cave Creek: Building and Fire Code Compliance on cavecreekaz.gov and Development Services . The site plan worksheet and zoning resources are at Building Permit Zoning Information . The codes your plans are reviewed against are adopted in Cave Creek Town Code Chapter 151: Building Regulations .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the Town's current applications and checklists. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with the Town of Cave Creek Development Services Department before you file.

Sources

  • Town of Cave Creek Building and Fire Code Compliance
  • Town of Cave Creek Development Services
  • Town of Cave Creek Building Permit Zoning Information
  • Cave Creek Town Code Chapter 151: Building Regulations (American Legal Publishing)

title: Cave Creek Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Desert Rural Lots and Where the House Goes
shortSummary: Cave Creek's Desert Rural (DR) zones require big yards. DR-190, DR-89, and DR-70 each need a 60 foot front and rear yard and a 30 foot side yard, capped at 10% lot coverage. DR-43 drops to 40 foot front and rear and 20 foot sides at 15%. Building height tops out at 25 feet.
bodyHtml:
Cave Creek protects its desert character with deep yards, so the setback, the open distance your home must keep from each property line, is larger here than in most Phoenix-area cities. The town's main residential zones are the Desert Rural (DR) districts, named for their minimum lot size in square feet. A DR-190 lot is over four acres; even the smallest DR district sits on nearly an acre. Those big lots come with big setbacks and tight lot-coverage limits.

What the districts require

Cave Creek's Chapter 2 sets the bulk regulations for each residential zone. The Desert Rural zones share a 25 foot, two-story height limit, but their yards and coverage step down as lots get smaller:

  • DR-190 / DR-89 / DR-70: front and rear yard 60 ft, side yard 30 ft, maximum lot coverage 10%.
  • DR-43: front and rear yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, maximum lot coverage 15%.
  • R-35 (Single Residence): 35,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, lot coverage 20%.
  • R-18 (Single Residence): 18,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 30 ft, interior side 10 ft, street side 20 ft, lot coverage 25%.

The town measures building height to the true high point, not an average:

The maximum building height is the calculated distance from original natural grade to the highest point on the roof through any section.

That wording matters on Cave Creek's sloped desert lots, where a tall roof over a low spot still counts against the 25 foot limit.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks and coverage drive the site plan before you draw the house:

  • Build-on-your-lot. For example, on a DR-43 acre, the 40 foot front and rear plus 20 foot sides carve a sizable border out of the parcel, and the 15% coverage cap limits the house-plus-garage footprint. The lot coverage limit and the setbacks are separate tests, and the house has to pass both.
  • Total land disturbance. Cave Creek also caps how much of the lot you may disturb, counting driveways, septic, and graded areas on top of the building footprint. In DR-43 that is 15% coverage plus 60% disturbance, for 75% total.
  • Hillside lots. Parcels in a designated hillside area face stricter disturbance limits, so the buildable pad can shrink further.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a Cave Creek plan gets kicked back, so the town confirms each yard distance before the permit moves forward.

What this means for you

Confirm your DR or R district first, because a DR-70 front yard of 60 feet pushes the house far off the road, while an R-18 lot only needs 30 feet. Plan the footprint against the lot-coverage cap early, since 10% on a DR-190 lot is generous in raw square feet but still ties the house size to the parcel. Remember Cave Creek measures height from natural grade to the highest roof point, so a steep lot does not buy you extra height. We map the setbacks, coverage cap, and disturbance limit together on day one, because in Cave Creek the land rules, not just the floor plan, decide what fits.

This entry connects to the rest of the Cave Creek module. The codes your plans are reviewed against are in Cave Creek Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Cave Creek Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The district setbacks, height limit, and lot-coverage caps are published by the Town of Cave Creek in Chapter 2 of the Zoning Ordinance (Residential Zones), effective December 4, 2025 and amended by Ordinance O2025-13. Find the current Chapter 2 document on the town's Ordinances & Guidelines page . The town's zoning hub is at Cave Creek Planning & Zoning .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks, coverage, and disturbance rules change with hillside overlays and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Cave Creek Planning before you design.

Sources

  • Town of Cave Creek Ordinances & Guidelines (Zoning Ordinance Chapter 2, Residential Zones)
  • Town of Cave Creek Planning & Zoning
  • Maricopa County Assessor parcel and zoning lookup

title: Corner Lot and Double-Frontage Setbacks in Arizona: The Street-Side Yard That Shrinks Your Buildable Area
shortSummary: A corner or through lot in Arizona carries a second front-style setback on its extra street frontage, called a street-side yard. It runs deeper than the interior side yard a mid-block lot uses, so the buildable area is smaller. Phoenix, Scottsdale, Mesa, Cave Creek, and Apache Junction each set their own street-side rule.
bodyHtml:
A corner lot in Arizona is not as buildable as it looks. Because it touches a street on two sides, the zoning code treats both street frontages like a front yard, so the lot carries a second deep setback (the open distance a house must keep from a property line) where a mid-block lot would have only a narrow side yard. That extra setback is the street-side yard, and it quietly shrinks the footprint you can build. The same thing happens on a through lot (also called a double-frontage lot), one that runs from one street to another with a front line on each end. This page explains the rule, shows each metro city's number, and flags why it trips up plan review.

What a street-side yard is

On a normal interior lot, the house keeps a deep front yard from the street, a deep rear yard, and two narrow side yards from the neighbors. A corner lot replaces one of those narrow side yards with a street-side yard, because that side now faces a street. The street-side yard is wider than an interior side yard, though usually not as wide as the true front. A through lot gets a front-style setback on both street ends, so it loses depth on both sides instead of keeping a rear yard.

The effect is the same in every case: the open border around the house grows, and the buildable area (the footprint left after you subtract the setbacks) shrinks. On a corner lot the squeeze hits the width on the street side. On a through lot it hits the depth. Either way, a floor plan that fits a mid-block lot may not fit the corner or through lot next door.

Each metro city sets its own street-side rule

The street-side number is set by each jurisdiction's own zoning code, so it varies across the cities Jematell Homes builds in:

  • Phoenix. Districts like R1-14 and RE-24 set an interior side yard of 10 feet but a street side yard of 15 feet, so the corner frontage gives up 5 extra feet. The ordinance lists them separately.
  • Scottsdale. A corner lot carries a front yard on each street frontage, so both streets get the full front setback, which can squeeze the buildable area more than a single interior side would.
  • Mesa. A through lot whose rear faces an arterial street must keep that rear yard at least 30 feet deep, which pushes the house forward and can leave two front-style yards on a double-frontage lot.
  • Cave Creek. In the R-18 Single Residence zone the interior side yard is 10 feet but the street side yard is 20 feet, double the interior side, on the corner frontage.
  • Apache Junction. Table 5-2 sets a separate street side column for every district. On an RS-7 lot the interior side is 7 feet while the street side is 10 feet; on the large RS-GR lot both interior and street side run 20 feet.
  • Fountain Hills. The Section 10.09 yard chart carries footnotes that adjust some yards for corner and canyon-side conditions, common on the town's sloped terrain.

So the same corner condition can cost you 5 extra feet in Phoenix, 10 extra feet in Cave Creek, or a full second front yard in Scottsdale. Apache Junction's code spells out how broadly a corner lot's footprint gets counted against the coverage cap, naming every roofed structure:

The footprints of all principal and accessory structures, including garages, carports, covered patios, and covered porches, shall be added together in order to calculate lot coverage.

The setback that shapes the footprint changes city to city, so you have to read your specific district's street-side number, not assume it matches the city next door.

When this comes into play

The street-side yard shapes the site plan before the house is drawn, and it is a recurring setback trap at plan review:

  • Reused plans. A plan that fit an interior lot gets dropped onto a corner lot, and the street-side yard cuts into the footprint. This is one of the most common corrections a residential plan gets on a corner parcel.
  • Garages and driveways. A corner lot often has to place the garage and driveway off the lower-traffic street, and the street-side setback plus sight-distance rules at the intersection limit where the driveway can go.
  • Lot coverage stacks on top. The street-side setback shrinks the footprint, and the lot coverage cap limits it again. The house has to pass both tests, so a corner lot can feel smaller than its square footage suggests.

For example, take a Cave Creek R-18 lot. A mid-block R-18 house keeps 10 foot interior side yards. The corner lot next door keeps a 10 foot interior side on the neighbor side but a 20 foot street side on the street side, so it loses 10 feet of buildable width that the mid-block lot keeps. Drop the same floor plan on both and only the mid-block one fits without a redesign or a variance.

What this means for you

Check whether your lot is a corner or through lot before you design, because the street-side or second-front setback changes the buildable area and the answer is in your district's own code. Pull the exact street-side number for your zone (Phoenix 15 feet on many districts, Cave Creek 20 feet in R-18, Apache Junction per Table 5-2, Scottsdale a full front yard on each street), since it varies city to city. Plan the garage and driveway around the corner setback and the intersection sight lines early, because they often decide which street you enter from. We map both street frontages, the street-side setback, and the lot-coverage cap together on day one of a corner-lot project, so the floor plan fits the parcel the first time instead of bouncing through review.

This explainer ties the per-city zoning entries together. For your district's full yard table, see the per-city zoning pages: Scottsdale , Phoenix , Mesa , Cave Creek , Fountain Hills , Apache Junction , Casa Grande , and Maricopa County . For the underlying terms, see setback and lot coverage . For the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The street-side and corner-lot setbacks above are published in each jurisdiction's zoning code: the City of Phoenix Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 6 , the City of Scottsdale Zoning Ordinance, Article V , the City of Mesa Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 5 , and the Apache Junction Land Development Code, Section 1-5-2 . Each city's full district table is confirmed in its per-city zoning entry above.

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Corner and through-lot rules change with overlays, hillside standards, and amendments, so confirm your lot's classification and street-side setback with the local planning department before you design.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 6 (Residential Districts)
  • City of Scottsdale Zoning Ordinance, Article V (District Regulations)
  • City of Mesa Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 5 (Residential Districts)
  • Apache Junction Land Development Code, Section 1-5-2 (Residential Bulk Regulations)

title: Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes: The 2024 I-Codes That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: For permit applications received on or after September 1, 2025, the Town of Fountain Hills builds to the 2024 International Residential, Building, Mechanical, Plumbing, Fuel Gas, Energy, Fire, and Swimming Pool and Spa codes, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code. The Town Council adopted them by reference in Ordinance 25-06, with local amendments.
bodyHtml:
Your home in the Town of Fountain Hills is checked against a stack of national model codes that the Town Council voted to adopt by reference, then edited for local desert and hillside conditions. Fountain Hills moved to the newest 2024 editions in 2025, so the exact rules differ from older sets and from neighboring jurisdictions still on the 2018 cycle. This page lists the codes Fountain Hills actually enforces today, when they took effect, and the ordinance that adopted them.

Which codes Fountain Hills enforces

Fountain Hills adopted the 2024 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2023 electrical code. The editions changed for permit applications received on or after September 1, 2025. Here is the current list the Town's Building Safety Division enforces:

  • 2024 International Residential Code (IRC), with Appendix G: one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2024 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2024 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2024 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2024 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2024 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2024 International Fire Code (IFC): fire and life-safety provisions.
  • 2024 International Swimming Pool and Spa Code (ISPSC): pools and spas.
  • 2023 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.
  • 1997 Uniform Code for the Abatement of Dangerous Buildings: unsafe-structure abatement.

The IRC is the code a single-family builder lives in day to day. The Town of Fountain Hills is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its Building Official, not the county and not the state, has the final word inside town limits.

What the Town says

The codified ordinance states the adoption plainly. Article 7-1 of the Town Code opens its building section by adopting each publication by reference:

The following listed publications, three copies of which are on file in the office of the Town Clerk and which are available for public inspection during normal business hours, are hereby adopted by reference, together with all appendices and supplements thereto.

The list that follows names the 2024 IBC, IMC, IPC, IFGC, IECC, IFC, ISPSC, and the 2024 IRC for One- and Two-Family Dwellings, plus the 2023 National Electrical Code. The Town then adds its own "2024 Fountain Hills Amendments" to the IBC, IRC, IECC, ISPSC, and IFC, so the rule you build to is the published 2024 edition as changed by those amendments. One wrinkle is worth knowing. Fountain Hills keeps a strong residential fire-sprinkler requirement, and the Town's ordinance ties any sprinkler cross-reference back to the 2006 Fire Code as amended, which it adopted in 2008, so the sprinkler rule is older than the rest of the stack.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your framing, bracing, and foundation details follow the 2024 IRC as amended, with Appendix G in play. A plan copied from an older 2018 or 2021 set may carry details the current code no longer matches, and it can get bounced in review.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2023 NEC. An electrician working from a 2017 or 2020 code book may miss newer required protections.
  • Energy compliance (insulation R-values, duct sealing, window U-factors) runs on the 2024 IECC as amended, which is a tighter path than towns still on a 2018 energy code.
  • A new home almost always needs a fire-sprinkler system here, because the Town's amendments require sprinklers across new occupancies. That is a real cost line a Fountain Hills build carries that some jurisdictions do not.

For example, suppose you submit a 3,000 square foot custom home plan in Fountain Hills in late 2025. The plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2024 IRC, your wiring against the 2023 NEC, your gas piping against the 2024 IFGC, and your energy sheet against the 2024 IECC. If your designer reused a set drawn to the 2018 codes a neighboring area still uses, several editions will not line up, and the plan comes back with corrections before a permit issues. Because edition is tied to when the application is received, a package filed before September 1, 2025 may still run under the prior code.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction. Build to Fountain Hills' 2024 I-codes and the 2023 NEC, with the Town's amendments applied, and plan for a residential fire-sprinkler system. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition or to a neighboring jurisdiction's code. Fountain Hills sits ahead of areas still on the 2018 cycle, so a set drawn for an older code is not automatically right for a town lot, and the sprinkler requirement alone can change your mechanical and water budget.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Fountain Hills building-codes module. From here, see Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review and what you file to get a permit. If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county and state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, editions, and effective date are published by the Town of Fountain Hills: Adopted Codes on fountainhillsaz.gov . The adopting ordinance text and the per-code amendments live in the codified Town Code Chapter 7, Buildings and Building Regulations , which Ordinance 25-06 repealed and replaced. Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with the Town of Fountain Hills Building Safety Division before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • Town of Fountain Hills Adopted Codes
  • Fountain Hills Town Code Chapter 7: Buildings and Building Regulations (Ordinance 25-06)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Fountain Hills Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Fountain Hills inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. You schedule every inspection through the Town's TOPPS portal, and inspections run Monday through Friday. After the final passes, the Town issues a Certificate of Occupancy that lets you move in.
bodyHtml:
The Town of Fountain Hills checks a new home at set points during construction, and each point has to pass before the next crew hides its work under concrete, drywall, or stucco. The last check is the final inspection, and clearing it releases your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. Here is the order, how Fountain Hills wants each inspection scheduled, and how the final leads to the CO.

The inspection sequence

The inspector follows the build from the ground up and catches each stage while the work is still exposed. A new home moves through roughly this order:

  1. Foundation. After the footing excavation and rebar are set, before concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical runs below the floor are in, before the slab buries them.
  3. Roof and framing. When sheathing, tie-downs, walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are complete, before any of it is covered.
  4. Insulation. Before drywall.
  5. Lath. Before stucco or plaster.
  6. Final. When the home is finished and all fixtures are set, before anyone occupies it.

The order is not optional, because an inspector cannot approve work that is already buried. Picture a 3,000 square foot Fountain Hills house where the framers finish and the drywallers hang board before the framing inspection is requested. With the wiring, plumbing rough-in, and fire blocking now sealed behind board, the inspector has nothing to verify, so the inspection fails. The crew opens the new drywall back up, re-inspects, then re-patches, adding a week and labor cost. The Town of Fountain Hills is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , and its Building Safety Division coordinates plan review and runs these inspections. Because the Town's adopted codes are the 2024 I-codes, the inspection points follow the 2024 IRC.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You schedule each inspection through TOPPS, the Town's Online Permitting and Planning System, at fountainhillsaz.gov/TOPPS. The Town requires it:

All applicants who have created an account and had a permit issued through the TOPPS (Town's Online Permitting and Planning System), or have an existing permit transferred to the new system, must schedule inspections using TOPPS.

Inspections are conducted Monday through Friday from 7 a.m. to 4 p.m. If you have questions about a request, Building Safety answers at 480-816-5123 or 480-816-5177. Request the inspection in TOPPS against the right permit, and have your permit number on hand.

The final inspection is the last lock between you and the keys. A passing final on a new home is what lets the Town issue the Certificate of Occupancy, the record that the house meets code and is cleared for living in. Until that final passes, no crew should conceal wiring, plumbing, or framing, because a failed inspection means cutting the cover back open and re-doing the patch.

When this comes into play

This process spans the entire construction phase:

  • Pour days. A footing or slab cannot be poured until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the inspection schedule sets the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical are all inspected before insulation and drywall. A failure here stops the close-in.
  • Move-in. The final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy. A closing or mortgage often needs the CO first.

What this means for you

Build your schedule around the inspection calendar, not the other way around. Each stage has to pass before the next trade covers its work, so a missed inspection drags everything after it. Set up your TOPPS account early, request inspections there, and plan around the Monday-to-Friday inspection window. Never let a crew bury wiring, plumbing, or framing before the inspector signs off. Hold off on moving in until the Certificate of Occupancy issues, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Fountain Hills module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements . To place inspections in the wider timeline, follow the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The inspection scheduling rule and TOPPS requirement are published by the Town of Fountain Hills: Building Safety on fountainhillsaz.gov . For permit applications and forms, see Permits and Applications .

Read this as a general guide, not a binding code interpretation. The Town can update its TOPPS workflow and CO requirements, so check the current steps with Fountain Hills Building Safety before you request an inspection.

Sources

  • Town of Fountain Hills Building Safety
  • Town of Fountain Hills Permits and Applications
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Fountain Hills Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: Permit, Sprinklers, and the $8,816 Impact Fee
shortSummary: Fountain Hills charges a new home three things: a building permit and plan review fee from the Town's fee schedule, a separate fire-sprinkler review fee, and a development impact fee. Under Resolution 2026-01, the impact fee is $8,816 per single-family home, effective April 6, 2026, with a 24-month freeze once you pull a permit.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Fountain Hills comes with three separate Town charges, and they hit at different times. First, a building permit and plan review fee, set by the Town's adopted fee schedule. Second, a fire-sprinkler fee, because Fountain Hills requires sprinklers in new homes and charges to review and inspect them. Third, a development impact fee, the large charge that pays for the roads, parks, and fire protection your home will use. This page gives you the real basis for each, with the impact-fee number the Town adopted for 2026.

The building permit and plan review fee

Fountain Hills sets the building permit and plan check fee on a per-unit basis, meaning by square foot and linear foot, under the rates in the Town's adopted fee schedule. The Town's own user fee study describes it that way: the Town "assesses building permit/plan check fees on a per unit basis (square foot, linear foot basis)." That is a different model from a flat valuation table. Your permit fee scales with the size of the house, and plan review is charged alongside it.

The exact per-square-foot rates live in the adopted fee schedule, which the Town Council resets each fiscal year (the Council adjusted the fee schedule again for FY2026). For example, a larger home and a small casita are billed the same per-square-foot rate, so the bigger the air-conditioned area, the larger the permit fee. The current Town fee schedule is published as a spreadsheet on the Town's website, and some Town document links are bot-blocked to automated tools, so the most reliable way to get today's exact rate is to open that page or call Development Services at 480-816-5100. Budget the permit and plan review as one combined building-department cost that grows with square footage.

The fire-sprinkler cost adder

Fountain Hills requires an automatic fire-sprinkler system in new one- and two-family homes, and the review and inspection of that system is billed on its own line. Based on the Town's fee study, the residential automatic sprinkler plan review and inspection fee runs about $340 as a base charge for up to 4,400 square feet, then roughly $0.08 per square foot above that. On a 5,500 square foot home, that is the $340 base plus about $88 for the additional 1,100 square feet, so roughly $428 for the sprinkler review and inspection. That is on top of the building permit fee, not included in it. The installed cost of the sprinkler system itself, paid to your fire-protection contractor, is a much larger separate number. Treat the Town fee and the install cost as two different lines.

The development impact fee: $8,816 per single-family home

Impact fees are charges a town collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth requires. Arizona authorizes them under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Fountain Hills adopted a new schedule by Resolution 2026-01, and the Town stated the single-family number directly:

The new development impact fees adopted by Resolution No. 2026-01 as shown below will become effective on April 6, 2026. Single Family Housing Unit $8,816.

So a single detached home pays $8,816 in combined development impact fees, effective April 6, 2026. The same resolution sets a multi-family unit at $5,462 and nonresidential categories by the 1,000 square feet. This impact fee is usually the largest single Town charge on the whole project, far larger than the permit or sprinkler fees.

There is a protection worth knowing. Under Town Code 7-2-10 and A.R.S. 9-463.05, Fountain Hills freezes the impact-fee schedule for 24 months once you submit a valid building permit application that then proceeds. For a single-family build, the fees in force when you pull the permit stay locked for 24 months after the first permit issues under your approved plan or plat.

When these fees come into play

  • At permit application. The per-square-foot building permit and plan review fee is invoiced when you apply, and the fire-sprinkler review fee is billed with the sprinkler plan.
  • Before the permit issues. Development impact fees are collected at or before permit issuance, so the $8,816 is part of the cash you bring to start construction, not a later bill.
  • The 24-month clock. If you have an approved plan, pulling the permit starts the freeze, which matters if the Town raises fees again mid-project.

What this means for you

Budget three separate Town lines: the per-square-foot permit and plan review fee, the fire-sprinkler review and inspection fee (around $340 plus $0.08 per square foot over 4,400), and the $8,816 single-family impact fee. The impact fee is the big one, and it is due up front, so plan for it in your start-of-construction cash. If you have plan approval, pulling the permit locks your fees for 24 months. Because the per-square-foot permit rates reset every fiscal year and some Town document links are bot-blocked, confirm today's exact permit and sprinkler rates with Fountain Hills Development Services before you finalize the budget.

This entry sits in the Fountain Hills building-codes module alongside Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, Fountain Hills Fire Sprinkler Requirements , which covers why the sprinkler fee exists, and Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes . For how these costs compare across the metro, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The $8,816 single-family impact fee, the April 6, 2026 effective date, and the 24-month freeze are reproduced from the Town of Fountain Hills' official developer notice on Resolution 2026-01, a public record: New Development Impact Fees letter . The per-unit permit basis comes from the Town's adopted fee schedule, posted on the Financial Reports and Budgets and Permits and Applications pages. The statute behind impact fees is A.R.S. 9-463.05 .

These fees change by Town resolution. The per-square-foot permit and sprinkler rates reset each fiscal year, so confirm the current schedule with Fountain Hills Development Services before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • Town of Fountain Hills New Development Impact Fees letter (Resolution 2026-01)
  • Town of Fountain Hills Permits and Applications
  • Town of Fountain Hills fee schedule (Financial Reports and Budgets)
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Fountain Hills Fire Sprinkler Requirements: Why New Homes Need Sprinklers and What It Costs
shortSummary: Fountain Hills requires an automatic fire-sprinkler system in essentially every new home, reaching all levels plus garages and carports. The Town adopted this rule in 2008, before Arizona's 2009 ban on new home-sprinkler mandates, so it is grandfathered and still enforced. Most neighboring cities do not require sprinklers, so this is a real cost driver.
bodyHtml:
A new home in the Town of Fountain Hills almost always needs an automatic fire-sprinkler system, and the rule reaches every level of the house plus attached garages and carports. This is the one local requirement that most surprises people building in Fountain Hills, because nearly every neighboring city dropped residential sprinkler mandates years ago. Fountain Hills kept its, and it can, because the rule predates a state law that froze new mandates. This page explains the ordinance, why it is still legal, what it covers, and the real cost it adds.

What the Town requires

Fountain Hills amends the fire code so that a sprinkler system covers practically any new building. The Town Code, in its added Section 903.2, states the rule:

An automatic sprinkler system shall be installed throughout all levels of all new occupancies of more than 0 square feet, to include all garages, and car-ports.

The phrase "more than 0 square feet" is not a typo. It means the threshold is effectively zero, so a normal house, its garage, and its carport are all in. The Town does carve out a short list of small detached structures that do not need sprinklers, including detached gazebos and ramadas, detached guard houses under 300 square feet, and detached storage sheds under 200 square feet used for private, non-commercial purposes. A house itself does not qualify for any of those exceptions.

The Town of Fountain Hills is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts and enforces this, so its Building Safety Division and fire code official, not the county or the state, apply the rule inside town limits.

Why the rule is still legal

Arizona banned new mandatory home-sprinkler rules in 2009, but Fountain Hills is grandfathered. State law A.R.S. 9-807 blocks a city from forcing sprinklers on a single-family or two-unit home, then carves out an exception for older ordinances:

This section does not apply to any code or ordinance that requires fire sprinklers in a residence and that was adopted before December 31, 2009.

Fountain Hills adopted its sprinkler requirement on April 17, 2008, before that cutoff. The Town wrote the protection into its 2024 code update. It states that adopting the newer fire code "shall not affect the validity of the Town's existing fire sprinkler requirements." Any residential sprinkler reference falls back to the 2006 International Fire Code as amended. So the rule that controls your house is older than the rest of the code stack, and it stands because of the date it was adopted.

When this comes into play

This rule shapes a Fountain Hills build from the first sketch:

  • Design. Your plan set has to show a sprinkler layout, and the system has to be engineered and permitted. It is not a finish item you add at the end.
  • Water service. Sprinklers can drive a larger water line or meter to the house, which affects your tap and your site plumbing, so it is settled early with the water provider.
  • Budget. Residential fire sprinklers in Arizona generally run about 2 to 5 dollars per square foot of covered area, including the garage. For example, on a 3,500 square foot home with an 800 square foot garage, that is roughly 8,600 to 21,500 dollars, a line most neighboring cities do not impose. This is the kind of item that belongs on your cost-driver list from day one, the same way what drives the cost of a custom home in Arizona lays it out.

This is separate from wildfire defensible space , the cleared buffer around a home in fire-prone terrain. Sprinklers protect the inside of the house; defensible space protects the outside. A Fountain Hills hillside lot can involve both.

What this means for you

Plan and budget for sprinklers from the first design meeting, not as a late add-on. Get the system on your plan set, confirm the water line size with the provider early, and price it into your cost per square foot, because it can move your number by several thousand dollars. The sprinkler system also gets inspected and has to pass before the Town issues your certificate of occupancy , so a missed or failed sprinkler test can hold up move-in. If you are comparing a Fountain Hills lot against one in a nearby city that does not require sprinklers, fold this cost into the comparison so the two are measured fairly.

This entry is a city-special topic in the Fountain Hills module. The full adopted-code list, including how the sprinkler rule sits on an older edition than the rest, is in Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes . What you file for the permit is in Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the applied question with cost detail, see do I need fire sprinklers in a new home in Fountain Hills .

Source

The sprinkler requirement and its exceptions are codified by the Town of Fountain Hills in Town Code Section 7-1-2 , with the adopted-code overview at Adopted Codes on fountainhillsaz.gov . The state law that lets the Town keep the rule is A.R.S. 9-807 on azleg.gov .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Code editions, amendments, and costs change, so confirm the current sprinkler requirement and pricing with the Fountain Hills Building Safety Division and your fire-sprinkler contractor before you rely on a number.

Sources

  • Fountain Hills Town Code Section 7-1-2 (Fire Code Amendments)
  • A.R.S. 9-807 (Mandated fire sprinklers in certain residences prohibited; exception)
  • Town of Fountain Hills Adopted Codes (Building Safety)
