title: Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and the Steps
shortSummary: A new home in the Town of Fountain Hills needs a Town building permit before construction starts. You apply through the Town's online TOPPS system with engineered site plans, two sets of construction drawings, and your parcel information. The Building Safety Division reviews the plans for code and zoning compliance, and the permit issues after fees are paid.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new home in the Town of Fountain Hills until the Town issues a building permit. The permit is the Town's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes, your zoning, and your site conditions. The Building Safety Division, a work unit inside the Town's Planning and Zoning Department, handles this for any lot inside town limits. This page walks the real steps: what you gather, how you submit it, how the Town reviews it, and what it takes to get the permit in hand.

What a residential permit requires

Fountain Hills runs new-home permits through TOPPS, the Town's Online Permitting and Planning System, not over the counter. A complete residential package for a one- or two-family home comes together from these pieces:

  • Two engineered site plans, drawn to scale, showing the house on the lot with setbacks (the required distance from each property line), grading, and drainage called out.
  • Two sets of construction drawings, at a maximum sheet size of 24 by 36 inches and preferably at a 1/4 inch equals 1 foot scale, covering framing, electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and energy, drawn to the Town's adopted 2024 I-codes.
  • A fire-sprinkler plan, because the Town's fire amendments require sprinklers in new homes, so this is not optional here.
  • Your parcel number, owner and property information, and contact details.
  • Sanitary District sign-off where you connect to public sewer, since the Fountain Hills Sanitary District reviews and approves the sewer connection separately from the Town.

The Town describes the division's job directly:

Through this system you can file an application, receive updates on the status of the review of the application, receive notification of application approval, and schedule inspections.

A package that is incomplete or a site plan that is not to scale gets handed back before review, which costs you days. Getting the engineered site plan and the construction set to the right scale and the right code edition the first time is the cheapest way to keep the review moving.

How review, fees, and issuance work

After you submit through TOPPS, the Building Safety Division coordinates the review of your plans for compliance with the adopted Town codes and your zoning. If corrections are required, you fix the sheets and resubmit through the same system. When the plans clear and the permit fees are paid, the permit issues, and you schedule inspections through TOPPS. Inspections run Monday through Friday, 7 a.m. to 4 p.m.

The fees are real numbers, not a mystery. For a one- and two-family home, the Town's published schedule runs roughly 73 cents per square foot for air-conditioned area and 55 cents per square foot for non-air-conditioned area, plus a landscape permit around $420 and a single-family dwelling development fee around $1,601. So a 3,000 square foot conditioned home carries a building-permit fee in the low thousands before the development and landscape fees, which is a line item you budget rather than a surprise.

Here is a real example. Suppose you submit a 2,800 square foot custom home in Fountain Hills with a site plan that shows the house too close to the front property line for your zoning district. Staff flags the setback in review. You shift the footprint or seek relief, then resubmit through TOPPS. That single correction round can add a week or two before the permit issues, which is why the site plan setbacks are worth getting right the first time.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Fountain Hills build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the engineered site plan, the construction set, and the sprinkler plan, you submit through TOPPS, and the review clock runs through any correction rounds before the permit issues.
  • Sewer or septic first. Many Fountain Hills lots connect to the Fountain Hills Sanitary District for sewer, which reviews your connection on its own track. A lot without sewer service may use septic, which runs through Maricopa County and the state, and the two have to line up on the same site plan before you build.
  • Zoning fit. The permit confirms your home meets the Town zoning for your district, including setbacks, grading, and hillside rules. A design that overbuilds the lot or crowds a property line gets caught here, before construction.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item, using the Town's per-square-foot rates plus the development and landscape fees, and prepare clean digital files for TOPPS. Get the engineered site plan to scale and the setbacks right the first time, because a setback problem is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Draw the set to Fountain Hills' current 2024 I-codes and include the required fire-sprinkler plan, so review does not stall on a wrong-code or missing-sheet correction. Line up the Fountain Hills Sanitary District sewer review in parallel, since the Town building permit alone does not approve your sewer connection. When the home passes its final inspections, the Town issues the certificate of occupancy that lets you legally move in.

This entry sits next to the codes in the Fountain Hills module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes . If your lot is on septic or a private well, the water and wastewater side follows the county and state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the full picture of a custom build, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The division's role, the TOPPS online system, and the inspection hours are published by the Town of Fountain Hills: Building Safety on fountainhillsaz.gov . The application forms, checklists, and fees are at Permits & Applications and Building Permit Forms & Checklists . The separate sewer review is handled by the Fountain Hills Sanitary District .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the Town's current applications and checklists. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with the Town of Fountain Hills Building Safety Division before you file.

Sources

  • Town of Fountain Hills Building Safety
  • Town of Fountain Hills Permits & Applications
  • Town of Fountain Hills Building Permit Forms & Checklists
  • Fountain Hills Sanitary District Building Permit Review

title: Fountain Hills Residential Zoning and Setbacks: District Yards for a New Home
shortSummary: Fountain Hills single-family zoning runs from R1-6 on small lots to R1-190 on multi-acre lots. R1-43 needs a 40 foot front yard, 30 foot sides, a 40 foot rear yard, and 20% lot coverage. Smaller R1-18 lots need 20, 10, and 30 feet, all under a 30 foot height cap.
bodyHtml:
In Fountain Hills, your zoning district sets how far the house must sit from each property line, a distance called the setback. The town's single-family districts are named for their minimum lot size, from R1-6 (6,000 square feet) up to R1-190 (190,000 square feet). As the lots get bigger, the required yards get deeper and the lot-coverage cap gets tighter, so the district decides how much house the lot can hold and where it goes.

What the districts require

Section 10.09 of the Fountain Hills Zoning Ordinance sets the lot size, yards, height, and coverage for each single-family district. The districts share a 30 foot building-height limit but differ in their yards and coverage:

  • R1-190: 190,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 60 ft, side yard 30 ft, lot coverage 5%.
  • R1-43: 43,560 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 40 ft, side yard 30 ft, lot coverage 20%.
  • R1-35: 35,000 sq ft lot, front yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, rear yard 40 ft, lot coverage 20%.
  • R1-18: 18,000 sq ft lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 10 ft, rear yard 30 ft, lot coverage 25%.
  • R1-10: 10,000 sq ft lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 7 ft, rear yard 20 ft, lot coverage 30%.
  • R1-6: 6,000 sq ft lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 5 ft, rear yard 20 ft, lot coverage 40%.

The table title states what it governs:

Density, Area, Building and Yard Regulations.

That single chart in Section 10.09 carries the minimum lot, lot width, height, front, side, and rear yards, and the coverage cap for every single-family district, so it is the one page that decides the buildable footprint on a Fountain Hills lot.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks and coverage shape the build before the floor plan is final:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer subtracts the yards from the lot to find the buildable area. For example, on a 145 foot wide R1-43 lot, the 30 foot side yards alone take 60 feet, leaving an 85 foot building width before the 40 foot front and rear yards trim the depth.
  • Lot coverage. Fountain Hills pairs the setbacks with a lot coverage cap that tightens as lots shrink, from 5% on R1-190 to 40% on R1-6. The setbacks and the coverage cap are separate tests, and the house has to pass both.
  • Hillside and corner lots. Footnotes adjust some yards for hillside, canyon-side, and corner conditions, which is common on the town's sloped desert terrain.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a Fountain Hills plan gets corrected, so the town checks each yard before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact district before you design, because the side yard alone swings from 5 feet in R1-6 to 30 feet in R1-43, which reshapes the whole footprint. Plan the house against the lot-coverage cap early, since 20% on an R1-43 acre limits the combined building footprint even when the lot looks large. The 30 foot height cap applies across the single-family districts, so a taller roofline is not an option without relief. We map the yards and coverage cap together on day one of a Fountain Hills project, because they decide whether a plan fits the lot.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Fountain Hills module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Fountain Hills Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The district lot sizes, setbacks, height limit, and lot-coverage caps are published by the Town of Fountain Hills in Section 10.09 of the Zoning Ordinance: View the Density, Area, Building and Yard Regulations on the Fountain Hills Zoning Ordinance . The town's planning hub is at Fountain Hills Planning & Zoning .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks change with hillside overlays, corner-lot rules, and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Fountain Hills Development Services before you design.

Sources

  • Town of Fountain Hills Zoning Ordinance, Section 10.09 (Density, Area, Building and Yard Regulations)
  • Town of Fountain Hills Planning & Zoning
  • Town of Fountain Hills Zoning Ordinance (full ordinance)

title: Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes (Unincorporated Areas): The 2018 I-Codes That Apply
shortSummary: Unincorporated Maricopa County builds to the 2018 International Residential, Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, and Fuel Gas codes, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, with the 2018 energy and green codes voluntary. The Board of Supervisors adopted them on August 17, 2022, through the county's Local Additions and Addenda, effective 30 days later with a grace period to December 30, 2022.
bodyHtml:
If your lot sits outside any city, in the unincorporated county near Rio Verde Foothills, the Cave Creek fringe, or open desert, then Maricopa County, not a city, is the authority that reviews your plans and inspects your build. The county runs an older code edition than the big cities next door, so the exact rules can differ. This page lists the codes unincorporated Maricopa County actually enforces, when the Board of Supervisors adopted them, and which pieces are mandatory versus voluntary.

Which codes the county enforces

Unincorporated Maricopa County adopted the 2018 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2017 electrical code. The county bundles its adoptions and local edits into one document, the Maricopa County Local Additions and Addenda (MCLAA). Here is the current list:

  • 2018 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2018 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2018 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2018 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2018 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2018 International Existing Building Code (IEBC): work on existing structures.
  • 2017 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.
  • 2018 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): adopted as voluntary, not mandatory.
  • 2018 International Green Construction Code (IgCC): adopted as voluntary, not mandatory.

The IRC is the one a single-family builder lives in day to day. The Maricopa County Planning and Development Department is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes in the unincorporated area, so the county's building officials, not a city and not the state, have the final word on a rural build.

What the county says

The county records the adoption plainly in its Adopted Regulations index:

Text Amendment to the Maricopa County Local Additions & Addenda to adopt and amend the following construction safety codes: 2018 International Building Code, 2018 International Residential Code, 2018 International Plumbing Code, 2018 International Mechanical Code, 2018 International Fuel Gas Code, 2018 International Green Code (voluntary), 2018 International Energy Conservation Code (voluntary), 2018 International Existing Building Code, and 2017 National Electric Code.

The Board of Supervisors approved that amendment on August 17, 2022. It took effect 30 days later. A grace period let projects use the prior codes through December 30, 2022. The codes also carry AZBO and MAG regional amendments, the standardized edits used across the Phoenix area, on top of each model code. So the rule you build to is the published 2018 edition (2017 for electrical) as changed by the county's MCLAA.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your framing, bracing, and foundation details follow the 2018 IRC as amended for the county. A plan copied from a newer 2021 or 2024 city project may carry details the county code does not require, or miss county-specific edits.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2017 NEC. This is two cycles behind Phoenix's 2023 NEC, so an electrician must use the right code book for the jurisdiction.
  • Energy compliance is voluntary at the county level, not mandatory. The 2018 IECC is adopted as a voluntary standard, so the county does not force the same energy paperwork a city like Scottsdale requires. Many builders still design to it for comfort and resale, but it is your choice, not a county mandate.

Here is a concrete example. Say you submit a custom home plan for a five-acre parcel near Rio Verde Foothills in 2026. The county plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2018 IRC. Your wiring is checked against the 2017 NEC. Your gas piping is checked against the 2018 IFGC. If your designer reused a set drawn to Scottsdale's 2021 codes, the editions will not line up. The plan can come back with corrections before a permit issues.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction. Build to Maricopa County's 2018 I-codes and the 2017 NEC, with the county's Local Additions and Addenda applied. Treat the energy code as voluntary unless you choose to follow it. That instruction prevents the most common rural-build mistake, which is using a nearby city's newer code on a county lot. The cities around you (Scottsdale on 2021, Phoenix on 2024) are ahead of the county. A set drawn for a city build is not automatically right for the county.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Maricopa County building-codes module. From here, see Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review, and Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home for the water and wastewater side a rural lot needs. For the bigger picture, see what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde and how to buy land to build a house in Arizona .

Source

title: Maricopa County Building Inspections for New Homes: The IRC R109 Sequence and How to Schedule
shortSummary: Unincorporated Maricopa County inspects a new home at fixed points set by IRC Section R109, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered. You schedule through your Permit Center account or the call center, and you reach the assigned inspector only on the day of the inspection.
bodyHtml:
A new home on unincorporated Maricopa County land gets checked at set points while it is built, and each point has to pass before a crew seals its work behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The county runs this on the older 2018 code it adopted, and the required inspection points come straight from Section R109 of that code. This page lays out the order, how you schedule each one through the county Permit Center, and the one scheduling rule that trips up rural builders.

The inspection sequence

The inspections follow the build from the ground up, and the county sets them where the work is still visible. The list comes from the 2018 International Residential Code (IRC) the county adopted, which fixes the required inspection points in Section R109. The typical order for a new house:

  1. Foundation. After the footing trench is dug and steel is set, before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical lines under the floor are in, before the slab covers them.
  3. Framing. When walls, fire blocking, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are all in place, before anything is covered.
  4. Plumbing, mechanical, and electrical rough-in. Verified at or alongside the framing stage while the lines are still open.
  5. Insulation and lath or gypsum board. Before drywall, plaster, or stucco goes on.
  6. Final. When the house is finished and all fixtures are set, before anyone moves in.

The order is not optional, because an inspector cannot approve what is already hidden. The 2018 IRC states the rule plainly:

Work shall not be done beyond the point indicated in each successive inspection without first obtaining the approval of the building official.

The Maricopa County Planning and Development Department is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) for land outside any city, so its inspectors, not a city and not the state, set and enforce this sequence on a rural lot.

How scheduling and the assigned inspector work

You schedule inspections yourself, and you cannot start until your permit is live. The county requires an issued or reissued permit before you can request any inspection. You then schedule two ways: sign in to your Permit Center account and request the inspection there, or call the county Call Center at 602-506-3301. The same Permit Center account you used to pull the permit handles the inspections.

One county practice catches first-time rural builders. You do not get your inspector's direct line in advance. The county tells you to reach the assigned inspector only on inspection day:

For information about your specific inspection, contact assigned inspector listed online at the Permit Center ONLY on the date of your scheduled inspection.

So you book the date through the portal, and the system assigns the inspector and posts their contact for that day. There is no calling ahead the week before to negotiate a window.

When this comes into play

This process runs across the entire construction phase of a rural build:

  • Pour days. You cannot pour a footing or slab until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the inspection calendar drives the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing plus the plumbing, electrical, and mechanical rough-in all get inspected before insulation and drywall. A failed framing inspection stops the whole cover-up, and on a remote parcel a re-inspection trip can cost you more calendar time than it would in town.
  • Move-in. The final inspection is the gate to the certificate of occupancy , the document that legally lets you occupy the home. The final inspection is the last sign-off before that paper issues.

Here is a concrete example. Suppose you build a 2,600 square foot custom home on a five-acre parcel near Rio Verde Foothills. Your framer finishes and the drywall crew hangs board before the framing inspection is called. The county inspector cannot see the wiring, plumbing rough-in, or fire blocking, so the framing fails. The crew cuts out new drywall to expose the framing, re-inspects, then re-patches, which can add a week and real labor cost on a site that is a long drive from the supply house.

What this means for you

Treat the inspection calendar as the spine of your build schedule, not an afterthought. Each phase has to pass before the next trade can cover its work, so a missed or failed inspection delays everything downstream, and the delay bites harder on a remote lot. Keep your Permit Center login handy, confirm the permit is issued before you book, and request the inspection type that matches the stage. Do not chase your inspector the day before, because the county only posts that contact on the scheduled day. Do not move furniture or family in before the final passes and the certificate of occupancy issues, since occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the unincorporated Maricopa County module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are listed in Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements . A rural lot's water and wastewater approvals are separate, in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . For the bigger picture, see what inspections are required when building a house in Arizona and what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde .

Source

The inspection scheduling, the issued-permit requirement, and the assigned-inspector rule are published by Maricopa County: Construction Permit Information on maricopa.gov , with the department overview at Planning and Development . The inspection points themselves come from the 2018 IRC the county adopted, listed at Adopted Regulations .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Inspection steps and scheduling change, so confirm the current process with Maricopa County Planning and Development before you schedule.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Construction Permit Information (Inspections)
  • Maricopa County Planning and Development Department
  • Maricopa County Adopted Regulations (2018 I-codes)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Maricopa County Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: The Valuation Table
shortSummary: Unincorporated Maricopa County charges new-home permit fees by construction valuation, using Table 1-A, not by square foot. A $500,000 home pays a $3,233.75 permit fee plus a 65 percent plan review fee, about $2,102. The county levies no city-style water, sewer, or parks impact fees, since most lots use private septic and wells.
bodyHtml:
Building a new home on unincorporated land in Maricopa County works differently from building inside a city. The county charges its building permit fee by construction valuation, the estimated dollar value of the work, run through a published table. There is no city-style stack of water, sewer, parks, and fire impact fees, because most county lots are served by private septic systems and wells, not municipal utilities. This page gives you the real Table 1-A numbers so you can budget the permit accurately.

How the valuation-based permit fee works

The county sets a single home's permit fee from Table 1-A in its Planning and Development fee schedule. You do not pick the valuation yourself out of thin air. The county determines it from the current Building Valuation Data published in "Building Standards," adjusted for Arizona. Here is the part of Table 1-A that covers a typical custom home:

$100,001 to $500,000: $993.75 for the first $100,000 plus $5.60 for each $1000, or fraction thereof, to and including $500,000. $500,001 to $1,000,000: $3,233.75 for the first $500,000 plus $4.75 for each additional $1000.

So the permit fee climbs in steps as the home's value rises. A home the county values at $500,000 lands at the top of the first bracket above, for a permit fee of $3,233.75. On top of that, the county adds a plan review fee equal to 65 percent of the calculated permit fee, which on that same home is about $2,102. Together the permit and plan review run roughly $5,336 for a $500,000 home, before drainage review, inspections, and addressing.

Those extras are real line items. Residential drainage review is a $60 admin fee plus a $300 engineered-review upcharge when required. Standard residential inspections run $150 each, and a failed inspection that has to be repeated is another $150. The schedule notes all fees may be rounded up to the next full dollar.

Why there are no city impact fees here

Arizona lets a city or county charge impact fees under A.R.S. 9-463.05 to pay for the public infrastructure new growth needs. Cities like Phoenix and Scottsdale use them for municipal water, sewer, parks, fire, and streets. Unincorporated Maricopa County generally does not, because a typical county home is not on city water or city sewer. It runs on a private well or a hauled-water arrangement and an on-site septic system, which you permit and pay for separately through the county environmental and the well or septic process.

That changes the budget math. Inside a city, impact fees can be the single largest fee on a new home. On a county lot, your big separate costs are the septic system permit and install and the well drilling, not a municipal impact fee. Confirm the current fee schedule with the county before you budget, since the Board of Supervisors updates these amounts and the valuation data changes.

When these fees come into play

  • Build-on-your-lot in the county. A custom home in an unincorporated area near Rio Verde, Cave Creek, or the desert edge runs through Table 1-A on valuation. Your designer files through the county Permit Center.
  • Septic and well. Because there is no city utility impact fee, plan and budget the septic permit and the well as their own costs. They often exceed what a small city permit would be.
  • Drainage. Many county lots sit in or near washes, so the drainage review fee and a possible engineered-review upcharge apply more often than they would on a flat city lot.

What this means for you

Budget the permit from the valuation, not the square footage, because the county runs off Table 1-A. A rough rule: a half-million-dollar home is in the low thousands for the permit plus 65 percent plan review, then add drainage and inspections. Do not budget for city impact fees on a true unincorporated lot, but do budget for the septic and well, which carry their own county permits and real install costs. Use the county's online fee estimator to price your specific valuation before you file.

This entry sits in the building-codes module beside Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards. For a metro-wide comparison of permit costs, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The valuation brackets and the 65 percent plan review rate above are reproduced from the Maricopa County Planning and Development Summary of the Fee Schedule, a public record: Summary of the Fee Schedule on maricopa.gov . For an interactive estimate of your project, use the county's Fee Schedule and Estimators page.

These fees are set by the Board of Supervisors and the valuation data updates over time. Confirm the current schedule with Maricopa County Planning and Development before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Planning and Development Summary of the Fee Schedule (Table 1-A, June 2024)
  • Maricopa County Fee Schedule and Estimators
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and the Steps
shortSummary: A new home on unincorporated Maricopa County land needs a county building permit before any construction starts. You apply online through the county Permit Center with a site plan, the required documents, and your parcel and owner information. The decision engine routes you to the right application, staff reviews for code compliance, and the permit issues after fees are paid.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new home in unincorporated Maricopa County until the county issues a building permit. The permit is the county's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes, your zoning, and your site conditions. The Maricopa County Planning and Development Department handles this for land outside any city. This page walks the real steps: what you gather, how you apply online, how the county reviews it, and what it takes to get the permit in hand.

What a residential permit requires

Maricopa County runs new-home permits through its online Permit Center. The county describes residential construction broadly and ties the value and code compliance of your home to pulling the permit first:

Residential Construction is defined as any construction that is not considered commercial in nature. The value, safety, and code compliance of your structure is heavily dependent on obtaining a building permit prior to construction or installation.

Before you apply, the county tells you to get prepared. A complete residential submittal comes together from these pieces:

  • A Permit Center account (a personal account or a company account), which you must create before you can start an application.
  • The right permit application for your project type, which the Permit Center decision engine helps you identify.
  • Your site plan, drawn to scale and legible, showing the house on the lot with setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out.
  • Your other required documents, which are listed on the permit applications themselves.
  • Your parcel number, owner and property information, and contact details.
  • Your project's lot coverage calculation and any known upfront fees.

A package that is incomplete or a site plan that is not to scale gets handed back before review, which costs you days.

How review and issuance work

After you sign in to the Permit Center, the county's decision engine walks you through the application. The county states the online flow directly:

The Permit Center decision engine tool will walk you through a number of questions and prompts. You will complete the online application, submit required PDF documents and pay any upfront fees, if applicable.

From there, staff reviews your plans for compliance with the adopted county codes and your zoning. If corrections are needed, you fix the sheets and resubmit through the portal. When the plans clear and fees are paid, the permit issues and you can schedule inspections through the same Permit Center.

Here is a concrete example. Suppose you submit a 2,800 square foot custom home on a county parcel near Cave Creek, with a site plan that shows the house too close to the front property line for your zoning district. Staff flags the setback in review and asks you to correct the plan. You shift the footprint or seek relief, then resubmit. That single correction round can add a week or two before the permit issues, which is why the site plan setbacks are worth getting right the first time.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every rural county build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the site plan and engineered documents, you apply through the Permit Center, and the review clock runs through any correction rounds before the permit issues.
  • Septic and well first. A rural lot usually has no city sewer or water, so your perc test and septic approval and your well work run alongside the building permit. The county's onsite wastewater (septic) permit is a separate application from the building permit, and the two have to line up on the same site plan.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, you rely on Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121, which only covers a home you intend to occupy yourself, not one built for sale or rent within a year. That choice decides who signs the application and carries responsibility.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item, and start the Permit Center account early, because you cannot apply without one. Get the site plan to scale and the setbacks right the first time, because a setback problem is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Build review and correction time into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant. On a rural lot, line up your septic and well work in parallel, since the building permit alone does not give you water or wastewater. Confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder, since that decides who signs the forms.

This entry sits between the codes and the rural utilities in the Maricopa County module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes . The water and wastewater side is covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home . For the full picture of a rural build, see what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde and how to buy land to build a house in Arizona .

Source

The permit steps, the Permit Center account requirement, and the online application flow are published by Maricopa County: Residential Construction on maricopa.gov . The separate onsite wastewater applications a rural lot needs are at Onsite Wastewater Forms and Applications .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the county's current applications and checklists. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with Maricopa County Planning and Development before you file.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Residential Construction Permit Information
  • Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater Forms and Applications
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Maricopa County Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Rural Lots and Where the House Sits
shortSummary: On unincorporated Maricopa County land, the Rural-43 zone (43,560 square foot lots, serving areas like Rio Verde) requires a 40 foot front and rear yard, 20 foot sides, a 35 foot height limit, and 25% lot coverage. Bigger Rural-70 and Rural-190 zones push the front and rear yards to 60 feet.
bodyHtml:
If your land sits in unincorporated Maricopa County, outside any city, the county is your zoning authority, and its rules decide how far the house must stand from each property line. That open distance is the setback. Most of this land carries Rural or single-family Residential zoning, named for the minimum lot size. Rural-43 (43,560 square feet, about one acre) is common in places like Rio Verde, while Rural-190 covers the largest lots. The bigger the district, the deeper the yards.

What the districts require

The county's Chapter 5 development-standards table sets the lot size, yards, height, and lot coverage for each district. The Rural and single-family zones share a 35 foot height limit:

  • Rural-190: 190,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 60 ft, side yard 30 ft, lot coverage 10%.
  • Rural-70: 70,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 60 ft, side yard 30 ft, lot coverage 15%.
  • Rural-43: 43,560 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, lot coverage 25%.
  • R1-35: 35,000 sq ft lot, front and rear yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, lot coverage 30%.
  • R1-18: 18,000 sq ft lot, front yard 10 ft (no less than 18 ft to a front-facing garage), side yard 10 ft, rear yard 30 ft, lot coverage 35%.

The county footnotes the front yard on smaller lots:

10' minimum but no less than 18' to the plane of the front-facing garage.

So on an R1-18 lot the house can come within 10 feet of the front line, but a garage that faces the street has to sit back 18 feet, the same idea Casa Grande and the cities use to keep a parked car off the sidewalk.

When setbacks come into play

In rural Maricopa County, setbacks and lot coverage shape the build before design starts:

  • Build-on-rural-land. For example, on a Rural-43 acre, the 40 foot front and rear plus 20 foot sides carve a wide border out of the parcel, and the 25% lot coverage cap limits the combined footprint of house, garage, and outbuildings. Both tests have to pass.
  • Septic and wells. County site plans also show setbacks from the septic tank and disposal field to the house, the well, and washes, on top of the property-line setbacks. This is standard on rural lots without city sewer.
  • Accessory buildings. Detached structures have their own, usually smaller, side and rear setbacks, so a shop or barn can sit closer to a side line than the main house.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a rural plan gets corrected, so the county checks each yard before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact zoning district before you buy or design, because Rural-43 and Rural-70 sit on similar large lots but the front and rear yards differ by 20 feet. Plan the footprint against the lot-coverage cap early, since 25% on a Rural-43 acre still ties the buildable area to the parcel. Lay out septic, well, and structure setbacks together, because on rural land those distances interact and can move the house. We map the property-line yards, coverage cap, and septic setbacks on day one of a rural build, because together they decide where the house can go.

This entry pairs with the rest of the unincorporated-county module. For a Rio Verde build, start with what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde, Arizona . The codes your plans are checked against are in Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes , the filing steps are in Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements , and the on-site water and waste rules are in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The district lot sizes, setbacks, height limit, and lot-coverage caps are published by Maricopa County in Chapter 5 of the Zoning Ordinance: View the Maricopa County Zoning Ordinance (PDF) on maricopa.gov . The county's zoning hub is at Maricopa County Zoning Ordinance .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks change with overlays, hillside rules, and ordinance amendments, so confirm your parcel's district and standards with Maricopa County Planning and Development before you design.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 5 (Zoning Districts and Development Standards)
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Zoning Ordinance
  • Maricopa County Planning & Development, Residential Construction
  • Arizona Revised Statutes Title 11 (County government and planning authority)

title: Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home
shortSummary: A rural Maricopa County home with no city sewer or water needs two separate approvals: a septic permit from Maricopa County Environmental Services, and a private well authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources. The septic side starts with a soils evaluation and perc test. The well side starts with a Notice of Intent to Drill filed with ADWR.
bodyHtml:
A new home on rural Maricopa County land usually has no city sewer line and no city water main, so you provide both yourself: a septic system for wastewater and a private well for water. These are two separate approvals from two different agencies, and neither comes with the building permit. The county handles septic. The state handles wells. This page explains what each one requires, in what order, and how they tie back to your building permit.

The septic side: a county onsite wastewater permit

Wastewater on a rural lot goes to an onsite system, a septic system, permitted by Maricopa County Environmental Services. The county runs septic approval in phases, starting with proving the soil can absorb the wastewater, then designing a system that fits. The county lists the applications you submit through its Permit Center:

Phase I - Site and Soils Evaluation Application. Phase II - Conventional System Design Application. Phase II - Alternative Systems Design application. Septic Permit Application.

In plain terms, the path runs like this. Phase I is the site and soils evaluation: the county or a registered professional checks the lot for limiting conditions and measures how fast the soil absorbs water. That absorption test is the perc test , short for percolation test, where water is timed draining from test holes in the proposed disposal area. The result sets a soil absorption rate. Phase II is the system design, sized to that rate and to your home's number of bedrooms. After the system is installed and inspected, you file a Request for Discharge Authorization, and the county signs off so the system can be used.

Here is a concrete example. Say your five-acre parcel near Rio Verde Foothills has tight, clay-heavy soil that drains slowly. The perc test returns a slow rate, so the conventional gravity leach field would have to be very large, or the lot cannot fit one. The county may then require an alternative system, which costs more. This is why the soils evaluation comes first: it can change your system type, your budget, and even where the house sits on the lot.

The well side: an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill

Water on a rural lot usually comes from a private well, and the well is authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources (ADWR), not the county. Before any drilling, you file paperwork with the state. ADWR states the rule directly:

Notice of Intent to Drill must be filed with ADWR. Forms are available on this site and must be submitted to ADWR accompanied by the appropriate filing fee. Authority to deepen an existing well or drill a new well will be valid for one year. After that, a new Notice of Intent must be filed. A licensed well-drilling contractor must perform the work.

A typical home well is an exempt well, which ADWR defines as a well with a pump capacity of no more than 35 gallons per minute used for domestic purposes. Filing the Notice of Intent registers the planned well and gives you a registration number, after which the licensed driller has one year to complete the work. One catch matters for small lots: if the well serves a parcel of 5 acres or fewer, the local health authority must review the application before it goes to ADWR. Much of Maricopa County also sits inside the Phoenix Active Management Area, which adds groundwater rules on top of the basic exempt-well process.

When this comes into play

These two approvals govern any rural build with no municipal utilities:

  • Buying the land. Before you close on a lot, the perc result and the odds of a good well are part of whether the land is even buildable. A failed perc or a dry-hole risk can sink a deal, so this work belongs in your due diligence, not after.
  • Designing the home. Your septic disposal area, your well, and your house all share the lot and have to keep required separation distances ( setbacks ) from each other and from property lines. The septic field and the well cannot sit on top of each other.
  • Sequencing the build. The county septic permit and the ADWR well authorization run in parallel with your county building permit, and all three appear on the same site plan.

What this means for you

Treat septic and well as their own projects with their own timelines and budgets, separate from the house. Start the perc test early, ideally during your due diligence before you buy, because a slow or failing result changes your system type and cost and can decide whether the lot works at all. File the Notice of Intent to Drill with ADWR and hire a licensed driller, and remember the registration is good for one year. If your parcel is 5 acres or smaller, expect the local health review step before ADWR. Put the septic field, the well, and the house on one site plan so the separation distances all work together.

This entry covers the rural-utility half of the Maricopa County module. The permit that ties them together is in Maricopa County Residential Building Permit Requirements , and the codes your home is built to are in Maricopa County Adopted Building Codes . For the full rural picture, see what you need to build on rural land in Rio Verde and how to buy land to build a house in Arizona .

Source

The septic applications and phased process are published by Maricopa County Environmental Services: Onsite Wastewater Forms and Applications on maricopa.gov . The well requirements are published by the state: Well Drilling in Arizona and the ADWR Permitting and Wells FAQ .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the agencies' current rules. Septic phases, well filings, and fees change, so confirm the live requirements with Maricopa County Environmental Services and the Arizona Department of Water Resources before you rely on them.

Sources

  • Maricopa County Onsite Wastewater Forms and Applications
  • Arizona Department of Water Resources: Well Drilling in Arizona
  • ADWR Permitting and Wells: Frequently Asked Questions

title: Mesa Adopted Building Codes: The 2018 I-Code Editions That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Mesa builds to the 2018 International Residential, Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fuel Gas, Existing Building, and Energy codes, plus the 2017 National Electrical Code, all with local amendments. The codes live in Mesa City Code Title 4. Mesa adopted its own Residential Code amendments in 2019.
bodyHtml:
Your house in Mesa is checked against a stack of national model codes that the City Council voted to adopt, then edited for local heat and soil. Knowing the exact edition matters, because a 2018 detail and a 2024 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs. This page lists the codes Mesa actually enforces today, where they live in city law, and where the local edits sit.

Which codes Mesa enforces

Mesa adopted the 2018 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2017 electrical code. Here is the current list:

  • 2018 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house. Mesa adopted its own Residential Code amendments in 2019.
  • 2018 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2018 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, duct sealing, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2018 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2018 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2018 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2018 International Existing Building Code (IEBC): remodels and additions to existing structures.
  • 2017 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the one a single-family builder lives in day to day. The City of Mesa is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its building officials, not the state, have the final word inside city limits.

Where the codes live in city law

Mesa does not just point at the raw model codes. It adopts them and layers its own edits inside the city code. The adopted codes and their amendments sit in Mesa City Code Title 4 (Building Regulations), where the Mesa Administrative Code (Section 4-1-4) and the Mesa Building Code (Section 4-2-1) carry the local changes. Mesa publishes a plain summary of why it edits the base codes:

The following list contains all sections of the 2018 Building Codes affected by the proposed amendments.

So the rule you build to is the published 2018 edition as changed by Mesa's Title 4 amendments. One real example from those amendments: Mesa requires a permit for a rooftop solar system larger than 10,000 square feet or one not installed by an Arizona licensed contractor, even though the base code would let smaller and contractor-installed systems skip the permit. That is a Mesa-specific rule a builder would miss by reading the model code alone.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your energy compliance path (insulation R-values, duct leakage testing, window U-factors) is set by the 2018 IECC as amended, plus the Mesa Residential Code energy rating index adopted in 2019. A plan drawn to a 2024 energy spec from a Phoenix job may not line up.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2017 NEC. An electrician working from a newer 2023 code book may add protections Mesa does not yet require, or quote a job to the wrong book.
  • Anything the model code says, a Mesa Title 4 amendment can override. The amendments are the tie-breaker, so they get read alongside the base code.

Here is a concrete example. Say you submit a custom home plan in Mesa in spring 2026. The plan reviewer checks your wall insulation against the 2018 IECC as amended, your wiring against the 2017 NEC, and your framing against the 2018 IRC. If your energy sheet was copied from a Phoenix project on the 2024 codes, the duct-leakage and insulation numbers may not match Mesa's adopted edition, and the plan comes back with corrections before a permit issues.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction: build to Mesa's 2018 I-codes and the 2017 NEC, with the Title 4 amendments applied. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition. Mesa sits a full cycle behind Phoenix, so a plan set built for a Phoenix lot is not automatically correct in Mesa. When you compare bids, confirm each contractor is quoting Mesa-current-code work, because retrofitting to code after a failed inspection costs far more than doing it right the first time.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Mesa building-codes module. From here, see Mesa Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review, and the certificate of occupancy that ends the process once the home passes final inspection. Mesa is in Maricopa County, so a rural Mesa-area lot with no city sewer or water routes its septic and well approvals through the county and the state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . To compare Mesa against the other cities Jematell builds in, see Arizona Building Code Editions by City . For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes and amendment documents are published by the City of Mesa: Mesa Building Code on mesaaz.gov . The codes are codified in Mesa City Code Title 4 (Building Regulations) . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with Mesa Development Services before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • City of Mesa Building Code
  • Mesa City Code Title 4 (Building Regulations)
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Mesa Building Inspections for New Homes: The IRC R109 Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Mesa inspects a new home in the order set by the 2018 IRC: foundation before concrete, rough plumbing and electrical, framing, then final. You schedule each one in the DIMES (Mesa E-Permit) portal, by text to 480-644-2428, or by automated phone. The Building Official issues the Certificate of Occupancy after the final passes.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Mesa is inspected in a fixed order while it is built, and each stage has to pass before the work gets covered by concrete, drywall, or stucco. Mesa builds to the 2018 International Residential Code (IRC), so the sequence follows IRC Section R109. The whole run ends with a final inspection that clears the way for your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. This page lays out the order, the three ways to schedule, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence under IRC R109

Mesa inspects the build from the ground up, calling each point while the work is still visible. The 2018 IRC sets the required stops, and Section R109.3 says it plainly:

It shall be the duty of the permit holder or their agent to notify the building official that such work is ready for inspection.

In order, the critical inspections are:

  1. Foundation, before concrete. The footing trenches, rebar, structural hold-downs, and the electrical grounding (UFER) are checked after forms and reinforcement are set but before any concrete is poured.
  2. Rough plumbing, mechanical, gas, and electrical. These are inspected before they are covered and before the framing inspection, while every pipe, duct, and wire is still open to view.
  3. Framing. Framing, firestopping, draftstopping, and bracing are inspected after the roof is on and after the rough trades pass.
  4. Final. Made after all work is complete and before anyone occupies the home.

The order is not optional because an inspector cannot approve what is already hidden. For example, say a Mesa framing crew finishes a 2,600 square foot house and the drywall crew hangs board before the rough electrical and framing are signed off. The inspector cannot see the wiring or the structural connections, so the work fails. Crews then cut out new drywall to expose it, re-inspect, and re-patch, adding a week and real labor cost. The City of Mesa is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , and its Development Services inspectors set and enforce this order.

How to schedule, and the hours

You request every inspection yourself, and you need the permit number to do it. Mesa gives three ways. First, online through DIMES, the city's name for its Mesa E-Permit (Accela) portal: open the permit record, find "inspections," and select schedule or request inspection. Second, by text message to 480-644-2428, sending the action (S to schedule, RS to reschedule, C to cancel), the permit number, the three-digit inspection code, and the date. Third, by the automated phone line, where you enter the same three-digit inspection code.

Regular inspections run 6:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m., Monday through Friday, excluding holidays. For work that has to be looked at after 5 p.m. on a weekday or on a weekend, Mesa runs an after-hours line at 480-644-4273. Pick the right three-digit code for the stage you are at, because the wrong code can send the wrong inspector and burn a day on your schedule.

How the final ties to your Certificate of Occupancy

The final inspection is the gate to occupancy. If an inspection fails, the inspector leaves correction notes, you fix the items, and you call for a re-inspection before work continues. On a new home, a passing final triggers the Certificate of Occupancy (CO). Mesa issues the CO after inspections are passed and any conditional holds are cleared, and the Building Official signs it. The CO lists the permit, the property address, the code edition, and the approved use. Only with the CO in hand are you legally cleared to move in, and a closing or a mortgage usually requires it.

For your build this plays out at three moments. On pour days, the foundation inspection has to pass before concrete goes in, so inspection drives the concrete schedule. At close-in, the rough and framing inspections gate the cover-up. At move-in, the final inspection and CO are what clear you to occupy.

What this means for you

Treat the inspection calendar as the backbone of your schedule, not an errand. Each stage must pass before the next trade covers its work, so one missed or failed inspection pushes everything behind it. Keep your permit number and the three-digit inspection codes handy, schedule through DIMES or the 480-644-2428 text line a day ahead, and never let a crew bury wiring, plumbing, or framing before it is signed off. Do not move family in until the Certificate of Occupancy is issued, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Mesa module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Mesa Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is in Mesa Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full journey, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The scheduling methods, inspection hours, and CO process above are published by the City of Mesa Development Services: Construction Inspections on mesaaz.gov . You schedule through the Mesa E-Permit (DIMES) portal . The required inspection stops come from 2018 IRC Section R109 , which Mesa adopted.

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. The Mesa city website blocks some automated visits, and scheduling numbers and CO steps change, so confirm the current process and codes with Mesa Development Services before you schedule.

Sources

  • City of Mesa Construction Inspections
  • Mesa E-Permit Online Portal (DIMES / Accela Citizen Access)
  • 2018 International Residential Code, Section R109 (Inspections)

title: Mesa Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: How the Charges Are Built
shortSummary: Mesa charges new-home permit fees from construction valuation run through a residential rate table, with a deposit due at plan submittal and the balance due before the permit issues. Separate water and wastewater capacity charges, set by meter size, act as the impact fees. Confirm the live dollar amounts on the city fee schedule.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Mesa carries two kinds of city cost: the building permit fee for plan review and inspections, and the development charges that pay for the water and sewer capacity your home will use. Mesa builds the permit fee from your home's construction value, not its square footage, and collects a deposit up front. The water and sewer side is charged as capacity fees keyed to your meter size. This page explains how each piece is calculated and where to pull the live number, since Mesa resets these amounts in its annual fee schedule.

How the permit fee is calculated

Mesa prices a new-home permit from construction valuation, the estimated dollar value of the work, run through a residential rate table in the Citywide Schedule of Fees and Charges. The city describes the method this way:

Valuations are determined by the applicant and verified by the Plans Examiner, and the Building Permit Fee is calculated by applying the Total Valuation to the Residential Rate Table.

For a small project under $25,000 in value, the minimum permit fee is set instead by the number of inspections the examiner decides the job needs.

Mesa collects the fee in two steps. A deposit is due when you submit your construction documents. The balance is calculated after plan review approves the plans, and the full permit fee is due before the permit issues. The city offers an expedited review that targets a five business-day turnaround, available for an extra charge equal to 100 percent of the building permit fee. So choosing expedited review effectively doubles the permit fee, which is a real budget decision on a custom home.

Because the rate table and the deposit percentage are published in the citywide schedule and updated by the city, confirm the current valuation rate and deposit on the live fee schedule before you budget. We could not pull the exact current dollar rates from the city's published PDF for this entry, so treat the structure here as the reliable part and get the live figure from the city.

The water and wastewater capacity fees are the impact fees

Separate from the permit fee, Mesa charges water and wastewater capacity fees on new construction. These are the city's version of impact fees , the charges a utility collects to pay for the plant and pipe capacity that new homes consume. Arizona authorizes them under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Mesa sets them by meter size, so a standard single-family home on a small residential meter pays the entry-level capacity fee, while larger meters pay much more.

The city's own rate proposal put the water capacity fee range across all meter sizes at roughly $7,719 to $1.4 million (small residential meter at the low end), and the wastewater capacity fee range at roughly $1,809 to $331,673. A typical new home sits near the bottom of each range, on the small meter. Mesa also adopts broader development impact fees under Chapter 17 of its city code. Because these reset by resolution, confirm the current fee schedule with the city before you budget. The capacity fees, not the permit fee, are usually the largest city cost on a Mesa new home.

When these fees come into play

  • Build-on-your-lot in Mesa. You file through the Mesa e-permit portal, pay the deposit at submittal, then pay the permit balance and the capacity fees before the permit issues.
  • Expedited review. If your schedule is tight, the five-day expedited option is available, but it doubles the permit fee. Decide whether the time is worth it.
  • Meter size. Because the capacity fees scale with meter size, oversizing your water meter beyond what the home needs raises the bill. Size it to the home.

What this means for you

Plan the permit fee from your home's construction value, and remember Mesa wants a deposit at submittal, not the whole fee at the end. Budget the water and wastewater capacity fees as a separate, larger line, and get the live meter-size amounts from the city, since those are the biggest city charge. If you are weighing expedited review, price it at a full extra copy of the permit fee. Use the Mesa Development Services fee schedule and the e-permit estimator to confirm your exact numbers before you file.

This entry sits in the building-codes module beside Mesa Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Mesa Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans must meet. For a metro-wide comparison of permit costs, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The fee basis above (valuation-based permit fee, submittal deposit, expedited-review surcharge, and meter-size capacity fees) is published by the City of Mesa Development Services: Residential Construction on mesaaz.gov . The dollar rates live in the Citywide Schedule of Fees and Charges , and the development impact fees are codified in Mesa City Code Chapter 17 .

Mesa resets these fees in its annual fee schedule, and the capacity-fee dollar amounts change. Confirm the current schedule with Mesa Development Services before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Mesa Residential Construction (Development Services)
  • City of Mesa Citywide Schedule of Fees and Charges
  • Mesa City Code Title 5, Chapter 17 (Development Impact Fees)

title: Mesa Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and How Review Works
shortSummary: A new home in Mesa needs a building permit before any work starts. You apply through Mesa's DIMES online portal with a scaled site plan, floor plans, and engineered construction documents, then pay fees. Mesa runs an all-electronic review and provides written comments or approval within 10 working days on a standard residential review.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Mesa home until the city issues a building permit. The permit is the city's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes and your lot's zoning. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how Mesa's online system handles it, how the city calculates your fee, and what the review timeline looks like.

What a residential permit requires

Mesa runs permits through its online portal, called DIMES, the Development Information Management and E-permit System. You create an account, upload your plan set, and pay fees there. Mesa's permitting is fully electronic, with no paper counter submittal for plans. A complete single-family submittal generally includes:

  • A site plan drawn to scale showing the house on the lot, with property lines, easements, fences, and setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out.
  • Floor plans, one sheet per level.
  • Structural and construction documents engineered for Arizona loads.
  • Energy compliance paperwork showing the design meets the 2018 IECC as amended by Mesa.

The city is specific about the site plan scale:

A fully dimensioned site plan drawn to scale is required... with a minimum scale of 1" = 20'-0" or 1/8" = 1'-0".

Plans that miss the format or scale rules get kicked back before review even starts, which costs you days.

How review and timeline work

Mesa commits to a clear clock on residential plans. The city provides written comments or approval within 10 working days for a standard review, and expedited review is available for an added fee. That 10-day window is not just a courtesy, it tracks Arizona's requirement that cities complete a first review of residential plans within ten business days.

For example, you upload a 3,000 square foot custom home set on a Monday, and under the 10-working-day window you get written corrections or an approval back within two weeks. The flow is: apply and upload through DIMES, the city runs plan review, you get written corrections or an approval, you fix and resubmit any sheets that drew comments, then you pay permit fees and the permit issues. After that, the work moves to field inspections, and the home is not legal to occupy until it passes final inspection and the city issues a certificate of occupancy .

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Mesa build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the DIMES package, uploads it, and the review clock runs toward that 10-working-day comment window.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, you carry the responsibility a contractor normally would. Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121 only covers a home you intend to occupy yourself, not one built for sale or rent within a year, so pulling your own permit ties you to that legal limit.
  • Resubmittals. If review finds corrections, you fix the sheets and re-upload through DIMES. Each round adds calendar time, so a clean first submittal is the cheapest path.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item and build the 10-working-day first-review window into your schedule, plus time for at least one round of corrections, rather than assuming the permit is instant. Get the site plan setbacks right the first time, because a setback violation is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Draw the energy and structural sheets to Mesa's adopted 2018 editions, not a newer set copied from a Phoenix job, since a wrong-edition sheet is the other frequent correction. Confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder, since that decides who carries responsibility for the build.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Mesa module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Mesa Adopted Building Codes . Mesa is in Maricopa County, so a rural Mesa-area lot with no city sewer or water also needs septic and well approvals, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . To see how Mesa's permitting compares with the rest of the metro, see Arizona Building Code Editions by City . For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The permit process, electronic submittal, and plan-review timeline are published by the City of Mesa: Building Permit and Plan Review on mesaaz.gov , with submittal detail on the Residential Construction page . The owner-builder limit is set by A.R.S. 32-1121 .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the city's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with Mesa Development Services before you file.

Sources

  • City of Mesa Building Permit and Plan Review
  • City of Mesa Residential Construction
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Mesa Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Where Your New Home Can Sit on the Lot
shortSummary: Mesa single-dwelling zoning runs from RS-6 on 6,000 square foot lots to RS-90 on 90,000 square footers, and Table 11-5-3 sets each yard. RS-6 needs a 10 foot front, 5 foot sides, and 20 foot rear. RS-43 needs a 22 foot front and 10 foot sides, all capped at 30 feet.
bodyHtml:
Before you draw a floor plan in Mesa, find your zoning district, because the district sets how far the house must sit from each property line. That open distance is called a setback, and it is the rule that decides where the house can go on the lot. Mesa names its single-dwelling districts RS, from RS-6 (6,000 square feet) up to RS-90 (90,000 square feet), and one table, Table 11-5-3.A.1, carries the yards for all of them.

What the districts require

Mesa's Section 11-5-3 sets the development standards for the RS districts. Every RS district caps building height at 30 feet, but the yards and coverage change as lots get bigger. The front setback below is for enclosed living space; attached garages sit farther back. Here are the published numbers:

  • RS-6: 6,000 sq ft min lot, front 10 ft, interior side 5 ft each (15 ft for both), rear 20 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 60%.
  • RS-7: 7,000 sq ft min lot, front 10 ft, interior side 5 ft each (15 ft for both), rear 20 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 60%.
  • RS-9: 9,000 sq ft min lot, front 15 ft, interior side 7 ft each (17 ft for both), rear 25 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 60%.
  • RS-15: 15,000 sq ft min lot, front 22 ft, interior side 7 ft each (20 ft for both), rear 30 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 50%.
  • RS-35: 35,000 sq ft min lot, front 22 ft, interior side 10 ft each (30 ft for both), rear 30 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 50%.
  • RS-43: 43,560 sq ft min lot, front 22 ft, interior side 10 ft each (30 ft for both), rear 30 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 40%.
  • RS-90: 90,000 sq ft min lot, front 22 ft, interior side 20 ft each (40 ft for both), rear 30 ft, height 30 ft, lot coverage 40%.

Mesa sets the garage farther from the street than the house itself:

Garages and Carports - front and side yards: 30 [feet for RS-90 through RS-15].

So on an RS-15 lot your front porch can sit 22 feet back while the garage door must sit 30 feet back, which is the rule that keeps a garage from dominating the street. On the smaller RS-7 and RS-6 lots that garage setback drops to 20 feet.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks and coverage drive the site plan before you draw the house:

  • Build-on-your-lot. For example, on an RS-43 acre lot, the 22 foot front, 30 foot rear, and 10 foot sides carve a border out of the parcel, and the 40% coverage cap limits the house-plus-garage footprint. The lot coverage cap and the setbacks are separate tests, and the house has to pass both.
  • Interior side, two ways. Mesa sets both a per-side minimum and a combined minimum. On RS-90 that is 20 feet on each side and 40 feet total, so you cannot bank both into one wide side yard.
  • Rear yard against an arterial. A rear yard backing an arterial street must be at least 30 feet deep, which can push the house forward on a through lot.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a Mesa plan gets corrected at review, so the city confirms each yard distance before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact RS district before you buy or design, because the jump from RS-6 to RS-90 changes the front yard from 10 feet to 22 feet and the sides from 5 feet to 20 feet. Watch lot coverage alongside the yards, since RS-6 allows up to 60% coverage while RS-43 and RS-90 allow only 40%, which caps the house size on a big lot. Mesa holds the height to 30 feet across every RS district, so your roofline is capped the same whether you build on a small lot or an acre. We run the setbacks, the garage setback, and the coverage cap on the first day of a Mesa project, because they decide whether a floor plan fits or has to change.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Mesa module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Mesa Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Mesa Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The per-district lot sizes, setbacks, the 30 foot height limit, and coverage caps are published by the City of Mesa in Table 11-5-3 of the Zoning Ordinance: view the RS district standards on municode.com . The city's code hub is at Mesa Development Codes, Ordinances, Regulations and Standards .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks and coverage change with overlays, the RSL small-lot standards, and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Mesa Development Services before you design.

Sources

  • City of Mesa Zoning Ordinance, Section 11-5-3 (Development Standards for the RS and RSL Districts)
  • City of Mesa Development Codes, Ordinances, Regulations and Standards
  • Maricopa County Assessor parcel and zoning lookup
