title: Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes: The 2015 I-Code Editions That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Paradise Valley builds to the 2015 International Residential, Building, Mechanical, Plumbing, Fuel Gas, Property Maintenance, and Energy codes, plus the 2014 National Electrical Code, with the 2021 International Fire Code on top. The Town adopted the 2015 International Codes by Ordinance 2016-02 on May 26, 2016.
bodyHtml:
Your house in Paradise Valley is checked against a stack of national model codes that the Town Council voted to adopt, then edited for local conditions. Knowing the exact edition matters, because a 2015 detail and a 2024 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs. Paradise Valley runs an older cycle than most of the metro, so this matters more here than almost anywhere Jematell builds. This page lists the codes the Town actually enforces today, when it adopted them, and what the gap means for your plans.

Which codes Paradise Valley enforces

Paradise Valley adopted the 2015 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2014 electrical code, with one newer code layered on for fire. Here is the current list, published by the Town:

  • 2015 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2015 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2015 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2015 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2015 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2015 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, duct sealing, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2015 International Property Maintenance Code (IPMC): upkeep of existing buildings.
  • 2021 International Fire Code (IFC): fire safety, the one code on a newer cycle than the rest.
  • 2014 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the one a single-family builder lives in day to day. The Town of Paradise Valley is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its building officials, not the state, have the final word inside town limits.

When and how the Town adopted them

Paradise Valley adopted the 2015 International Codes by Ordinance 2016-02 on May 26, 2016, and codified them in Chapter 5 (Building and Construction) of the Town Code. The fire code was later updated to the 2021 edition on its own track. The Town states the adopted set plainly:

International Codes adopted by Town of Paradise Valley: 2015 International Residential Code, 2015 International Building Code...

The Town also fixes the local design values a Paradise Valley plan must use, including a 115 mph wind design speed, Seismic Design Category B, a 12 inch frost depth, and a moderate-to-heavy termite hazard. Those numbers come from the adopted 2015 IRC as the Town filled in its local Table R301.2 data, so they belong on the structural sheets from the start.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your energy compliance path (insulation R-values, duct leakage testing, window U-factors) is set by the 2015 IECC, which is three cycles behind Phoenix's 2024 energy code. The proof you submit is different, and usually lighter, than a newer city would demand.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2014 NEC. That is three cycles behind the 2023 NEC a Phoenix job uses, so the required electrical protections differ.
  • The fire code is the exception. Paradise Valley enforces the 2021 IFC, newer than its other codes, so fire-access, sprinkler, and water-supply rules follow the more current edition.

Here is a concrete example. Say you submit a custom home plan in Paradise Valley in spring 2026. The plan reviewer checks your framing against the 2015 IRC, your energy sheet against the 2015 IECC, your wiring against the 2014 NEC, and your fire details against the 2021 IFC. If your set was drawn for a Scottsdale lot on the 2021 codes and the 2020 NEC, the energy and electrical sheets are built to newer editions than Paradise Valley adopted, and the mismatch can trigger plan-review corrections even though the newer work is technically stronger.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction: build to Paradise Valley's 2015 I-codes and the 2014 NEC, with the 2021 fire code. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition. Paradise Valley runs an unusually old cycle, so a plan set built for any neighboring city is almost never a clean match. The town is known for large custom estates, and its fire code being a step ahead of its building codes is the wrinkle that catches builders, so confirm fire-access and water-supply details to the 2021 IFC even while the rest of the set follows 2015.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Paradise Valley building-codes module. From here, see Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review, and the certificate of occupancy that ends the process once the home passes final inspection. Paradise Valley is in Maricopa County, so a lot with no town sewer or water routes its septic and well approvals through the county and the state, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . To compare Paradise Valley against the other cities Jematell builds in, see Arizona Building Code Editions by City . For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

title: Paradise Valley Building Inspections for New Homes: Scheduling, Sequence, and CO
shortSummary: Paradise Valley inspects a new home in stages, from foundation before concrete through framing to final, with each stage passing before the work is covered. You schedule by the Town's interactive phone line at 480-348-3581 (24/7) or online through the Citizen Portal. A final inspection is required on every permit before occupancy.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Paradise Valley gets inspected in a set order as it is built, and each stage must pass before the work is buried behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The sequence follows the International Residential Code (IRC) Section R109, which the Town adopted. It ends with a final inspection that is required on every permit and clears the home for occupancy. This page covers how to schedule, the order of the stops, and how the final ties to your move-in.

How to schedule, with the next-day cutoff

You request each inspection yourself, and you need a valid permit number to do it. The Town gives two ways, and it is direct about both:

The Town of Paradise Valley has now implemented an interactive phone response system that provides access for the scheduling of building inspections 24 hours a day, seven days a week. You may dial into this system at 480-348-3581. Calls received before 3 p.m. will be issued a confirmation number, and the requested inspection performed the next business day.

The second way is online through the Citizen Portal, the Town's Accela permitting system, where you can request inspections, track status, and upload documents. You must have a valid permit number for either system. The 3 p.m. cutoff is the one to plan around: a request logged at 2:45 p.m. lands the next business day, while one at 3:15 p.m. slips a day. If the system gives you trouble, a Building Services staff member answers at 480-948-7411.

For example, suppose your superintendent calls in a framing inspection at 4 p.m. on a Thursday thinking it will be seen Friday. Because it missed the 3 p.m. cutoff, it is scheduled for the following business day, and on a holiday week that can push the drywall crew back several days. Logging the request before 3 p.m. the day before is the habit that keeps the trades moving.

The inspection sequence

Paradise Valley inspects the build from the ground up, calling each point while the work is still visible. The IRC sets the required stops, and the Town is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that enforces them. In order:

  1. Foundation, before concrete. Footing trenches, rebar, hold-downs, and the electrical grounding are checked after forms and reinforcement are set, before any pour.
  2. Rough plumbing, mechanical, gas, and electrical. Checked before they are covered and before framing, while pipes, ducts, and wires are open.
  3. Framing. Checked after the roof is on and the rough trades pass, covering framing, firestopping, draftstopping, and bracing.
  4. Final. Made after all work is complete and before anyone occupies the home.

An inspector cannot approve what is already hidden, so the order is fixed. This matters more in Paradise Valley than in most of the metro because the Town runs an older code cycle (the 2015 IRC), and large custom estates here carry long inspection lists. A failed stage means correction notes, a fix, and a re-inspection before work continues.

The final inspection and occupancy

The Town states that a final inspection must be performed on every permit, including demolition permits. On a new home, that final is the gate to occupancy. After it passes and any holds clear, the Town issues the Certificate of Occupancy (CO), the document that legally lets you move in and that a closing or mortgage usually requires. The final inspection is therefore the single most schedule-critical visit on the whole job.

This plays out at three moments in your build. On pour days, the foundation inspection has to pass before concrete goes in. At close-in, the rough and framing inspections gate the cover-up. At move-in, the final and the CO clear you to occupy.

What this means for you

Build the inspection calendar into your schedule and protect the 3 p.m. cutoff. Schedule through the 480-348-3581 line or the Citizen Portal the day before you need an inspection, and always have your permit number ready. Never let a crew cover wiring, plumbing, or framing before it passes, because exposing it again costs a week. Do not move family in until the Certificate of Occupancy is issued, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Paradise Valley module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is in Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full journey, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The phone scheduling system, the 3 p.m. cutoff, the permit-number requirement, the Citizen Portal option, and the every-permit final inspection rule are published by the Town of Paradise Valley: Inspection Scheduling on paradisevalleyaz.gov . You schedule online through the Citizen Portal . The required inspection stops come from 2015 IRC Section R109 , the edition the Town adopted.

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Scheduling steps and CO requirements change, so confirm the current process with Paradise Valley Building Services before you schedule.

Sources

  • Town of Paradise Valley Inspection Scheduling
  • Town of Paradise Valley Citizen Portal (Accela Citizen Access)
  • 2015 International Residential Code, Section R109 (Inspections)

title: Paradise Valley Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: Valuation Plus a 65 Percent Review
shortSummary: Paradise Valley prices a new-home building permit by construction valuation, set from the building valuation data in the Town's Master Fee Schedule. In-house plan review adds 65 percent of the permit fee and covers up to two reviews, with extra reviews at $145 per hour. The Town charges no municipal impact fee.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Paradise Valley carries a building permit fee built on the construction valuation of your house, plus a separate plan review fee charged as a percentage of that permit fee. Paradise Valley is an estate town with no municipal water or sewer impact-fee stack, so the permit and its review are the main Town charges, and your well and septic are the big separate costs. This page gives you the real fee basis from the Town's own schedule, with honest flags on where the live dollar figure has to come from the Town.

How Paradise Valley sets the permit fee

Paradise Valley prices a new-home permit from valuation, the estimated construction cost, and the Town's schedule is clear about where that value comes from:

The most current building valuation data as stated in the Town of Paradise Valley Master Fee Schedule shall be used for purposes of determining valuations. The Town reserves the right to verify construction valuation.

So the Town sets your home's valuation from the building valuation data published in its Master Fee Schedule, then runs the permit fee off that figure. Two things follow. First, a larger or higher-value home pays a larger permit fee, because the fee tracks the valuation. Second, the Town can verify and adjust your valuation, so a low self-reported number does not lower the fee if it falls below the Town's data. Paradise Valley builds large custom estates, so valuations here run high, and the permit fee runs high with them.

The 65 percent in-house plan review fee

On top of the permit fee, Paradise Valley charges an in-house plan review fee, and the Town states the rate plainly:

The in-house review fee is 65% of the building permit fee. The review fee covers up to two plan reviews. Additional reviews will be charged at a rate of $145.00 per hour.

So plan review is 65 percent of the permit fee, not a flat charge. If your permit fee works out to $4,000, the in-house review adds about $2,600, for roughly $6,600 in Town building charges before any trade permits. That review covers your first two plan submittals. If your set comes back twice and needs a third review, each added hour is billed at $145. On a complex hillside estate that bounces through review more than twice, those hourly charges add up, which is a real reason to submit a clean, code-correct set the first time.

The exact permit-fee dollars come from the building valuation data in the Master Fee Schedule, which the Town updates and which is not reliably machine-readable from the Town site. So treat the structure here (valuation-based permit fee, plus 65 percent review, plus $145 per hour past two reviews) as the verified part, and pull the live permit figure for your valuation from the Paradise Valley Building Department at 480-948-7411.

Why there is no municipal impact fee

Impact fees are charges a city collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth uses, authorized in Arizona under A.R.S. 9-463.05. Many Valley cities charge them for water, sewer, parks, and fire. Paradise Valley runs no town water or town sewer utility and does not levy that municipal stack. Water service comes from private providers, and a lot without sewer uses an on-site septic system.

That shifts the budget. Inside a fee-charging city, impact fees can be the largest line on a new home. In Paradise Valley, your large separate costs are the septic system (permitted through Maricopa County and the state) and your water connection through the serving utility, not a town impact fee. So the building permit, the 65 percent review, and your septic and water are the real numbers to plan around.

When these fees come into play

  • At permit application. The Town sets your valuation, invoices the permit fee, and adds the 65 percent in-house review. The review covers your first two submittals.
  • Re-reviews cost money. A third or later plan review is billed at $145 per hour, so corrections are not free. A clean first submittal saves real dollars.
  • Septic and water are separate. Because there is no town impact fee, budget the septic permit and install and the water connection on their own, through the county, state, and your water provider.

What this means for you

Plan the Paradise Valley permit as a valuation-based fee plus a 65 percent in-house review, the same two-part structure the Town publishes. Expect the Town to verify your valuation, so build an honest cost number, since estate-scale valuations drive a high permit fee here. Keep your plan set clean to stay inside the two included reviews and avoid the $145-per-hour re-review charge. Then budget your septic and water connection separately, because Paradise Valley has no municipal impact fee to fold in. The dollar figures behind the valuation data change when the Town updates its schedule, so confirm the current permit fee for your valuation with the Paradise Valley Building Department before you rely on a number here.

This entry sits in the Paradise Valley building-codes module alongside Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans are checked against. For how the permit basis differs across the metro, see valuation versus per-square-foot permit pricing in Arizona and how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The valuation basis, the 65 percent in-house review rate, the two included reviews, and the $145-per-hour re-review charge are published in the Town of Paradise Valley's Master Fee Schedule and building-permit materials, a public record: Master Fee Schedule on paradisevalleyaz.gov . The permit process and forms are on the Town Building Department and Permits and Handouts pages. The statute behind impact fees is A.R.S. 9-463.05 .

These fees change when the Town updates its schedule, and the live per-valuation figures are not easily pulled from the Town site. Confirm the current permit fee, the review fee, and your septic and water costs with the Paradise Valley Building Department before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • Town of Paradise Valley Master Fee Schedule (Building Safety)
  • Town of Paradise Valley Building Department
  • Town of Paradise Valley Permits and Handouts
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Paradise Valley Hillside Development Requirements: How Slope Limits What You Can Build
shortSummary: Paradise Valley's Hillside Development Regulations (Article XXII) control building on land sloped 10% or steeper. The steeper the lot, the less of it you may disturb, dropping from 60% of the building site at a 10% slope to about 34% at 15% and into single digits on very steep ground. A Hillside Building Committee reviews every hillside home first.
bodyHtml:
If your Paradise Valley lot sits on a slope of 10 percent or more, the Town's Hillside Development Regulations decide how much of that lot you are allowed to touch. These rules, in Article XXII of the Town's Zoning Ordinance, exist to protect the mountains and foothills the Town is known for, and they get stricter as the ground gets steeper. On a steep estate lot, more than half the parcel can be off-limits to grading or building. This page explains the rule, who it hits, and how slope sets the size of the house you can actually fit.

What the hillside rules do

Article XXII limits how intensely you can develop sloped land. The Town states its purpose directly:

This article exists to establish provisions to: a) regulate the intensity of development; b) preserve and protect the hillside environment; c) provide for the safety and welfare of the Town and its residents.

The rules kick in on any land with a slope of 10 percent or more. The Town treats that as hillside terrain. The heart of the ordinance is a cap on disturbed area, the part of the natural ground you alter by grading, cutting, filling, removing plants, or trenching. The steeper the building site, the smaller that allowed share. The Town sets it on a sliding scale in its Table 1, Slope Category / Lot Disturbance Limitations:

  • At a 10 percent building-site slope, you may disturb up to 60 percent of the site.
  • At a 15 percent slope, the limit drops to about 34 percent.
  • At a 25 percent slope, it is roughly 13 percent.
  • On very steep ground past 50 percent, the allowed disturbance falls into the single digits.

So slope, more than lot size, controls how much house and yard you can build. A wash that creates a natural 10 percent slope can pull a parcel into these rules even if the rest of the lot is flat.

Who reviews it and how it limits a build

Paradise Valley does not just apply the table on paper. A Hillside Building Committee reviews every new home on a hillside lot. It looks at land disturbance, grading, drainage, retaining walls, building materials, exterior lighting, and height before any permit issues. The Town requires its approval before earth moves:

Plans for any development in the Hillside Development Area must be approved by the Town and appropriate permits issued before any clearing and grubbing, grading, bulldozing, blasting, or movement of earth is commenced.

The Town of Paradise Valley is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts and enforces these rules, so its hillside committee and building staff, not the county or the state, have the final say. This sits on top of your normal setbacks (the required distance from each property line) and lot coverage (the share of the lot the footprint may cover). On a hillside lot, the disturbance cap is usually the tighter limit.

What this means for you

Check the slope before you buy a Paradise Valley lot, because slope, not acreage, sets your buildable area. For example, suppose you are choosing between two half-acre lots. One has an 11 percent slope and the other a 25 percent slope. On the gentler lot you can disturb most of the site, but on the steep one you may be limited to roughly an eighth of it, which can force a smaller footprint, a tighter driveway, and more retaining wall. Two lots the same size can support very different homes.

Build the hillside review into your schedule and budget. The Hillside Building Committee is an added approval step before your building permit, so it adds calendar time a flat-lot build does not have. Plan the disturbance limit, drainage, and retaining walls into the design from the first sketch, because reworking a footprint to fit the cap is one of the more expensive corrections here. You can look up a parcel's slope on the Maricopa County Assessor parcel viewer to get a rough read before you make an offer.

This entry is a city-special topic in the Paradise Valley module. The codes your home is built to are in Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes , and what you file for the permit is in Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the lot-buying side, see how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , and for the full journey, the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The hillside review and permitting are run by the Town of Paradise Valley Building Department . The purpose, the disturbance limits, and the review process are set in the Town's Zoning Ordinance, Article XXII, administered by the Hillside Building Committee . The Town's building codes are in the Town Code .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Slope categories and disturbance limits depend on the specific parcel, so confirm your lot's hillside classification with the Paradise Valley Planning Department before you rely on a number.

Sources

  • Town of Paradise Valley Hillside Building Committee (Article XXII review)
  • Town of Paradise Valley Town Code (Building and Construction)
  • Maricopa County Assessor Parcel Viewer (parcel slope lookup)

title: Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and How Review Works
shortSummary: A new home in Paradise Valley needs a building permit before any work starts. You apply through the Town's online Citizen Portal with a scaled site plan, floor plans, and engineered construction documents, then pay fees from the Town's Master Fee Schedule. Town staff review every plan against the adopted Town codes before the permit issues.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Paradise Valley home until the Town issues a building permit. The permit is the Town's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes and your lot's zoning. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how the Town's online system handles it, how fees are set, and what review looks like for a custom home in a town known for large estates.

What a residential permit requires

Paradise Valley takes applications through its online Citizen Portal, the Town's Accela-based permitting system. You create an account, upload your plan set, and submit there. The Town issues separate permits for the different parts of a job, and the Building Department lists the types it handles:

Building, Banner, Demolition, Electrical, Fence/Site Walls, Mechanical, Plumbing, and Pool/Spa permits.

A complete single-family submittal generally includes a site plan drawn to scale showing the house on the lot with setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out, floor plans, and structural and construction documents engineered for Paradise Valley's local design values, including the 115 mph wind speed and Seismic Design Category B the Town fixes for its area. The Town reviews and approves every set against its adopted codes before a permit issues.

How fees and timing work

Fees come from the Town's published Master Fee Schedule, which sets the cost of each permit type, so you can price the permit before you apply. The Town also runs on a tight counter clock and states it plainly:

The Town of Paradise Valley does not accept application submittals, payments, nor release permits after 3:30 p.m.

The Building Department is open Monday through Friday, 7 a.m. to noon and 1 to 4 p.m., with the office closed for lunch. For example, if you submit a custom home set at 3 p.m., it clears the same-day cutoff, but a resubmittal you upload at 3:45 p.m. does not count until the next business day. The flow is: submit through the Citizen Portal, Town staff review the plans against the adopted Town codes, you address any corrections and resubmit, then you pay fees and the permit issues. After that the work moves to field inspections, and the home is not legal to occupy until it passes final inspection and the Town issues a certificate of occupancy .

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Paradise Valley build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the Citizen Portal package, you pay from the Master Fee Schedule, and the Town reviews the set against its adopted 2015 codes and 2021 fire code.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, you carry the responsibility a contractor normally would. Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121 only covers a home you intend to occupy yourself, not one built for sale or rent within a year, so pulling your own permit ties you to that legal limit.
  • Resubmittals. If review finds corrections, you fix the sheets and re-upload through the portal. Each round adds calendar time, and the 3:30 p.m. cutoff means a late-day resubmittal does not count until the next business day, so plan your filing earlier in the day.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item and pull the Master Fee Schedule early so the permit cost is no surprise. Get the site plan setbacks right the first time, because a setback violation is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected, and it matters on the large estate lots Paradise Valley is built around. Draw your structural, energy, and electrical sheets to the Town's adopted editions, the 2015 I-codes and 2014 NEC, with fire details to the newer 2021 fire code, since a wrong-edition sheet is the other frequent correction. Plan submittals and resubmittals before the 3:30 p.m. cutoff so you do not lose a day, and confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Paradise Valley module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes . Paradise Valley is in Maricopa County, so a lot with no town sewer or water also needs septic and well approvals, covered in Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements . To see how Paradise Valley compares with the rest of the metro, see Arizona Building Code Editions by City . For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The permit types, portal, fee schedule, and counter hours are published by the Town of Paradise Valley: Building Department on paradisevalleyaz.gov . The adopted codes your plans are checked against are codified in the Town Code, Chapter 5 (Building and Construction) . The owner-builder limit is set by A.R.S. 32-1121 .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the Town's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and hours change, so confirm the live requirements with the Paradise Valley Building Department before you file.

  • Town of Paradise Valley Building Department
  • Town of Paradise Valley Town Code (Chapter 5, Building and Construction)
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Paradise Valley Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Estate Lots and Where the House Goes
shortSummary: Paradise Valley is an estate town: the base R-43 district requires a full acre per home, with a 40 foot front and rear yard and 20 foot sides. The larger R-175 needs over four acres and 40 foot yards all around. Most lots cap building height near 24 feet, well below the 30 feet allowed in nearby cities.
bodyHtml:
Every zoning district in this town is built around the same idea: large lots, deep yards, and a low profile. The base single-family district, R-43, requires a full acre per home, and the largest district, R-175, requires over four acres. There are no small-lot single-family zones here the way Phoenix or Mesa have them. That pattern is why the setback, the open distance your home must keep from each property line, runs wide and the height limit runs low across Paradise Valley.

What the districts require

Paradise Valley's Article X, Table 1001-A1, sets the minimum lot, yards, and floor-area ratio for each district. Side yards depend on height: a one-story house up to 24 feet can use the smaller side yard, while a two-story house needs the larger one. Here are the published numbers:

  • R-175: 175,000 sq ft min lot (over 4 acres), 165 ft lot width, front 40 ft, side 40 ft, rear 40 ft, floor-area ratio 25%.
  • R-43: 43,560 sq ft min lot (one acre), 165 ft lot width, front 40 ft, side 20 ft (40 ft for two stories or over 24 ft tall), rear 40 ft, floor-area ratio 25%.
  • R-35: 35,000 sq ft min lot, 150 ft lot width, front 40 ft, side 20 ft (40 ft for a second story), rear 40 ft, floor-area ratio 25%.
  • R-18: 18,000 sq ft min lot, 120 ft lot width, front 40 ft, side 20 ft, rear 40 ft, floor-area ratio 25%.
  • R-10: 10,000 sq ft min lot, 100 ft lot width, yards per the recorded plat or as existing.

Building height is tied to lot size, and it tops out low:

Less than 3 [acres]: 24 [feet] not including chimneys. 3 or greater but less than 4: 26. 4 or greater: 30 including chimneys.

So a one-acre R-43 lot is held to 24 feet, well under the 30 feet allowed in Phoenix, Scottsdale, or Mesa. Only the largest estate lots over four acres reach 30 feet. On a non-hillside R-43 or R-175 lot, an extra rule applies. A building may not break through an angled plane that rises from the 20 foot setback line. This holds two-story mass back from the property edge.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks and floor-area ratio drive the site plan before you draw the house:

  • Build-on-your-lot. For example, on a one-acre R-43 lot, the 40 foot front and rear plus 20 foot sides carve a large border out of the parcel, and the 25% floor-area ratio caps total floor area. The lot coverage idea here is the floor-area ratio, and the house has to pass it and the setbacks both.
  • Two-story side yards. A second story on an R-43 lot doubles the side yard from 20 feet to 40 feet, so going up costs you width on the sides.
  • Hillside lots. Parcels in a designated Hillside area follow separate rules, so a sloped lot can have a smaller usable pad than its acreage suggests.

A setback miss on the site plan is a common reason a Paradise Valley plan gets corrected at review, so the town confirms each yard distance and the height plane before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact district and lot size first, because height in Paradise Valley follows acreage: under 3 acres caps at 24 feet, 3 to 4 acres at 26 feet, and only 4 acres or more reaches 30 feet. Plan your floor area against the 25% ratio early. On a one-acre R-43 lot that works out to roughly 10,890 square feet, before garages and porches are counted. Decide on one or two stories up front, because a second story widens the R-43 side yards from 20 feet to 40 feet. We map the setbacks, the height plane, and the floor-area ratio together on the first day of a Paradise Valley project, because the town's estate rules, not just the floor plan, decide what fits.

This entry connects to the rest of the Paradise Valley module. The codes your plans are reviewed against are in Paradise Valley Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Paradise Valley Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The per-district lot sizes, yards, floor-area ratio, and the lot-size height limits are published by the Town of Paradise Valley in Article X (Height and Area Regulations), Table 1001-A1, of the Zoning Ordinance, effective February 26, 2021: the ordinance is linked from the Paradise Valley Planning page and the Planning Application Portal .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks, the height plane, and hillside rules change with amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Paradise Valley Community Development before you design.

Sources

  • Town of Paradise Valley Zoning Ordinance, Article X (Height and Area Regulations), Table 1001-A1
  • Town of Paradise Valley Planning Application Portal (Zoning Ordinance)
  • Maricopa County Assessor parcel and zoning lookup

title: Phoenix Adopted Building Codes: The 2024 PBCC Editions That Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Phoenix builds to the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code, which adopts the 2024 International Residential, Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fuel Gas, and Energy codes plus the 2023 National Electrical Code, with local amendments. The City Council approved it June 18, 2025, by Ordinance G-7397, effective August 1, 2025.
bodyHtml:
Your house in Phoenix is checked against a stack of national model codes that the City Council voted to adopt, then edited for local heat and soil. Knowing the exact edition matters, because a 2018 detail and a 2024 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs. This page lists the codes Phoenix actually enforces today, when they took effect, and where the local edits live.

Which codes Phoenix enforces

Phoenix adopted the 2024 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2023 electrical code. The whole package is bundled as the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code (PBCC). Here is the current list:

  • 2024 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2024 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2024 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, duct sealing, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2024 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures. Phoenix also adopts the 2024 Uniform Plumbing Code (UPC) as an alternate path.
  • 2024 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2024 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2023 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the one a single-family builder lives in day to day. The City of Phoenix is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its building officials, not the state, have the final word inside city limits.

What the city says

Phoenix is direct about when the new code took over:

On June 18, 2025, the Phoenix City Council approved the adoption of the 2024 Phoenix Building Construction Code (PBCC). The City of Phoenix is transitioning to this updated code with an effective date of August 1, 2025.

The 2024 PBCC was adopted by Ordinance G-7397, and each model code carries its own Phoenix amendments. Accessibility follows the referenced ICC A117.1-2017 standard as scoped in the amended 2024 IBC, plus the 2010 ADA Standards for Accessible Design. So the rule you build to is the published 2024 edition as changed by Phoenix's amendment document for that code.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your energy compliance path (insulation R-values, duct leakage testing, window U-factors) is set by the 2024 IECC as amended, not an older energy code. A plan drawn to a 2018 energy spec can get bounced in review.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2023 NEC. An electrician working off a 2017 code book may miss newer required protections.
  • Anything the model code says, a Phoenix amendment can override. The amendment documents are the tie-breaker, so they get read alongside the base code.

Here is a concrete example. Suppose you submit a custom home plan in Phoenix in spring 2026. The plan reviewer checks your wall insulation against the 2024 IECC as amended, your wiring against the 2023 NEC, and your framing against the 2024 IRC. If your energy sheet was copied from an older 2018 project, the duct-leakage and insulation numbers may not match, and the plan comes back with corrections before a permit issues.

Timing is the wrinkle in Phoenix right now. The prior code was the 2018 PBCC (Ordinance G-6463) with the 2017 NEC. If you pulled a permit before August 1, 2025, your project may still run under that 2018 edition. Edition is tied to when the permit was issued, not the calendar today.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction: build to Phoenix's 2024 I-codes and the 2023 NEC, with the city amendments applied. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition. When you compare bids, confirm each contractor is quoting current-code work, because retrofitting to code after a failed inspection costs far more than doing it right the first time.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Phoenix building-codes module. From here, see Phoenix Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review, and Phoenix Building Inspections for New Homes for how they get verified in the field. For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, effective date, and amendment documents are published by the City of Phoenix: Building Construction Codes on phoenix.gov . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with Phoenix Planning and Development before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix Building Construction Codes
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona

title: Phoenix Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Phoenix inspects a new home in stages, from before-concrete through framing to final, and each stage must pass before the work is covered up. You request inspections through the AIRS phone line or the online permitting system. After the final inspection passes, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy that lets you move in.
bodyHtml:
A Phoenix home is inspected in a set order while it is being built, and each phase has to pass before the work is buried behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The whole sequence ends with a final inspection that unlocks your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. This page lays out the order, how to request each inspection, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence

Inspections follow the build from the ground up, and the city calls each one while the work is still visible. Phoenix groups the critical points like this:

  1. Before concrete is poured. Underground plumbing and electrical, foundation rebar, structural hold-downs, footing depth and width, and the electrical grounding (UFER) are all inspected before any concrete covers them.
  2. Rough framing (strap and brace). Framing member spacing and size, ties and clips, headers and lintels, and shear wall location and size are inspected after the framing is up.
  3. Pre-drywall (before insulation and drywall). The rough plumbing, mechanical, and electrical are checked, windows are checked for safety glazing and energy requirements, the outside water-resistant covering goes in before stucco, and if the roof is tile, the roof is loaded with the truss calculations on site.
  4. Final. Counters, cabinets, plumbing fixtures, outlets and lights, registers, smoke detectors, doors and windows, gas vents, HVAC equipment, and the water meter are all installed and inspected.

The order matters because an inspector cannot approve what is already hidden. For example, suppose a framer finishes a 2,800 square foot Phoenix house and the drywall crew hangs board over the walls before the pre-drywall inspection is called. The inspector cannot see the wiring, plumbing rough-in, or framing connections, so the work fails. The crew has to cut out sections of new drywall to expose the work, then re-inspect and re-patch, which can add a week and real labor cost. The City of Phoenix is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , so its inspectors set and enforce this sequence.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You request each inspection yourself. Phoenix gives two ways:

By phone using the Automated Inspection Request System (AIRS) at 602-495-0800. This is a computer-based interactive voice response system. Inspection requests made before 9 p.m. will be scheduled for the next business day.

You can also request online through the city's permitting system by selecting "Cancel or Request Inspections" and entering the key number printed on your permit receipt. For residential permits, the city now schedules through the SHAPE PHX portal, and many home projects qualify for remote video inspections.

If an inspection fails, the contractor gets a red tag that lists the issues to fix. After the fix, you call for a re-inspection, and work can continue once it passes. The final inspection is the gate to occupancy. Phoenix is plain about the result:

The final inspection is conducted when work on the project is completed. This includes all off-site (right-of-way) and on-site (lot) improvements. If the project requires a Certificate of Occupancy, one will be issued. If not, a green tag will signify compliance.

A passing final on a new home triggers the Certificate of Occupancy, and only then are the gas and electric utilities cleared to be turned on permanently. Permits stay valid for two years after issuance, but they expire if work does not start within six months or stops for more than six months.

When this comes into play

This process runs across the entire construction phase of your build:

  • Pour days. You cannot pour a footing or slab until the before-concrete inspection passes, so the inspection schedule drives the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. The pre-drywall inspection covers electrical, plumbing, mechanical, and the water-resistant covering before insulation and drywall. A failure here stops the whole cover-up.
  • Move-in. The final inspection and Certificate of Occupancy are what legally clear you to occupy, and they release permanent utility power. Closing a sale or starting a mortgage often requires the CO in hand.

What this means for you

Treat the inspection calendar as the spine of your build schedule, not an afterthought. Each phase has to pass before the next trade can cover its work, so a missed or failed inspection delays everything downstream. Request inspections early through AIRS at 602-495-0800 or the online system, and have your permit key number and the right inspection type ready. Never let a crew cover wiring, plumbing, or framing before it is inspected, because a red tag means cutting it back open. Do not move family in before the Certificate of Occupancy is issued, because occupying without it is a code violation, and your power may not be permanently on yet.

This entry closes the Phoenix module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Phoenix Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Phoenix Residential Building Permit Requirements . For how this fits the full journey, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The inspection process, scheduling options, red-tag rule, and Certificate of Occupancy step are published by the City of Phoenix: Building Construction Inspections on phoenix.gov . For the residential critical-inspection points and remote inspections, see Residential Inspections .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Inspection steps and CO requirements change, so confirm the current process with Phoenix Planning and Development before you schedule.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix Building Construction Inspections
  • City of Phoenix Residential Inspections
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Phoenix Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: The 2025 Numbers
shortSummary: Phoenix charges new-home impact fees by service area, and most apply only in designated growth areas, not the built-out core. A growth-area single-family home pays a fire fee near $1,165 to $1,833, a police fee near $711 to $1,118, a $348 parks fee, and a $70 library fee, plus water and sewer fees.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Phoenix carries two kinds of city cost: the building permit fee for plan review and inspections, and the development impact fees that pay for the fire, police, parks, streets, water, and sewer capacity your home adds demand to. The big surprise in Phoenix is that most impact fees depend on where you build. They apply in designated growth areas on the edges of the city, and in the built-out core they are zero. This page gives you the real 2025 numbers so you can budget the right amount for your lot.

How Phoenix impact fees work by service area

Phoenix sets impact fees under Ordinance G-7375, approved April 9, 2025 and effective June 23, 2025. The fee schedule splits the city into service areas such as Northwest, Deer Valley, Northeast, Paradise Ridge, Estrella, Laveen, Ahwatukee, and Balance of City. A single-family dwelling in a growth service area is charged like this in the 2025 schedule:

Fire $1,165 to $1,833 per dwelling; Police $711 to $1,118 per dwelling; Parks $348 per dwelling; Libraries $70 per dwelling; Major Arterials (streets) $744 to $2,082 per dwelling; Storm Drainage $133 to $216 per dwelling.

The exact number inside each range depends on which service area your lot sits in. The most important line in that table is the Balance of City column, which is $0 for fire, police, parks, libraries, and storm drainage. In plain terms, if you build inside the established part of Phoenix rather than a designated growth area, those infrastructure impact fees do not apply at all. That is a real cost difference between an infill lot and a new-edge subdivision lot.

The water and wastewater fees apply almost everywhere

Separate from the growth-area fees above, Phoenix charges water, wastewater, and Water Resource Acquisition fees that are based on your meter size, not square footage. A standard single-family home on a meter up to 1 inch pays a Water Resource Acquisition fee of $2,009 in the 2025 schedule, plus water transmission, water treatment, wastewater collection, and wastewater treatment components that vary by service area. Added together, the water and sewer side is usually the largest single chunk of a Phoenix new-home fee bill, often several thousand dollars. Because there are several stacked components and they differ by area, pull the live total for your meter and service area from the official schedule before you budget.

Arizona authorizes all of these development fees under A.R.S. 9-463.05, which requires that they be tied to the actual cost of serving new growth. Confirm the current fee schedule with the city before you budget, because the dollar amounts and the service-area boundaries are reset by ordinance and can change.

When these fees come into play

  • Edge-of-city subdivisions. A lot in Estrella, Laveen, or Deer Valley pays the full stack of growth-area impact fees plus water and sewer. Budget for all of it.
  • Infill and teardown rebuilds. A new home on a Balance-of-City lot skips the fire, police, parks, library, and storm-drainage fees, but still pays water and wastewater. That is a meaningful saving.
  • Timing. Phoenix City Code 29-11 locks the fee schedule in effect at first-permit issuance for later permits in the same platted development for 24 months, so the schedule that applies is the one live when your subdivision's first permit pulls.

What this means for you

First, find out which service area your lot is in, because it decides whether you owe the growth-area impact fees at all. A core infill lot can be thousands of dollars cheaper than an edge lot on impact fees alone. Second, treat the water and wastewater fees as the largest line, and get the meter-size total from the city early. Third, separate the permit fee (plan review and inspections, billed by the Planning and Development Department) from these impact fees, since they are different charges collected at different points.

This entry sits in the Phoenix building-codes module beside Phoenix Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Phoenix Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans must meet. For a metro-wide comparison, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The per-dwelling and per-meter figures above are reproduced from the City of Phoenix 2025 Development Impact Fee Schedule by Land Use, effective June 23, 2025, a public record: Fee Schedules by Land Use on phoenix.gov . The program, the ordinance, and the current schedules are indexed on the city's Impact Fees program page .

These fees change by ordinance, and the service-area splits are technical. Confirm the current schedule and your lot's service area with Phoenix Planning and Development before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix 2025 Development Impact Fee Schedule by Land Use (effective 6-23-2025)
  • City of Phoenix Impact Fees program page
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Phoenix Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and the Review Steps
shortSummary: A new home in Phoenix needs a building permit before any work starts. You submit proposed building plans using the Residential Plan Submittal Plan Review Checklist, plus a Construction Permit/Plan Review Application, two sets of plans and calculations, and a plot plan. Staff reviews for code compliance, then issues the permit after fees are paid.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Phoenix home until the city issues a building permit. The permit is the city's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes, your zoning, and any approved site conditions. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how the package has to be put together, how the city reviews it, and what it takes to get the permit in hand.

What a residential permit requires

Phoenix runs new-home permits through its Planning and Development Department. For a new house, you submit your plans using the city's Residential Plan Submittal Plan Review Checklist so nothing required is missing. A complete single-family submittal includes:

  • A completed Construction Permit / Plan Review Application.
  • Two sets of plans and calculations, including the structural and energy work engineered for Arizona loads.
  • A plot plan showing the lot and the orientation of the house on the lot, with setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out.
  • Energy code compliance paperwork, since Phoenix collects an Energy Code Compliance Certificate fee on residential work.

The city states the submittal step plainly:

The customer submits proposed building plans to the residential permit counter using the Residential Plan Submittal Plan Review Checklist, along with a completed Construction Permit / Plan Review Application. All submittals must include two sets of plans and calculations and a plot plan showing the lot and orientation of the house on the lot.

A package that is incomplete gets handed back before review starts, which costs you days. Note that grading, drainage, or hillside permits, if your lot needs them, must be obtained before the building permit can issue.

How review and issuance work

After you submit, staff checks the package for completeness and, if complete, collects the review fees. Then the formal review begins. Phoenix describes the core of it directly:

Staff reviews the plans for compliance with adopted city codes, design review requirements and any approved site plan conditions of approval. If corrections are required, staff will contact the customer to pick up the plans, make corrections and re-submit for review.

When the plans clear, the city notifies you that they are ready, you pay the permit fees, and the permit issues. So the flow is: submit a checklist-complete package, pay the review fee, pass plan review (with a correction loop if needed), then pay the permit fee and pick up the permit.

Here is a concrete example. Suppose you submit a 3,000 square foot custom home in Phoenix with a plot plan that shows the house 4 feet from the side property line where the zoning requires 5 feet. Staff flags the setback in plan review, contacts you to pick up the plans, and you shift the footprint or seek relief before re-submitting. That single correction round can add a week or two before the permit issues, which is why the plot plan setbacks are worth getting right the first time.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Phoenix build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the checklist-based package with the plot plan and engineered calculations, and the review clock runs through any correction rounds before the permit issues.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, you rely on Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121, which only covers a home you intend to occupy yourself, not one built for sale or rent within a year. That choice decides who signs the application and carries responsibility.
  • Resubmittals. If review finds corrections, you pick up the plans, fix the sheets, and re-submit. Each round adds calendar time, so a clean first submittal is the cheapest path.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item, and split it in your head into the review fee (paid up front) and the permit fee (paid at issuance). Get the plot plan setbacks right the first time, because a setback violation is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Build review time into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant, and confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder, since that decides who signs the forms. If your lot is hillside or needs grading, line up those permits first, because they gate the building permit.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Phoenix module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Phoenix Adopted Building Codes . Once the permit issues, the work gets verified through Phoenix Building Inspections for New Homes . For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The submittal items, required plot plan, and review-to-issuance steps are published by the City of Phoenix: Residential Process Overview on phoenix.gov . For applications, checklists, and contacts, see Residential Projects .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the city's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with Phoenix Planning and Development before you file.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix Residential Process Overview
  • City of Phoenix Residential Projects
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Phoenix Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Where Your New Home Can Sit on the Lot
shortSummary: Phoenix single-family zoning runs from R1-6 on 6,000 square foot lots to RE-43 on one-acre lots, and each district sets its own setbacks. R1-6 allows a 15 foot front and 10 foot side yard at 30 feet tall. RE-43 needs a 40 foot front and 30 foot sides, also capped near 30 feet.
bodyHtml:
Before you draw a floor plan in Phoenix, find your zoning district, because the district sets how far the house must sit from each property line. That open distance is called a setback, and it is the rule that decides where the house can go on the lot. Phoenix names its single-family districts by minimum lot size, from R1-6 (6,000 square feet) up to the Residential Estate RE-43 (43,560 square feet, one acre), and the yards get deeper as the lots get bigger.

What the districts require

Each Phoenix residential district has its own development standards in Chapter 6 of the Zoning Ordinance. The required yards, minimum lot, height, and lot coverage all change by district. Here are the numbers the city publishes:

  • R1-6: 6,000 sq ft min lot, front 15 ft, side 10 ft (1-story) or 15 ft (2-story), rear 15 ft (1-story) or 20 ft (2-story), height 2 stories and 30 ft, lot coverage 50% (plus 10% for an ADU).
  • R1-10: 10,000 sq ft min lot, front 15 ft, side 10 ft (1-story) or 15 ft (2-story), rear 15 ft (1-story) or 20 ft (2-story), height 2 stories and 30 ft, coverage 40% primary (50% total).
  • R1-14: 14,000 sq ft min lot, front 30 ft, interior side 10 ft, street side 15 ft, rear 20 ft, height 2 stories and 30 ft.
  • R1-18: 18,000 sq ft min lot, front 25 ft, side 10 ft, rear 30 ft, height 2 stories and 30 ft, coverage 30% (plus 10% for an ADU).
  • RE-24: 24,000 sq ft min lot, front 30 ft (up to 40 ft on built-up blocks), interior side 10 ft, street side 15 ft, rear 30 ft, height 2 stories and 30 ft, coverage 25%.
  • RE-35: 35,000 sq ft min lot, front 40 ft, side 20 ft, rear 40 ft, height 2 stories and 30 ft, coverage 25%.
  • RE-43: 43,560 sq ft min lot, front 40 ft, side 30 ft, rear 30 ft, height 2 stories and 30 ft, coverage 20%.

The R1-14 ordinance states the height limit in plain terms:

No building shall exceed the height of two stories, not to exceed 30 feet, and no dwelling structure shall be erected to a height of less than one story.

So a small R1-6 lot and a one-acre RE-43 lot share the same 30 foot ceiling, but their yards are far apart. The RE-43 house sits 40 feet off the street with 30 foot sides; the R1-6 house can come within 15 feet of the front and 10 feet of the sides.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks shape the build before design starts:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer subtracts the front, side, and rear setbacks from the lot to find the buildable area, the footprint left for the house. On a 120 by 200 foot RE-43 lot, you lose 40 feet off the front, 30 off the rear, and 30 off each side, leaving a band about 60 feet wide.
  • Corner lots. For example, an R1-14 corner lot carries a 15 foot street-side setback on the second frontage, which can squeeze the pad more than an interior lot with only a 10 foot interior side.
  • Patios and garages. The house, garage, and any casita must stay inside the setback lines. Phoenix also sets garage setbacks, like 18 feet from the back of sidewalk for a front-loaded garage in the R1 districts.

A setback miss on the site plan is one of the most common reasons a Phoenix residential plan gets corrected at review, so the city checks each setback distance before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact district before you buy or design, because the jump from R1-6 to RE-43 changes the front yard from 15 feet to 40 feet and the sides from 10 feet to 30 feet. Watch lot coverage alongside the setbacks, since an R1-6 lot allows up to 50% coverage while an RE-43 lot allows only 20%, which can cap the house size on a big estate lot. Phoenix has been amending its R1-6, R1-8, and R1-10 standards, so pull the current section for your zone. We run the setbacks, coverage cap, and buildable area on the first day of a Phoenix project, because they decide whether a floor plan fits or has to change.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Phoenix module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Phoenix Adopted Building Codes , and the filing steps are in Phoenix Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The per-district setbacks, lot sizes, heights, and coverage limits are published by the City of Phoenix in Chapter 6 of the Zoning Ordinance (Sections 605 through 613): view the Zoning Ordinance contents on phoenix.municipal.codes . The city's planning hub is the Phoenix Planning and Development Department , and lot-coverage basics are explained in the city's Frequently Asked Zoning Questions .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks change with overlays, infill options, and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Phoenix Planning and Development before you design.

Sources

  • City of Phoenix Zoning Ordinance, Chapter 6 (Residential Districts), Sections 605-613
  • City of Phoenix Planning and Development Department
  • City of Phoenix, Frequently Asked Zoning Questions (lot coverage)

title: Pinal County Septic and Well Requirements for a New Home
shortSummary: A new home on rural Pinal County land with no city utilities needs two separate approvals: a septic permit from the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, which ADEQ delegates to the county, and a private well authorized by the state through an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill. On lots of 5 acres or fewer, the county reviews the well.
bodyHtml:
There is usually no city sewer line and no city water main to tie into on rural Pinal County land, so you provide both yourself: a septic system for wastewater and a private well for water. These are two separate approvals from two different offices, and neither comes with the building permit. The county handles septic. The state handles wells. If you are building in or near Casa Grande or Apache Junction on a lot outside city utilities, this is the page for the water and wastewater half of your project. It explains what each approval requires, in what order, and how they tie together.

The septic side: a county permit ADEQ delegates

Wastewater on a rural lot goes to an onsite system, a septic system, permitted by the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division, also called the Septic Program. The state owns the underlying rule. The Arizona Department of Environmental Quality (ADEQ) holds the authority for onsite wastewater permits and delegates it to each of Arizona's 15 counties, which then issue the permits on ADEQ's behalf. Pinal County describes the division's job directly:

Issue permits to construct septic systems (Conventional and Alternative). Inspect septic system construction. Review and endorse well permit applications for referral to the Arizona Department of Water Resources on parcels 5 acres and smaller.

In plain terms, the county runs the whole onsite-wastewater process. It permits and inspects both conventional systems (up to 24,000 gallons per day) and alternative systems (up to 3,000 gallons per day), the type used when ordinary soil conditions will not support a standard gravity leach field. Before a permit issues, your lot needs a site inspection by a qualified inspector, and the soil has to be tested for how fast it absorbs water. That absorption test is the perc test , short for percolation test, where water is timed draining from test holes in the proposed disposal area. The result sets the soil absorption rate, which decides the system type and size.

For example, say your five-acre parcel south of Casa Grande has tight, caliche-heavy soil that drains slowly. The perc test returns a slow rate. A conventional gravity leach field would then have to be very large, or the lot cannot fit one. The county may require an alternative system, which costs more to build and to maintain. This is why the soils work comes first. It can change your system type, your budget, and even where the house sits on the lot.

The well side: an ADWR Notice of Intent to Drill

Water on a rural lot usually comes from a private well, and the well is authorized by the Arizona Department of Water Resources (ADWR), the state agency, not the county. Before any drilling, you file paperwork with the state. The rule is the same statewide:

Prior to drilling a new well, or deepening or modifying an existing well, a person must file a Notice of Intent to Drill with the Department. Authority to deepen an existing well or drill a new well will be valid for one year. A licensed well-drilling contractor must perform the work.

A typical home well is an exempt well, which ADWR defines as a well pumping no more than 35 gallons per minute for domestic use. You file ADWR's Notice of Intent to Drill (form 55-40) with the filing fee. That registers the planned well and gives the licensed driller one year to complete it. Two catches matter for small lots. First, for an exempt domestic well on a parcel of 5 acres or fewer, ADWR requires a site plan showing the well is at least 100 feet from any septic tank or sewer, plus approval by the county health authority. Second, that county review is the step Pinal handles. The Aquifer Protection Division reviews and endorses the well application before it goes to ADWR on parcels 5 acres and smaller. So on a small lot, the county touches both your septic and your well.

When this comes into play

These two approvals govern any rural Pinal County build with no municipal utilities:

  • Buying the land. Before you close on a lot, the perc result and the odds of a good well are part of whether the land is even buildable. A failed perc or a dry-hole risk can sink a deal, so this work belongs in your due diligence, not after.
  • Designing the home. Your septic disposal area, your well, and your house all share the lot and have to keep required separation distances ( setbacks ) from each other and from property lines. ADWR's own 100-foot well-to-septic rule on small parcels is one of those distances, so the well and the leach field cannot crowd each other.
  • Sequencing the build. The county septic permit and the ADWR well authorization run in parallel with your city or county building permit, and all three appear on the same site plan.

What this means for you

Treat septic and well as their own projects with their own timelines and budgets, separate from the house. Start the perc test early, ideally during due diligence before you buy, because a slow or failing result changes your system type and cost and can decide whether the lot works at all. File the Notice of Intent to Drill with ADWR and hire a licensed driller, and remember the authority is good for one year. If your parcel is 5 acres or smaller, expect the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division to review the well application and confirm the 100-foot setback before ADWR signs off. Put the septic field, the well, and the house on one site plan so all the separation distances work together.

This entry covers the rural-utility half of the Pinal County builds. Because Casa Grande and Apache Junction are their own permit offices, the codes your home is built to are in Casa Grande Adopted Building Codes and Apache Junction Adopted Building Codes . The Maricopa-side equivalent of this page is Maricopa County Septic and Well Requirements , which covers a different county office. For the full picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona and how to buy land to build a house in Arizona .

Source

The septic program and the small-parcel well review are published by the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division: Wells & Septic on pinal.gov . The state delegation of onsite-wastewater permitting to the counties is published by ADEQ's Onsite Wastewater Treatment Program . The well requirements are published by the state at Well Drilling in Arizona .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the agencies' current rules. Septic permits, well filings, and fees change, so confirm the live requirements with the Pinal County Aquifer Protection Division and the Arizona Department of Water Resources before you rely on them.

  • Pinal County Wells & Septic (Aquifer Protection Division)

title: Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes: Which I-Code Editions Apply to Your Build
shortSummary: Scottsdale builds to the 2021 International Residential Code, 2021 International Building, Plumbing, Mechanical, Fuel Gas, and Energy codes, plus the 2020 National Electrical Code. The building and fire codes took effect January 1, 2023, with local City Council amendments layered on top of each model code.
bodyHtml:
Your house in Scottsdale is checked against a stack of national model codes that the City Council voted to adopt, then edited for local conditions. Knowing the exact edition matters, because a 2018 detail and a 2021 detail can call for different framing, wiring, or energy specs. This page lists the codes Scottsdale actually enforces today, when they took effect, and where the local edits live.

Which codes Scottsdale enforces

Scottsdale adopted the 2021 family of International Codes (often called I-codes) for almost everything, plus the 2020 electrical code. Here is the current list:

  • 2021 International Residential Code (IRC): one- and two-family homes and townhouses. This is the main code for a custom house.
  • 2021 International Building Code (IBC): larger and commercial structures.
  • 2021 International Energy Conservation Code (IECC): insulation, windows, duct sealing, and HVAC efficiency.
  • 2021 International Plumbing Code (IPC): water supply, drains, and fixtures.
  • 2021 International Mechanical Code (IMC): heating, cooling, and ventilation.
  • 2021 International Fuel Gas Code (IFGC): gas piping and gas appliances.
  • 2021 International Fire Code (IFC): fire safety, sprinklers, and access for firefighting.
  • 2020 National Electrical Code (NEC): all wiring and electrical work.

The IRC is the one a single-family builder lives in day to day. The City of Scottsdale is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts, amends, and enforces these codes, so its building officials, not the state, have the final word inside city limits.

What the city says

The city is direct about why the raw model codes do not stand alone:

Building codes are adopted and amended by City Council to account for local conditions and practices.

Scottsdale's building and fire codes took effect January 1, 2023. The energy and green code pieces, including the 2021 IRC and IECC local amendments, took effect January 7, 2023, and the green construction code amendments became mandatory July 1, 2023. Each model code has its own separate amendment document. So the rule you build to is the published 2021 edition as changed by Scottsdale's amendment PDF for that code.

When this comes into play

The edition controls real decisions from day one of design:

  • Your energy compliance path (insulation R-values, duct leakage testing, window U-factors) is set by the 2021 IECC as amended, not an older energy code. A plan drawn to a 2018 energy spec can get bounced in review.
  • Outlet spacing, arc-fault and ground-fault protection, and panel sizing follow the 2020 NEC. An electrician working off a 2017 code book may miss newer required protections.
  • Anything the model code says, a Scottsdale amendment can override. The amendment PDFs are the tie-breaker, so they get read alongside the base code.

Here is a concrete example. Say you submit a custom home plan in Scottsdale in spring 2026. The plan reviewer checks your wall insulation against the 2021 IECC as amended, your wiring against the 2020 NEC, and your framing against the 2021 IRC. If your energy sheet was copied from an older 2018 project, the duct-leakage and insulation numbers may not match, and the plan comes back with corrections before a permit issues.

If you pulled a permit before the 2023 effective dates, your project may still run under the prior edition. Edition is tied to when the permit was issued, not the calendar today.

What this means for you

Hand your designer, structural engineer, and trades one clear instruction: build to Scottsdale's 2021 I-codes and the 2020 NEC, with the city amendments applied. That single sentence prevents the most common and expensive plan-review correction, which is a sheet drawn to the wrong edition. When you compare bids, confirm each contractor is quoting current-code work, because retrofitting to code after a failed inspection costs far more than doing it right the first time.

This entry is the anchor for the rest of the Scottsdale building-codes module. From here, see Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements for how these codes get checked during plan review, and Scottsdale Building Inspections for New Homes for how they get verified in the field. For the bigger picture, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The adopted codes, effective dates, and amendment documents are published by the City of Scottsdale: Building Code Information on scottsdaleaz.gov . Arizona's broader adoption picture is tracked by the code authors at ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Editions and amendments change, so confirm the current adopted code and any project-specific amendment with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you rely on a specific edition.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Building Code Information
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona
metaDescription: Scottsdale uses the 2021 IRC, IBC, IECC, IPC, IMC, IFGC and the 2020 NEC, effective 2023, plus local amendments. Here is the exact list for your build.
