title: Scottsdale Building Inspections for New Homes: The Sequence Through Final and CO
shortSummary: Scottsdale inspects a new home in stages, from foundation to framing to final, and each stage must pass before the next part of the build is covered up. After the final inspection passes with no code violations, the city issues a Certificate of Occupancy that lets you legally move in.
bodyHtml:
A Scottsdale home is inspected in a fixed order while it is being built, and each phase has to pass before the work is buried behind concrete, drywall, or stucco. The whole sequence ends with a final inspection that unlocks your Certificate of Occupancy, the document that legally lets you move in. This page lays out the order, how to schedule each one, and how the final ties to the CO.

The inspection sequence

Inspections follow the build from the ground up. The city calls each one at a point where the work is still visible. The typical order for a new house:

  1. Foundation. When the footing excavation is done and steel reinforcement is set, but before any concrete is poured.
  2. Under-slab. When the plumbing, mechanical, and electrical lines under the floor are in, before the slab is poured over them.
  3. Roof deck. When sheathing and tie-downs are complete.
  4. Framing. When walls, fire blocking, draft stops, and the rough plumbing, electrical, and mechanical are all in place, before anything gets covered.
  5. Insulation. Before the drywall goes up.
  6. Lath or gypsum board. Before plastering or stucco is applied.
  7. Final. When the house is complete and all fixtures are set, before anyone occupies it.

The order matters because an inspector cannot approve what is already hidden. For example, suppose a framer finishes a 2,800 square foot Scottsdale house and the drywall crew hangs board over the walls before the framing inspection is called. The inspector cannot see the wiring, plumbing rough-in, or fire blocking, so the work fails. The crew has to cut out sections of new drywall to expose the framing, then re-inspect and re-patch, which can add a week and real labor cost to the schedule. The City of Scottsdale is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) , so its inspectors set and enforce this sequence.

How scheduling and the final tie to your CO

You schedule inspections yourself through the city. Projects started in the new Scottsdale SPUR portal schedule there: pick the permit, open the Inspections tab, choose the inspection type, and request a date. On inspection day you can also reach your assigned inspector by phone in an early-morning window, around 6 to 6:30 a.m. in summer or 7 to 7:30 a.m. in winter.

The final inspection is the gate to occupancy. The city is plain about what the result means:

Upon passing a Final Inspection, a Certificate of Occupancy (C of O) is issued, allowing for the business to begin operation or a homeowner to move in.

If you need to move in before every last item is finished, Scottsdale can issue a Temporary Certificate of Occupancy (TCO) after a special inspection. The TCO requires a refundable cash deposit equal to the total of your permit fees or two thousand five hundred dollars, whichever is greater, held until the full CO issues. The CO rules live in Scottsdale Revised Code, Chapter 31, Article VII.

When this comes into play

This process runs across the entire construction phase of your build:

  • Pour days. You cannot pour a footing or slab until the foundation or under-slab inspection passes, so the inspection schedule drives the concrete schedule.
  • Close-in. Framing, electrical, plumbing, and mechanical all get inspected at the framing stage before insulation and drywall. A failed framing inspection stops the whole cover-up.
  • Move-in. The final inspection, or a TCO with its deposit, is what legally clears you to occupy. Closing a sale or starting a mortgage often requires the CO in hand.

What this means for you

Treat the inspection calendar as the spine of your build schedule, not an afterthought. Each phase has to pass before the next trade can cover its work, so a missed or failed inspection delays everything downstream. Schedule early through SPUR, and have your permit number and the right inspection type ready. If your timeline is tight near the end, ask about a Temporary Certificate of Occupancy, but budget for the refundable deposit it requires. Do not move furniture or family in before the CO or TCO is issued, because occupying without it is a code violation.

This entry closes the Scottsdale module loop. The codes your work is inspected against are in Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes , and the permit that authorizes the build is covered in Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements . For how this fits the full journey, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The inspection sequence, scheduling, and Certificate of Occupancy rules are published by the City of Scottsdale: Certificate of Occupancy on scottsdaleaz.gov . For the full list of inspection types and contacts, see Inspection Services .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Inspection steps and CO requirements change, so confirm the current process with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you schedule.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Certificate of Occupancy
  • City of Scottsdale Inspection Services
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IRC inspection requirements)

title: Scottsdale Building Permit and Impact Fees for a New Home: What You Actually Pay
shortSummary: Scottsdale charges new-home permit fees by square foot, not by valuation. A custom home pays a $195 base fee, $0.94 per square foot of air-conditioned area, and $0.54 per square foot of covered non-A/C area, plus a $379 Certificate of Occupancy. Water and sewer development fees are billed separately by meter size.
bodyHtml:
A new home in Scottsdale comes with two separate buckets of city money. First, building permit fees pay for plan review and inspections. Second, development fees (often called impact fees) pay for the water, sewer, and other infrastructure your home will use. This page gives you the real FY2025-26 numbers from the city's official schedule, so you can put a line item in your budget instead of guessing.

How Scottsdale calculates the permit fee

Scottsdale prices a new-home permit by square footage, not by construction value. That is different from most Arizona cities, which run permit fees off a valuation table. The city's FY2025-26 Single Family Residential schedule, adopted under Resolution 13391 and effective July 1, 2025, charges a custom home like this:

Single Family Custom - Base fee $195. Livable area with A/C $0.94 sq. ft. Covered area (non A/C) $0.54 sq. ft. Certificate of Occupancy $379. GIS fee $363. Lowest Floor Certificate Review $237.

So the base fee is a flat $195. The living-area rate is $0.94 for every square foot that is air conditioned. The covered-area rate is $0.54 for every square foot of garage, covered patio, or other roofed space that is not air conditioned. On top of that you pay a one-time Certificate of Occupancy charge of $379. That is the document the city issues when the home is approved to live in. You also pay a GIS fee of $363 for mapping, and a $237 floor-certificate review where flood elevation applies.

Take a real example. Say you build a 3,000 square foot air-conditioned home with a 600 square foot garage and a 400 square foot covered patio. That is 1,000 square feet of non-A/C space. The math is: $195 base, plus 3,000 x $0.94 = $2,820, plus 1,000 x $0.54 = $540, plus $379 and $363. That comes to about $4,297 in building-permit charges. Trade permits for electrical, plumbing, mechanical, pool, and walls add to that, because the schedule states it is not all-inclusive.

The development (impact) fees are billed separately

The permit fee above does not include impact fees . Those are charges a city collects on new construction to pay for the public infrastructure that growth requires. Arizona lets cities charge them under A.R.S. 9-463.05. In Scottsdale they are published as Water and Wastewater Development Fees in a separate 2025 report. They are charged by meter size, not square footage. A standard single-family home on a typical residential meter pays a water development fee plus a wastewater (sewer) development fee. These are usually the largest city charge on the whole project, often several thousand dollars each. They are set in their own report that updates each year, so pull the live figure for your meter size from the city's water rates-and-fees page.

This is the high-cost wrinkle people miss. The square-foot permit fee is predictable and modest. The water and wastewater development fees can dwarf it. Confirm the current fee schedule with the city before you budget. Both the permit rates and the development fees are reset by city resolution and can change every fiscal year.

When these fees come into play

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer submits through the Scottsdale SPUR portal, and the city invoices the square-foot permit fee at application. Development fees are typically collected later, tied to your water meter and connection.
  • Custom vs. standard plan. The schedule has separate lines for a Single Family Custom home and a Single Family Standard Plan (a repeated builder plan), and the standard plan adds a 15% admin site-review fee. Make sure your home is billed on the right line.
  • Garages and covered patios. Because covered non-A/C area is billed at $0.54 per square foot, a big garage or deep covered patio raises the permit fee. It is a real number, not a rounding error, on a large home.

What this means for you

Budget the permit fee and the development fees as two different things, because they hit at different times and the development fees are far larger. For the permit, get an accurate square-footage count split between A/C living area and covered non-A/C area, since that split drives the bill. For the development fees, ask the city for the current water and wastewater amounts for your meter size, since those reset yearly.

This entry sits in the Scottsdale building-codes module alongside Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements , which covers what you submit, and Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes , which covers the standards your plans are checked against. For how these costs compare across the metro, see how much a building permit costs in Arizona .

Source

The per-square-foot rates and flat charges above are reproduced from the City of Scottsdale's official FY2025-26 Single Family Residential permit fee schedule (Resolution 13391), a public record: Permit Fee Schedule, SFR on scottsdaleaz.gov . The separate water and wastewater development fees are published in the 2025 Water and Wastewater Development Fees Report .

These fees change by city resolution, often every fiscal year. Confirm the current schedule with Scottsdale Planning and Development and the Water Department before you rely on any number here for a budget.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Permit Fee Schedule, Single Family Residential (FY2025-26, Resolution 13391)
  • City of Scottsdale 2025 Water and Wastewater Development Fees Report
  • City of Scottsdale Plan Review Services
  • Arizona Revised Statutes 9-463.05 (Development fees)

title: Scottsdale Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESL): Desert Preservation Limits on Your Lot
shortSummary: Scottsdale's Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESL) is a zoning overlay covering 134 square miles of north and east Scottsdale desert and mountains. It forces each lot to permanently preserve a share of land as Natural Area Open Space, from about 15% on flat lower desert up to 80% on steep hillside, which shrinks where you can build a custom home.
bodyHtml:
If your Scottsdale lot is north or east of the Central Arizona Project canal, the Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance (ESL) likely controls how much of it you can touch. The ESL is a zoning overlay, a layer of extra rules on top of your base zoning, that protects desert and mountain land by forcing each property to keep a set share of itself in natural open space. On a custom-home lot, that share is land you cannot build on, pave, or grade, so it shapes the house before you draw a single wall. This page explains what the ESL is, who it hits, and what it means for a build.

What the ESL is and what it protects

The ESL covers 134 square miles of north and east Scottsdale desert and mountain terrain. The city is direct about why it exists:

The intent and purpose of the ESL is to identify and protect environmentally sensitive lands in the City and to promote public health and safety by managing development on these lands.

The core tool is Natural Area Open Space (NAOS), land that has to be left in its natural state forever. The ordinance requires that a percentage of each property be permanently preserved as NAOS, and the amount is not a flat number. It rises with two things: the landform (Lower Desert, Upper Desert, or Hillside) and the land slope. The required NAOS runs from about 15 percent on flat lower desert up to as much as 80 percent on steep hillside. The steeper and more sensitive the ground, the more of it you must leave alone. The ordinance also protects specific features like desert washes, vegetation, and mountain ridgelines from disturbance.

The City of Scottsdale is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that adopts and enforces the ESL, so its Planning and Development staff review every north-Scottsdale lot against it.

Who it hits and how it limits a build

The ESL bites hardest on north Scottsdale custom-home lots, the large desert and foothill parcels where most build-on-your-lot projects happen. Here is what it changes:

  • Your buildable area shrinks. NAOS is carved out of the lot first, so your house, driveway, pool, and graded yard all have to fit in what is left. On a hillside parcel that can mean over half the lot is off-limits.
  • The desert you keep is mapped and protected. NAOS is recorded, often by easement, and you cannot later clear it for an addition or a sport court. It is permanent.
  • It stacks with normal limits. ESL preservation sits on top of your setbacks (the required distance from each property line) and your lot coverage (the share of the lot the building footprint may cover). NAOS, setbacks, and coverage together define the real envelope.

For example, suppose you buy a one-acre upper-desert lot on a 10 to 15 percent slope in north Scottsdale and the ESL sets your NAOS at 45 percent. Nearly half an acre has to stay untouched desert. Your 4,000 square foot house, garage, driveway, and pool all have to fit on the remaining portion, inside your setbacks and coverage limit. A floor plan drawn as if the whole acre were buildable will not fit, and the design comes back to be reworked.

What this means for you

Check the ESL before you buy a north Scottsdale lot, not after. Two same-size lots can have very different buildable areas once NAOS is applied, so the asking price per acre can be misleading. Ask the seller or the city for the lot's landform and slope so you can estimate the NAOS percentage. You can look up the parcel on the Maricopa County Assessor parcel viewer to get started. Then design the house to the land you can actually use. Build the NAOS line into your site plan from the first sketch, because moving a footprint to clear preserved desert is one of the more expensive corrections in a Scottsdale review. If the lot is steep enough, the city's Foothills Overlay can add further hillside design rules on top of the ESL, so confirm which overlays apply.

This entry is a city-special topic in the Scottsdale module. The codes your structure is built to are in Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes , and what you file for the permit is in Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the lot-buying side, see how to buy land to build a house in Arizona , and for the code picture, what building codes apply in Scottsdale .

Source

The ESL purpose, the NAOS requirement, and the covered area are published by the City of Scottsdale: Environmentally Sensitive Lands Overlay on scottsdaleaz.gov . The related hillside rules are at Foothills Overlay , and the full code index is at Codes and Ordinances .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Overlay boundaries and NAOS percentages depend on the specific parcel, so confirm your lot's requirement with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you rely on a number.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Environmentally Sensitive Lands Overlay (ESL)
  • City of Scottsdale Foothills Overlay
  • City of Scottsdale Codes and Ordinances
  • Maricopa County Assessor Parcel Viewer (parcel landform and slope lookup)

title: Scottsdale Green Building Program: The Voluntary Green Path for New Homes
shortSummary: Scottsdale's Green Building Program is a voluntary checklist that lets a new single-family home earn a Scottsdale Green Building designation for energy, water, and material efficiency. It is not required for a custom home. The mandatory International Green Construction Code (IgCC) that Scottsdale adopted in 2023 applies only to commercial and multifamily projects, not single-family houses.
bodyHtml:
Building a custom home in Scottsdale does not force you into a green building rating, but the city gives you a clear, voluntary path to earn one. Scottsdale's Green Building Program is a checklist a single-family home can follow to get a Scottsdale Green Building designation for being more energy, water, and material efficient than code requires. People often confuse this with Scottsdale's mandatory green construction code, which is a different thing that does not apply to houses. This page sorts out which is which, who each one hits, and what it means for your build.

Two different green rules, and which one applies to a house

Scottsdale runs two separate green tracks, and the difference matters for a custom-home builder.

The first is the voluntary Green Building Program for residential projects. A new home is not required to join. If you opt in, your design follows the program's One- and Two-Family Residential Green Building Checklist. The city verifies it, and the home earns a Green Building designation. The program is one of the oldest in the country. The city keeps a directory of participating architects, designers, and builders.

The second is the mandatory International Green Construction Code (IgCC), which sounds like it would cover houses but does not. Scottsdale adopted it as a mandatory code, and the city is clear about which buildings it touches:

The IgCC makes it easier for developers of commercial and multi-family projects to be green designated.

That mandatory IgCC took effect July 1, 2023. It applies to new commercial and multifamily buildings, not single-family detached homes. So a custom house is never required to meet the IgCC. The City of Scottsdale is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) that runs both tracks. Its Planning and Development staff administer the voluntary program and enforce the mandatory code.

What the voluntary program actually involves

For a new home, the green choice is yours. It sits on top of the energy rules you already have to meet. Every Scottsdale house must comply with the mandatory energy code, the 2021 IECC as amended. That code already sets insulation R-values (a measure of how well insulation resists heat flow), window performance, and duct sealing. The voluntary program goes beyond that baseline. It scores choices like higher-efficiency HVAC, water-saving fixtures, solar readiness, shade and orientation for the desert sun, and lower-impact materials.

The program is built on the standard residential code. It does not replace the 2021 IRC your house is framed and wired to. It is an added designation, not a separate code path. So joining changes your specs and your paperwork, not the core engineering of the home.

When this comes into play and what it means for you

The voluntary program is a design-phase decision, so make the call early:

  • If you want the designation, tell your designer at the start. Hitting green targets is far cheaper when insulation, glazing, HVAC, and orientation are planned in, rather than retrofitted late.
  • If you do not, you are not penalized. Your home still has to meet the mandatory 2021 IECC energy code, but nothing forces the extra green checklist on a single-family build.
  • Do not let the IgCC scare you. For example, if a contractor tells you a new Scottsdale house must meet the International Green Construction Code, that is wrong for a single-family home. The IgCC is a commercial and multifamily code. Your house follows the IRC and the IECC, plus the green program only if you choose it.

For a Scottsdale build, the practical takeaway is simple. Green is optional for your house, energy efficiency under the IECC is not, and the scary-sounding IgCC does not apply to you. If resale value or lower utility bills matter to you in the desert, the voluntary program is a credible, city-backed way to document a more efficient home.

This entry is a city-special topic in the Scottsdale module. The full mandatory code list, including the energy code your house must meet, is in Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes . What you file for the permit is in Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the plain-language code overview, see what building codes apply in Scottsdale , and for the full journey, the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The voluntary residential program and its checklist are published by the City of Scottsdale: Green Building Program on scottsdaleaz.gov . The mandatory IgCC and its commercial and multifamily scope are at Green Codes , and the full adopted-code list is at Building Code Information .

This page is general information, not a code interpretation. Program details and adopted codes change, so confirm the current requirements with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you rely on them.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Green Building Program
  • City of Scottsdale Green Codes (IgCC)
  • City of Scottsdale Building Code Information
  • ICC Code Adoptions for Arizona (IECC/IgCC editions)

title: Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements: What You File and What It Costs
shortSummary: A new home in Scottsdale needs a building permit before any work starts. You apply through the Scottsdale SPUR portal with a site plan, floor plans, and engineered construction documents, pay plan-review fees based on square footage, and wait for staff review and approval before the permit issues.
bodyHtml:
You cannot pour a footing or frame a wall on a new Scottsdale home until the city issues a building permit. The permit is the city's sign-off that your plans meet the adopted codes and your lot's zoning. This page walks the real steps: what you submit, how the file has to be formatted, how the city calculates your fee, and what the review timeline looks like.

What a residential permit requires

Scottsdale runs permits through an online portal called Scottsdale SPUR. You create an account, upload your plan set, and pay fees there. A complete single-family submittal generally includes:

  • A site plan showing the house on the lot, with setbacks (the required distance from each property line) called out.
  • Floor plans, one sheet per floor level, drawn at a minimum 1/8 inch scale.
  • Structural and construction documents engineered for Arizona loads.
  • Energy compliance paperwork showing the design meets the 2021 IECC as amended.
  • An Owner Builder Form when the owner, not a licensed contractor, is pulling the permit.

The city is specific about file format:

PDF file format is the standard for submittal and reviewing plans.

Individual upload files must stay under 100 MB, RGB color is accepted, and most documents print at 8.5 by 11 or 11 by 17 inches. Plans that do not follow the format get kicked back before review even starts, which costs you days.

How fees and timeline work

Plan-review fees are due at application, and the city does not start formal review until you pay. The city states the trigger plainly:

Fees are to be paid at the time of application for plan review, and applications will not be processed until payment is received.

For a house, Scottsdale charges on square footage, and it counts both the air-conditioned living area and the non-air-conditioned area such as a garage or covered patio. So a 3,000 square foot home with a 600 square foot garage and a 400 square foot covered patio is billed on all 4,000 square feet, not just the climate-controlled space.

The flow is: submit through SPUR, the city checks the package for completeness, then sends an invoice and payment link. After you pay, staff begins the formal plan review. Single-family homes typically go straight to permit review under administrative approval, so they skip the public hearings that some larger projects need.

When this comes into play

This process governs the front end of every Scottsdale build:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer assembles the SPUR package, you pay the square-footage-based fee, and the clock starts after payment, not after upload.
  • Owner-builder. If you pull the permit yourself instead of using a licensed contractor, you must file the Owner Builder Form at submittal, before the permit can issue. Arizona's owner-builder exemption under A.R.S. 32-1121 only covers a home you intend to occupy yourself, not one built for sale or rent within a year, so the form ties your permit to that legal limit.
  • Resubmittals. If review finds corrections, you fix the sheets and re-upload through the same portal. Each round adds calendar time, so a clean first submittal is the cheapest path.

What this means for you

Budget for the permit as a real line item and ask early how the city will count your square footage, since garages and covered patios push the fee up. Get the site plan setbacks right the first time, because a setback violation is one of the most common reasons a residential plan gets corrected. Build review time into your schedule rather than assuming the permit is instant, and confirm whether you or your contractor is the permit holder, since that decides who signs the forms and carries responsibility.

This entry sits between the codes and the field work in the Scottsdale module. The standards your plans are checked against are listed in Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes . Once the permit issues, the work gets verified through Scottsdale Building Inspections for New Homes . For the full sequence of building a home, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The submittal rules, file-format standards, and fee-payment trigger are published by the City of Scottsdale: Plan Submittal Requirements on scottsdaleaz.gov . For the broader review process and contacts, see Plan Review Services .

This page is general information, not a substitute for the city's current checklist. Submittal items, fees, and timelines change, so confirm the live requirements with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you file.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Plan Submittal Requirements
  • City of Scottsdale Plan Review Services
  • A.R.S. 32-1121 (Persons not required to be licensed as contractors)

title: Scottsdale Residential Zoning and Setbacks: Where Your New Home Can Sit on the Lot
shortSummary: Scottsdale single-family zoning runs from R1-7 on small lots to R1-190 on multi-acre lots, and each district sets its own setbacks. R1-7 needs a 20 foot front, 5 foot side, and 25 foot rear yard. R1-43 needs 40, 20, and 35 feet, all under a 30 foot height cap.
bodyHtml:
Before you draw a floor plan in Scottsdale, you need to know your zoning district, because the district sets how far the house must sit from every property line. That open distance is called a setback, and it is the single rule that decides where the house can go on the lot. Scottsdale's single-family districts are named for their minimum lot size, from R1-7 (7,000 square feet) up to R1-190 (190,000 square feet), and the setbacks get deeper as the lots get bigger.

What the districts require

Each Scottsdale single-family district has its own property development standards section in Article V of the Zoning Ordinance. The required yards, the minimum lot, and the height ceiling all change by district. Here are the numbers the city publishes for the common districts:

  • R1-7: 7,000 sq ft minimum lot, front yard 20 ft, side yard 5 ft each side, rear yard 25 ft, height 30 ft.
  • R1-18: 18,000 sq ft minimum lot, front yard 35 ft, side yard 10 ft, rear yard 30 ft, height 30 ft.
  • R1-35: 35,000 sq ft minimum lot, front yard 40 ft, side yard 15 ft, rear yard 35 ft, height 30 ft.
  • R1-43: 43,560 sq ft minimum lot, front yard 40 ft, side yard 20 ft, rear yard 35 ft, height 30 ft.
  • R1-190: 190,000 sq ft minimum lot, front yard 60 ft, side yard 30 ft, rear yard 60 ft, height 30 ft.

The ordinance states the building-height limit the same way in each district:

No building shall exceed thirty (30) feet in height, except as otherwise provided in Article VII.

So a one-acre R1-43 lot and a small R1-7 lot share the same 30 foot height cap, but their yards are very different. The R1-43 house sits 40 feet back from the street; the R1-7 house can come within 20 feet.

When setbacks come into play

Setbacks shape the build from day one, before design starts:

  • Build-on-your-lot. Your designer subtracts the front, side, and rear setbacks from the lot to find the buildable area, the footprint left for the house. For example, a 100 by 200 foot R1-43 lot loses 40 feet off the front, 35 off the rear, and 20 off each side, leaving a buildable band roughly 60 feet wide and 125 feet deep.
  • Corner lots. In Scottsdale, a corner lot has a front yard on each street frontage, which can squeeze the buildable area more than a standard interior lot.
  • Patios and garages. The main house, garage, and any casita must stay inside the setback lines. Some features like eaves and certain front porches have separate allowances under Article VII.

A setback violation is one of the most common reasons a Scottsdale residential plan gets corrected at review, which is why the city checks the setback distances on the site plan before the permit issues.

What this means for you

Confirm your exact zoning district before you buy or design, because the difference between R1-7 and R1-43 changes the front yard from 20 feet to 40 feet and reshapes the whole footprint. Scottsdale's single-family height limit is a flat 30 feet, so your roofline is capped the same in every R1 district. Watch lot coverage and the buildable area together, since deep setbacks on a big lot can still leave a narrow building pad. We run the setbacks and buildable area on the first day of a Scottsdale project, because they decide whether a floor plan fits or has to change.

This entry pairs with the rest of the Scottsdale module. The codes your plans are checked against are in Scottsdale Adopted Building Codes , and the filing process is in Scottsdale Residential Building Permit Requirements . For the full build sequence, see the steps to building a custom home in Arizona .

Source

The per-district setbacks, lot sizes, and the 30 foot height limit are published by the City of Scottsdale in Article V of the Zoning Ordinance: View the District Regulations on municode.com . The district list and minimum lot sizes are set in Article IV, Table 4.100.A , and the city's zoning hub is at Scottsdale Zoning Ordinance Resources .

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Setbacks change with overlays, hillside rules, and amendments, so confirm your lot's district and standards with Scottsdale Planning and Development before you design.

Sources

  • City of Scottsdale Zoning Ordinance, Article V (District Regulations)
  • City of Scottsdale Zoning Ordinance Resources
  • City of Scottsdale Single-Family Residential Districts, Article IV (Table 4.100.A)

title: IRC E3601 & E3602: Electrical Service & Panel
shortSummary: IRC E3601 and E3602 govern a home's electrical service: the power feed and main panel. A single-family home needs a service rated at least 100 amps, fed by conductors sized to the load, with a clear 36-inch-deep working space kept open in front of the panel.
bodyHtml:
The electrical service is the power feed from the utility into your main panel, and Sections E3601 and E3602 of the International Residential Code (IRC) set how big it has to be and how it is installed. These sections decide the amperage rating, where the main disconnect goes, and how much open space has to stay in front of the panel so it can be worked on safely. This page covers the service size and panel rules that drive electrical design on a custom home.

What E3601 and E3602 require

E3601 covers the service: the conductors and equipment that bring utility power to the house. The headline number is the minimum rating. A one-family dwelling must have a service with a rating of not less than 100 amperes, three-wire. The service conductors then have to be sized to the home's calculated load, so a large house with electric heat, a pool, and EV charging needs a bigger service even though 100 amps is the floor.

E3602 covers the service equipment: the main panel and disconnect. The service disconnect has to be readily accessible, either outside or inside near the point where the conductors enter. Here is the service-rating requirement:

Services shall have a rating of not less than 100 amperes, 3-wire.

A second rule trips up a lot of installs: the working space. A clear area at least 36 inches deep, at least 30 inches wide (or the panel width if wider), and 6.5 feet tall must stay open in front of the panel, with room for the door to swing 90 degrees. You cannot bury a panel behind shelving or a water heater.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

In the Valley, 100 amps is rarely enough for a custom home. Air conditioning is the swing factor: in Phoenix and Scottsdale the AC load is large and runs hard, so even a modest custom home usually lands on 200 amps, and large or all-electric homes go to 320 or 400 amps, often with a meter-main and subpanels. A licensed electrician runs a load calculation under the IRC's Chapter 36 method to size it, instead of guessing.

For example, a 4,000-square-foot Scottsdale home with two AC units, a pool, a casita, and EV charging will typically need a 320- or 400-amp service, and the panel location has to keep that 36-inch working clearance in a garage or utility wall, often inside conditioned space so the gear is not baking in 115-degree heat. Plan the panel wall early; relocating a panel after rough-in to recover clearance is costly. For sizing, see what size electrical service does a custom home need in Arizona .

Edition note

The IRC's electrical chapters track the National Electrical Code (NEC). The 100-amp service minimum and the 36-inch working-space depth are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. What changes between editions is which NEC cycle the requirements mirror, and a few cities adopt the standalone NEC separately from the IRC, so the adopted electrical code can differ from the building-code edition. The Arizona building code editions by city page lists the electrical code for each jurisdiction.

How it connects

The service E3601 sizes is what feeds everything else, including any rooftop solar tie-in under R324 and the HVAC load behind M1401 HVAC sizing . Enforcement runs through your local AHJ and its electrical inspector. If you are weighing panel upgrades, see what is a smart electrical panel and is it worth it in a custom home .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC E3601 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short rating clause is excerpted; the explanation is ours.

Sources

  • 2021 IRC Section E3601 Services (ICC Digital Codes)
  • Chapter 36 Services, 2021 IRC (UpCodes)
  • City of Phoenix Building Construction Codes (adopting authority)

title: IRC M1401 & Manual J: HVAC Sizing
shortSummary: IRC M1401.3 requires heating and cooling equipment to be sized from a real load calculation, not a square-foot guess. The load is figured with ACCA Manual J, and the equipment is then picked with Manual S, so the system matches the actual house.
bodyHtml:
Section M1401.3 of the International Residential Code (IRC) bans the oldest shortcut in HVAC: picking an air conditioner by square footage. It requires the equipment to be sized from an actual load calculation of your specific house. In Arizona, where the system runs against 110-degree heat for months, that calculation is the difference between a house that cools evenly and one that short-cycles, runs humid, and wears out early.

What M1401.3 requires

The code names two ACCA (Air Conditioning Contractors of America) methods and requires both steps. First you calculate the load with Manual J , then you select the equipment with Manual S. Here is the rule:

Heating and cooling equipment and appliances shall be sized in accordance with ACCA Manual S based on building loads calculated in accordance with ACCA Manual J.

Manual J adds up how much heating and cooling the house actually needs. It looks at insulation levels, window area and orientation, air leakage, ceiling height, and the local design temperature, room by room. Manual S then takes that load and picks real equipment from the maker's performance data. That includes how much sensible (temperature) and latent (humidity) capacity the unit puts out. The point is to match the equipment to the load, not to round up to the nearest big number.

Why bigger is not better

For example, a 3,000-square-foot home in Scottsdale built tight, with good insulation and modern windows, might calculate to a 4-ton cooling load, while the old rule-of-thumb would have slapped in a 5-ton unit. An oversized air conditioner cools the air fast, then shuts off before it has run long enough to pull moisture out, so the house feels cold and clammy and the compressor short-cycles. That cycling wastes energy and shortens equipment life. Right-sizing through Manual J avoids it.

The Arizona cooling load is driven by the sun. Roof and attic heat, west-facing glass, and a design temperature near 110 degrees all push the number up. That is why this calculation pairs so tightly with the building envelope. A higher ceiling R-value and better windows lower the Manual J load. That lets you install a smaller, more efficient unit. The efficiency of that unit is rated by its SEER number, so the load calc and the SEER rating work together: size it right, then make it efficient.

What inspectors and lenders look for

Many Arizona jurisdictions ask for the Manual J report at permit, and energy programs and some construction lenders want it too. The HVAC contractor or a mechanical designer produces it from the plans. If the design changes (you add west glass or vault a ceiling), the load changes, so the calculation should reflect the final drawings, not an early version.

Edition note

The Manual J and Manual S requirement is consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition moved and renumbered the provision and tightened the documentation expectations, but the core rule (calculate the load with Manual J, select with Manual S) is the same. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

HVAC sizing depends on the building envelope set by N1102 building thermal envelope and the attic conditions from R806 roof ventilation . For how the home's shell affects comfort and load, see are stucco homes good for Arizona , and for pairing efficiency with on-site power, is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona .

Source

Read the section text on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC M1401.3 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours.

title: IRC M1601: Duct Construction & Insulation
shortSummary: IRC M1601 sets how ducts are built, sealed, and insulated. Joints and seams must be sealed against air leaks, and supply ducts run through an unconditioned attic must be insulated to at least R-8, which in Arizona heat is one of the biggest factors in a comfortable, efficient home.
bodyHtml:
In an Arizona home the ductwork usually runs through the attic, which in July hits 150 degrees. If those ducts leak or are poorly insulated, you are paying to cool the attic instead of the bedrooms. Section M1601 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the rulebook for how ducts are built and sealed, and the energy chapter sets how much insulation they need. Together they decide how much of your cooled air actually reaches the rooms.

What M1601 requires

M1601 covers how ducts are made and joined: the gauge of sheet metal, the listing of flexible and factory-made ducts, and how everything is held together. The part that homeowners feel most is the sealing rule in M1601.4, which says the connections in a duct system have to be made airtight:

Joints, seams and connections shall be made substantially airtight.

That means joints get sealed with mastic or a UL 181-listed tape before any insulation goes on, not held with cloth duct tape that dries out and falls off in attic heat. The insulation level itself comes from the energy chapter (Chapter 11 of the IRC, which mirrors the IECC). For our climate zone, supply ducts in an unconditioned attic must be insulated to at least R-8, and other ducts outside the conditioned space, such as returns, to at least R-6. The R-value is the measure of that insulation's resistance to heat flow.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, in a Scottsdale home with the air handler and ducts in a vented attic, leaky ducts can lose 20 to 30 percent of the cooled air before it reaches a register. Sealing the system to the M1601 standard and wrapping the supply runs to R-8 keeps that air where you paid to send it. Many Valley builders go further and either bury the ducts in deep attic insulation or move the whole system into a conditioned attic, because R-8 wrap around a duct sitting in 150-degree air is still fighting a steep gradient.

This ties directly to how the system was sized. A duct that leaks or sweats undercuts the Manual J load calculation that picked the equipment, and it drags down the real-world efficiency of even a high- SEER unit. Tight, well-insulated ducts are what let an efficient air conditioner actually deliver its rating. For how the home's shell affects all of this, see are stucco homes good for Arizona .

Edition note

The duct construction and sealing rules in M1601 are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The insulation R-values are tied to the energy code, and the R-8 supply / R-6 other-duct levels for attic ducts have held across these editions. The 2021 and 2024 editions kept tightening the duct-tightness testing and verification language, so newer adoptions tend to enforce a measured leakage test, not just a visual check. Which edition and energy code your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

Duct construction is the delivery side of the HVAC system sized under M1401 and Manual J , and it depends on the attic conditions set by R806 roof ventilation and the insulation in N1102 the thermal envelope . For pairing an efficient system with on-site power, see is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC M1601 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours. The duct insulation R-values come from the IRC energy chapter.

title: IRC N1102 / IECC: Building Thermal Envelope
shortSummary: IRC N1102 sets the insulation and window minimums for a home's thermal envelope. In Arizona climate zone 2B the prescriptive numbers are about R-38 ceilings, R-13 wood-frame walls, a 0.40 maximum window U-factor, and a 0.25 maximum solar heat gain coefficient to keep the sun out.
bodyHtml:
The thermal envelope is the insulated shell of your house: the ceiling, the walls, the floor, and the windows and doors that separate conditioned air from the outdoors. IRC Chapter 11 (Section N1102), which mirrors the International Energy Conservation Code (IECC), sets the minimum insulation and window performance for that shell. In Phoenix, where summer afternoons hit 115 degrees, these numbers decide how hard your air conditioner works and how high the August bill runs.

What N1102 requires for Arizona (zone 2B)

The Phoenix metro sits in climate zone 2B, the hot-dry zone, and the code's prescriptive table gives a set of minimum R-values (the measure of how well insulation resists heat flow) plus window limits. For zone 2 the headline numbers are: a ceiling of about R-38, a wood-frame wall of R-13 (or R-0 cavity plus R-10 continuous), and an exposed floor of R-13. Slab-edge insulation is not required in this zone. Here is the table note:

Where Section R402.1.3 requires R-49 insulation in the ceiling, installing R-38 over 100 percent of the ceiling area requiring insulation shall be deemed to satisfy the requirement for R-49.

The window numbers matter most here

In a cooling climate, the windows do more damage than the walls. N1102 caps two window properties. The U-factor, which measures heat conduction, must be 0.40 or lower. The solar heat gain coefficient (SHGC), which measures how much of the sun's heat passes through the glass, must be 0.25 or lower for glazed fenestration. That low SHGC is the Arizona-specific lever: a 0.25 SHGC window blocks three-quarters of the sun's heat, which is why low-E, spectrally selective glass is standard on Valley custom homes and why west-facing glass without it bakes a room.

What it means in practice

For example, a custom home in Fountain Hills built to the prescriptive path gets R-38 blown into the attic, R-13 batts in the 2x4 walls (or a 2x6 wall with more), and low-SHGC dual-pane windows rated at or below 0.40 U-factor and 0.25 SHGC. A house can also pass on a performance path, where a software model trades a better wall against a slightly worse window, but the prescriptive numbers above are the simple route most builders use. Air sealing and a proper vapor barrier strategy round out the envelope, though in the dry zone 2B climate, interior vapor retarders are generally not required.

How it connects

The envelope works with the mechanical and slab sections of the home. The slab itself is governed by IRC R506 concrete slab-on-ground floors , and the R-value concept here is the same one explained in the R-value glossary entry . Strong insulation also lets you size the air conditioner smaller, which lowers both the upfront equipment cost and the monthly bill.

Edition note

The zone 2 prescriptive R-values (R-38 ceiling, R-13 wall) and the 0.40 U-factor / 0.25 SHGC window limits are the same in the 2018 and 2021 IRC/IECC. The 2024 IECC pushes the envelope and equipment harder in some zones and adds new options, so a city on the 2024 code may enforce tighter targets. Because Arizona cities adopt the energy code on their own timelines, confirm the edition your jurisdiction enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

Source

Read the table on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC N1102.1.3 on codes.iccsafe.org . The I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short ceiling-insulation note is quoted here; the rest is our explanation.

title: IRC P2503: Plumbing Inspection & Tests
shortSummary: IRC P2503 requires plumbing to be tested before it is covered. The drain, waste, and vent piping gets a rough-in test, usually a 10-foot water column held for 15 minutes with no leaks, and the water supply gets its own pressure test, so leaks are found before walls close.
bodyHtml:
A plumbing leak is cheap to fix when the pipe is exposed and expensive once it is buried in a wall or under the slab. Section P2503 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the part that forces the leaks into the open early, by requiring the system to be tested and inspected before anything covers it. These are the inspections that gate the rest of your build, because drywall cannot go up until plumbing passes.

What P2503 requires

P2503 calls for inspection at set stages and a pressure test on each system. The big one is the rough-in test of the drain, waste, and vent (DWV) piping, in P2503.5, run before the piping is covered:

The DWV system shall be tested on completion of the rough piping installation.

The standard method fills the DWV piping with water to a point at least 10 feet above the highest fitting being tested, then holds that head for at least 15 minutes with no visible leak. Plastic systems can also be tested with a vacuum instead of water. The water supply piping gets its own test under P2503.7, pressurized and held to prove it is tight. The building sewer and any other buried lines are checked before backfill, because once the trench is closed a leak is invisible.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, on a slab-on-grade home in Scottsdale, the under-slab DWV is tested and inspected before the concrete is poured, because there is no fixing it afterward without breaking the slab. The plumber caps the system, fills the stacks with water to build the 10-foot head, and the inspector watches for any drop or drip. Only after that passes does the slab go down. The same logic drives the rough-in water-supply test before the walls are closed.

In Arizona this matters in two specific ways. Most homes are slab-on-grade, so a missed under-slab leak means jackhammering finished floors later. And the Valley's hard, mineral-heavy water and the heat-cycling of pipes make tight joints worth proving up front. Passing P2503 is also a scheduling gate: your framing, insulation, and drywall inspections all wait behind it. For how local inspectors layer their own steps on the model code, see what building codes apply in Scottsdale .

Edition note

The notable change here is the test head. The 2015 IRC dropped the DWV water test head to 5 feet, and the 2021 IRC raised it back to 10 feet to match the air-test pressure, so a 2018-edition jurisdiction may still be enforcing the 5-foot head while a 2021 jurisdiction uses 10 feet. The 15-minute hold and the requirement to test before covering have been stable. The 2024 IRC carries the 10-foot head forward. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page, and it directly changes how your plumber sets up the test.

How it connects

These tests gate the inspection sequence that also covers the structural and mechanical work, including the systems set under M1601 duct construction . The under-slab plumbing is tested before the foundation work in R403 footings and the slab are completed.

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC P2503.5 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours. ICC's own summary of the 2021 DWV testing change covers the head-pressure update.

title: IRC R301: Design Criteria (Loads)
shortSummary: IRC R301 sets the loads your house must safely carry: dead weight, live loads, wind, and earthquake. In the Phoenix area that means designing for about a 115 mph ultimate wind, a low seismic category, and essentially no snow load, so the structure is sized for the right local forces.
bodyHtml:
Every house has to stand up to forces it never sees on a calm day: its own weight, the people and furniture inside, the wind in a monsoon storm, and the shaking of an earthquake. Section R301 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the list of those forces and the minimum a home has to be built to resist. It is the starting point an engineer or plan reviewer uses before sizing a single beam.

What R301 requires

R301 says a home must be built to safely support all the loads that act on it. The code groups them and points to the numbers for each:

Buildings and structures shall be designed and constructed in accordance with the provisions of this code.

The loads break down into a few buckets. Dead load is the permanent weight of the building itself: framing, roof tile, drywall, and finishes. Live load is the temporary weight of use, set by Table R301.5, which calls for 40 pounds per square foot in living areas and 30 pounds per square foot in sleeping rooms. Wind load comes from Section R301.2.1, which uses the ultimate design wind speed maps from ASCE 7-16. Seismic load comes from R301.2.2, which sorts each site into a Seismic Design Category based on how much the ground is expected to shake. Snow load is set by R301.2.3, and in most of the Valley it is effectively zero.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, for a custom home in Scottsdale or Cave Creek, the local design numbers are mild compared with much of the country. The ultimate design wind speed across most of Maricopa and Pinal County runs about 115 mph, which sets how walls are braced and how the roof is tied down against monsoon microbursts. The ground snow load is essentially nothing, so unlike a Flagstaff home, a Valley roof is not sized for feet of snow. Seismic Design Category here is on the low end, usually A or B, so earthquake detailing is minimal next to California. That mix lets the structure focus on wind uplift and the real Arizona threat, which is heat and sun on the roof and walls rather than snow or quakes. The same heat is why the ceiling R-value matters more here than any snow rating.

The live and dead loads do not change with climate. A floor still has to carry 40 pounds per square foot of people and furniture whether it is in Phoenix or Portland. What the local design values change is the wind bracing and the near absence of snow and seismic demand.

Edition note

The biggest change to land in this section was the wind speed maps. The 2018 and 2021 IRC both reference ASCE 7-16 ultimate wind speeds, which lowered the design wind speed for much of the western United States compared with older editions that used a different map and conversion. The 2024 IRC carries the same load framework forward with minor updates. The live load table and the dead load approach have been stable across all three editions. Which edition your city enforces, and any local wind or seismic amendments, is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R301 is the load source that the rest of the structural chapters answer to. The wall bracing in R602 resists the wind load defined here, the R403 footings carry the dead and live load down to the soil, and the R703 exterior covering has to stay attached under wind pressure. For how local rules stack on top of the model code, see what building codes apply in Scottsdale .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R301 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours. FEMA's free compilation of the 2021 IRC wind provisions is a useful independent reference on how the wind maps work.

title: IRC R302: Fire-Resistant Construction
shortSummary: IRC R302 sets the fire separation between your garage and the living space. A wall shared with the house needs at least 1/2-inch gypsum board on the garage side, and a ceiling under habitable rooms above the garage needs 5/8-inch Type X gypsum board, plus a self-closing rated door.
bodyHtml:
The attached garage is the most fire-prone room touching your house. It holds the car, the gas can, the water heater, paint, and the lawn mower, all next to the place your family sleeps. IRC Section R302 builds a fire-resistant wall and ceiling between the garage and the living space so a fire in the garage is slowed down long enough for everyone to get out. This page covers the garage separation, the part of R302 most homeowners actually deal with.

What R302.6 requires

The separation is built from gypsum board (drywall), and the required thickness depends on what is on the other side. Table R302.6 sets two key numbers. The wall between the garage and the house, and the garage ceiling, needs at least 1/2-inch gypsum board applied to the garage side. But where there is habitable space above the garage, like a bedroom or bonus room over the bays, the ceiling under that room must be at least 5/8-inch Type X gypsum board, which is the denser, more fire-resistant grade.

The door between the garage and house is governed too. Here is the door requirement:

Openings from a private garage directly into a room used for sleeping purposes shall not be permitted. Other openings between the garage and residence shall be equipped with solid wood doors not less than 1 3/8 inches in thickness, solid or honeycomb-core steel doors not less than 1 3/8 inches thick, or 20-minute fire-rated doors, equipped with a self-closing or self-latching device.

What it means in practice

In plain terms: you cannot have a door from the garage straight into a bedroom, and the garage door into the house has to be a solid or rated door that swings shut and latches on its own. The self-closing hinge is the part homeowners most often defeat by propping the door open, which quietly removes the protection the code requires.

For example, on a Scottsdale custom home with a guest suite over a three-car garage, the builder hangs 5/8-inch Type X on the entire garage ceiling under that suite and 1/2-inch on the common wall, then sets a 20-minute rated, self-closing door at the entry. That ceiling upgrade is easy to miss on plans, and an inspector will fail the drywall inspection if the wrong board went up under the living space.

How it connects

R302 also covers fire separation between attached dwelling units, draftstopping, and flame-spread limits on foam insulation, but for a single-family custom home the garage separation is the provision that shapes the build. It works alongside the egress and alarm sections: IRC R311 means of egress gives you the way out, IRC R314 smoke alarms warns you early, and R302 buys the minutes in between.

Edition note

The garage separation thicknesses (1/2-inch general, 5/8-inch Type X under habitable space) and the door rules are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition reorganizes some adjacent fire provisions but does not change these garage numbers. As always, your city may amend the chapter, so confirm the adopted edition on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R302.6 on codes.iccsafe.org . The I-Codes are copyrighted; only the short door clause above is quoted, and the rest is our explanation.

title: IRC R303: Light, Ventilation & Heating
shortSummary: IRC R303 sets the minimum natural light, fresh air, and heat for habitable rooms. Each room needs window glass equal to at least 8 percent of its floor area and openable area equal to 4 percent, unless you swap those windows for mechanical ventilation and electric light.
bodyHtml:
Every living room, bedroom, and den in your house needs a baseline of daylight and fresh air, and Section R303 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets that floor. The rule is built around two percentages tied to the size of the room. It also gives you a way out of the window math that matters a lot in the desert, where a wall of operable glass means a wall of heat gain. This page covers the numbers and the mechanical swap that Arizona builders use constantly.

What R303 requires

A habitable room is one you live in: bedrooms, living rooms, dining rooms, dens. Bathrooms, closets, hallways, and storage are not habitable, so they are exempt from the light rule. For each habitable room, R303 sets two minimums measured against the floor area:

  • Glazing (window glass) of at least 8 percent of the room's floor area, for natural light.
  • Openable area of at least 4 percent of the room's floor area, for natural ventilation.

Here is the core requirement:

Habitable rooms shall have an aggregate glazing area of not less than 8 percent of the floor area of such rooms.

So a 200-square-foot bedroom needs at least 16 square feet of glass and at least 8 square feet of that has to open. R303 also requires every habitable room to be heatable to at least 68 degrees Fahrenheit, measured 3 feet above the floor.

The desert swap: mechanical vent in lieu of openable windows

This is the part that shapes Arizona homes. R303 lets you skip the openable-window rule if you install mechanical ventilation. When a whole-house system (or one moving 0.35 air changes per hour) is installed per Section M1505, the glass no longer has to open. You can go further. Add that system plus electric light averaging 6 footcandles at 30 inches above the floor, and the room does not need the natural-light glass either.

In practice, almost nobody throws their windows open in a Phoenix July. Big fixed glass with a sealed, conditioned envelope and balanced mechanical ventilation is the norm. For example, on a Scottsdale custom home with floor-to-ceiling fixed windows in the great room, the design leans on the mechanical-ventilation exception instead of forcing operable sashes that would leak conditioned air. The egress exception is separate: a bedroom still needs its operable escape window under R310 no matter what R303 allows.

Edition note

The 8 percent glazing and 4 percent ventilation minimums, and the mechanical-ventilation swap, are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition tightened whole-house mechanical ventilation requirements in Chapter 15 (M1505), which is the section the R303 exception points to. Which edition applies depends on your city, so confirm it on the Arizona building code editions by city page before sizing windows or a ventilation system.

How it connects

R303 governs the windows; R305 ceiling height governs the volume of those same habitable rooms, and the two are checked together at framing. The mechanical-ventilation exception ties straight into the home's fresh-air and makeup air design and the conditioned space envelope. For the inspection sequence that verifies all of this, see what inspections are required when building a house in Arizona .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R303.1 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short glazing clause is excerpted; the explanation is ours.

title: IRC R305: Ceiling Height
shortSummary: IRC R305 sets the minimum ceiling height for the rooms you live in at 7 feet (84 inches). Bathrooms, laundry rooms, and basements can drop to 6 feet 8 inches, and beams or ducts may hang down to 6 feet 4 inches without failing the room.
bodyHtml:
Ceiling height is one of the few code numbers a homeowner can check with a tape measure, and Section R305 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets the floor. The headline is simple: rooms you live in need at least 7 feet of clear height. The exceptions are where it gets useful, because they decide whether a dropped beam, a soffit over a kitchen, or a low basement passes. This page lays out the numbers and where they bend.

What R305 requires

The base rule covers habitable rooms (bedrooms, living and dining rooms, dens), plus hallways and bathrooms. They must have a ceiling height of at least 7 feet, which is 84 inches, measured from the finished floor to the finished ceiling. Here is the requirement:

Habitable space, hallways and portions of basements containing these spaces shall have a ceiling height of not less than 7 feet.

A few spaces get a break. Bathrooms, toilet rooms, and laundry rooms can drop to 6 feet 8 inches (80 inches), measured to the lowest projection like a fixture or fan. And not every part of a room has to hit the full height: beams, ducts, soffits, and similar obstructions may project down to 6 feet 4 inches (76 inches) above the floor, as long as they are spaced at least 4 feet apart. Sloped ceilings only have to meet the minimum over the part of the room counted as floor area.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

Arizona custom homes rarely flirt with the 7-foot floor in main rooms. Standard plate height here is 9 or 10 feet, and great rooms often run 12 feet or higher, partly for the look and partly because tall volume and high windows help vented heat rise away from where you sit. Where R305 actually gets tested is at the obstructions: a wood beam in a vaulted great room, a bulkhead hiding a duct run, or the underside of a stair.

For example, on a Cave Creek home with an exposed glulam beam crossing the living room, the beam can hang as low as 6 feet 4 inches and still pass, but a designer who drops it lower for proportion forces a redesign at framing. The same math governs a stair soffit over a hallway and the headroom under an HVAC chase in a conditioned attic. Garages are not habitable, so R305 does not set their height, but a car lift or a tall RV bay drives its own clearance, covered in what ceiling height you need for a car lift .

Edition note

The 7-foot habitable minimum and the 6-foot-8 bath/laundry allowance are unchanged across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition clarified the beam-and-obstruction projection down to 6 feet 4 inches with the 4-foot spacing, which earlier editions handled with slightly different wording. Confirm which edition your city enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R305 sets the vertical size of the same habitable rooms that R303 light and ventilation sizes for daylight and air. Tall ceilings interact with the conditioned space envelope and with thermal mass strategies common in desert design. For what builders actually spec in the Valley, see what ceiling height is standard in an Arizona custom home .

Source

title: IRC R308: Safety Glazing
shortSummary: IRC R308 requires safety glazing, usually tempered glass, in spots where someone could fall through and get cut. That means glass in and beside doors, around tubs and showers, near stairs, and large low panes. Tempered glass breaks into dull pebbles instead of sharp shards.
bodyHtml:
Safety glazing is glass made to break safely, and Section R308 of the International Residential Code (IRC) decides where your house has to use it. The reason is blunt: people walk into glass doors, slip in showers, and trip on stairs, and ordinary annealed glass breaks into long knife-edged shards. Tempered glass breaks into small dull cubes that rarely cut deep. This page lists the "hazardous locations" R308 flags and the exact distances that trigger the requirement, because that is what gets caught at inspection.

What R308 requires

R308 names specific hazardous locations where glazing must be safety glass, meaning tempered or laminated glass that carries a permanent maker's mark in the corner. The common triggers in a house:

  • Glass in any door, including sliding, swinging, and bifold doors.
  • Glass next to a door, where the bottom edge is under 60 inches off the floor and the glass is within 24 inches of the door's edge in the plane of the door.
  • Glass at tubs, showers, spas, and pools, where the bottom edge of the glass is less than 60 inches above the standing or walking surface.
  • Glass at the bottom of a stairway, within a 60-inch arc and less than 36 inches above the landing.
  • Large low panes: glass bigger than 9 square feet whose bottom edge is under 18 inches and top edge over 36 inches above the floor, where you could walk into it.

Here is the door clause that drives most callouts:

Glazing in fixed and operable panels of swinging, sliding and bifold doors shall be considered to be a hazardous location.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

Arizona custom homes are heavy on glass, so R308 shows up everywhere. Multi-slide and pocket glass walls onto a patio are all doors, so every panel is tempered. The fixed sidelight beside the front door is tempered if it sits within 24 inches of the door. Frameless glass shower enclosures, a near-universal feature in Scottsdale and Paradise Valley primary baths, are tempered top to bottom. A window beside a soaking tub is tempered if its sill is under 60 inches above the tub deck.

For example, on a Paradise Valley home with a glass wall in the great room and a freestanding tub set against an exterior window, the plan check will flag both the slider panels and the tub window for tempered glass before the permit issues. Catch it in the window schedule and the cost is small; miss it and you replace installed glass. Note that safety glazing and low-e glass are different things: low-e controls heat, tempering controls breakage, and a desert window often needs both.

Edition note

The hazardous-location list and the trigger distances (24 inches beside a door, 60 inches at wet areas, the 9-square-foot large-pane rule) are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition refined the stairway and railing glazing provisions in R308.4.6 and R308.4.7, but the everyday door, tub, and shower triggers did not move. Confirm the edition your city enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R308 protects the glazed openings that R303 light and ventilation requires and that the home's fenestration schedule lays out. It pairs with the guard and fall rules in R312 and the stair safety rules in R311.7 stairways . All three get verified at the final inspection in the sequence required when building a house in Arizona .

title: IRC R310: Emergency Escape & Rescue Openings
shortSummary: IRC R310 requires every bedroom and basement to have at least one window or door big enough to climb out of in a fire. The opening must give 5.7 square feet of clear space, at least 24 inches tall and 20 inches wide, with the sill no higher than 44 inches off the floor.
bodyHtml:
Every bedroom in your house needs a second way out, and Section R310 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the rule that sets the size of that escape route. The idea is simple: if a fire blocks the bedroom door, you, a child, or a firefighter in full gear has to be able to get through a window. R310 turns "big enough" into hard numbers so nobody has to guess.

What R310 requires

Every sleeping room, every basement, and every habitable attic must have at least one operable emergency escape and rescue opening. That is usually a window. A door to the outside also counts. The opening has to meet four numbers, all measured with the window opened the normal way from the inside:

  • Net clear opening of at least 5.7 square feet.
  • Net clear height of at least 24 inches.
  • Net clear width of at least 20 inches.
  • Sill height no more than 44 inches above the finished floor.

"Net clear" means the actual hole you climb through with the sash open, not the rough frame size. Here is the core requirement:

Emergency escape and rescue openings shall have a net clear opening of not less than 5.7 square feet.

Those numbers fight each other on purpose. You cannot hit 5.7 square feet using only the 24-inch and 20-inch minimums, because 24 times 20 is just 3.3 square feet. One dimension has to grow. A grade-floor opening is allowed to drop to 5.0 square feet, but bedrooms above or below grade need the full 5.7.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

On a custom home in Scottsdale or Cave Creek, this drives window selection in every bedroom. For example, a standard single-hung window only opens half its height, so the glass has to be tall enough that the open half still clears 24 inches. Casement windows pass more easily because the whole sash swings out. Your window schedule should call out the egress units by tag so the framing rough opening is right the first time.

The 44-inch sill rule matters for design choices people love in the Valley: tall window walls, deep window seats, and beds on raised platforms. If a remodel or a tall built-in pushes the effective sill above 44 inches, that bedroom fails egress even if the window is huge. Window wells handle below-grade openings on the rare Arizona basement, and they have their own minimum size and ladder rules.

Edition note

The four core numbers (5.7 square feet, 24-inch height, 20-inch width, 44-inch sill) are the same in the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition reorganized the section and folded in the window-well and bar-grille details, but the dimensions a homeowner cares about did not move. Which edition your city enforces is what varies, so check the Arizona building code editions by city page before you finalize a window schedule.

How it connects

R310 is the fire-escape partner to R311 means of egress , which covers the normal way out (doors, hallways, stairs). R310 is the backup route when the normal one is blocked. Both get checked at the framing and final inspections before you get a certificate of occupancy . For the full inspection sequence, see what inspections are required when building a house in Arizona .

Source

title: IRC R311: Means of Egress
shortSummary: IRC R311 sets the safe path out of a house. Stairs allow a maximum 7.75-inch riser and require at least a 10-inch tread, hallways must be at least 36 inches wide, and every home needs one side-hinged egress door giving at least 32 inches of clear width.
bodyHtml:
Means of egress is code language for the path you take to get out of the house in an emergency. IRC Section R311 makes sure that path is wide enough, the stairs are walkable, and there is at least one real door to the outside. These rules exist because most stair falls and trapped-occupant situations trace back to stairs that are too steep, halls that are too tight, or an exit that does not actually open. Here are the numbers a custom home has to hit.

What R311 requires

The stair geometry is the most-checked part. The maximum riser height (the vertical step) is 7.75 inches, and the minimum tread depth (the part your foot lands on) is 10 inches. Those two numbers together set a comfortable, safe slope. The stairway itself must be at least 36 inches wide above the handrail. Here is the tread-and-riser language:

The maximum riser height shall be 7 3/4 inches. The minimum tread depth shall be 10 inches.

Two more numbers complete the path. Hallways serving the egress route must be at least 36 inches wide. And every dwelling needs at least one egress door that is side-hinged and gives at least 32 inches of clear width with the door open 90 degrees, which works out to roughly a 36-inch door slab. That egress door cannot require a key or special knowledge to open from the inside.

Why the numbers are what they are

A 7.75-inch riser paired with a 10-inch tread keeps the stair shallow enough that you can descend quickly in the dark without pitching forward. The code also limits how much risers can vary within one flight (3/8 inch), because an uneven step is what trips people. The 36-inch hall and 32-inch door clearance exist so an adult, or two people helping a third, can move through without turning sideways during a fire.

For example, on a two-story custom home in Phoenix, an interior designer who wants a dramatic open stair still has to land the rise and run inside these limits, or the framing inspector rejects it and the stair gets rebuilt. The cheap fix is to design the run correctly on paper; the expensive fix is tearing out poured treads.

How it connects

R311 is the "get out" half of life safety. It pairs with IRC R314 smoke alarms , which wake you up, and the emergency escape opening rules for bedrooms in R310, which give a second way out of each sleeping room. The fire separation in IRC R302 fire-resistant construction buys the time you need to use these exits.

Edition note

The core stair geometry (7.75-inch maximum riser, 10-inch minimum tread, 36-inch width) and the 32-inch egress-door clear width are unchanged across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. Handrail and guard details get small tweaks between editions, but the headline egress numbers hold steady. Confirm which edition and amendments your city enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

Source
