title: IRC R311.7: Stairways
shortSummary: IRC R311.7 sets stair geometry so people climb safely. Risers cap at 7.75 inches, treads run at least 10 inches, headroom is at least 6 feet 8 inches, the stair is at least 36 inches wide, and a graspable handrail sits 34 to 38 inches above the nosings.
bodyHtml:
Stairs cause more household injuries than almost any other built feature, so Section R311.7 of the International Residential Code (IRC) nails down the geometry that makes them safe to climb: how steep the steps, how much headroom, how wide the run, and what a handrail has to feel like in your hand. This page goes deeper on the stair itself than the broader egress page does, covering headroom, handrail graspability, landings, and winders, the details that get a custom stair rejected or approved.

What R311.7 requires

The core geometry comes in pairs. The maximum riser (the vertical face of a step) is 7.75 inches, and the minimum tread (the depth your foot lands on) is 10 inches. Beyond that, R311.7 sets the surrounding dimensions:

  • Width: at least 36 inches above the handrail height.
  • Headroom: at least 6 feet 8 inches (80 inches) measured vertically from the nosing line to the ceiling or beam above.
  • Uniformity: risers within a flight may vary no more than 3/8 inch, and treads the same.
  • Nosing: where treads are solid, a nosing of 3/4 inch to 1.25 inches is required on stairs with closed risers.

Here is the geometry clause:

The maximum riser height shall be 7 3/4 inches. The minimum tread depth shall be 10 inches.

Handrails, landings, and winders

A handrail is required on at least one side of any stair with four or more risers. It must sit 34 to 38 inches above the nosings and be continuous the length of the flight. Graspability matters: a round rail must be 1.25 to 2 inches across, and other shapes have a perimeter and finger-recess spec so a falling hand can actually close around it. A decorative rail too fat or flat to grip fails.

Landings at the top and bottom must be at least as deep as the stair is wide, up to 36 inches. Winders, the pie-shaped treads on a turning stair, must give at least 10 inches of tread depth measured 12 inches in from the narrow side, and never less than 6 inches at the narrow point. These are the details that trip up a dramatic curved or spiral stair.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

On a two-story Scottsdale or Cave Creek custom home, the open feature stair is where R311.7 bites hardest. A designer who pushes the rise to look dramatic or wraps a tight curve has to keep every riser at or under 7.75 inches, hold 6 feet 8 inches of headroom under the floor header above, and land the winders at 10 inches of effective tread. For example, a steel-and-wood floating stair still needs a graspable handrail at 34 to 38 inches and infill that meets the guard rules in R312 . Designing it right on paper costs a revision; rebuilding poured or welded treads after the framing inspector measures them costs real money.

Edition note

The 7.75-inch riser, 10-inch tread, 36-inch width, and 6-foot-8 headroom are unchanged across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. Handrail graspability and winder details get minor wording tweaks between editions, but the headline numbers hold. Confirm the edition your city enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R311.7 is the stair detail under the broader R311 means of egress page, which covers the whole exit path (doors, halls, the egress door). It works hand in glove with R312 guards for the open sides and rails, and R308 safety glazing for any glass at the bottom landing. All get verified before the certificate of occupancy . For the full check sequence, see what inspections are required when building a house in Arizona .

title: IRC R312: Guards & Window Fall Protection
shortSummary: IRC R312 requires a guard at any walking surface more than 30 inches above the ground, at least 36 inches tall, with openings too small to pass a 4-inch sphere. R312.2 adds window fall protection: an operable window sill lower than 24 inches needs a limiter or guard.
bodyHtml:
A guard is the barrier that keeps people from falling off a raised floor, balcony, or stair, and Section R312 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets how tall it has to be and how tight the gaps. The same section adds a second protection most people miss: a rule for low operable windows on upper floors, so a child cannot push out a screen and fall. This page covers both the guard numbers and the window-sill rule, which together drive a lot of stair and balcony design in two-story homes.

What R312 requires for guards

A guard is required wherever a walking surface (a floor, balcony, landing, or the open side of a stair) sits more than 30 inches above the ground or floor below, measured within 36 inches horizontally of the edge. Once a guard is required, three numbers govern it:

  • Guard height of at least 36 inches above the walking surface, or above the line connecting stair nosings.
  • Openings small enough that a 4-inch sphere cannot pass through, from the walking surface up to the required height.
  • On the open side of stairs, the triangle at the bottom may pass a 6-inch sphere, a small allowance for the stringer geometry.

Here is the height clause:

Required guards at open-sided walking surfaces ... shall be not less than 36 inches in height as measured vertically above the adjacent walking surface.

The 4-inch sphere rule is why balusters sit roughly 4 inches apart and why a horizontal "ladder" rail design is risky: inspectors check that a child cannot slip or climb through.

What R312.2 requires for windows

Section R312.2 is the window fall-protection rule. Where an operable window sits on an upper floor with the bottom of its clear opening less than 24 inches above the finished floor, and the outside drop is more than 72 inches to the grade below, the window needs protection. You meet it one of two ways: raise the opening so its bottom is at least 24 inches off the floor, or install a fall-prevention device or window opening limiter that stops the sash from opening enough to pass a 4-inch sphere. The limiter still has to release for emergency escape under R310 .

What it means for an Arizona custom home

Two-story homes in Scottsdale, Fountain Hills, and the hillside lots of Paradise Valley run into both rules constantly. An open great-room loft, a second-floor walkway, and any balcony over a patio all need a 36-inch guard with tight infill. Designers who want a clean glass or cable look have to detail it so it still blocks a 4-inch sphere and resists the required load.

For example, on a two-story Fountain Hills home with bedroom windows low to the floor and a steep lot dropping away outside, R312.2 forces either a higher window sill or factory opening limiters on those sashes. The cheap path is to set the window heights right on the plans; the expensive path is retrofitting limiters after the framing inspector flags it. These details are checked before you get a certificate of occupancy .

Edition note

The 36-inch guard height, the 30-inch trigger, and the 4-inch sphere rule are unchanged across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. Window fall protection (R312.2) has been in the code since the 2009 edition and its 24-inch sill threshold is stable. Confirm which edition your city enforces on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R312 guards the same stairs and landings that R311.7 stairways shapes, and its window rule works alongside the escape-window sizing in R310 . Glass guards and low glazing near these spots also trigger R308 safety glazing . For where it all gets checked, see what inspections are required when building a house in Arizona .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R312 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short guard clause is excerpted; the explanation is ours.

title: IRC R313: Automatic Residential Fire Sprinklers
shortSummary: IRC R313 calls for an automatic fire-sprinkler system in new one- and two-family homes. But Arizona law (A.R.S. 9-807) bars cities from enforcing that mandate on most new homes, so it is deleted by amendment almost everywhere. Fountain Hills is the local exception.
bodyHtml:
Section R313 of the International Residential Code (IRC) requires an automatic fire-sprinkler system in new one- and two-family homes. That is the model code. In Arizona, though, a state law overrides it almost everywhere, so most new homes here are built without sprinklers even though the IRC asks for them. This page explains what R313 says, why Arizona cities delete it, and the one local jurisdiction where you still need sprinklers.

What R313 requires

R313 says an automatic residential fire-sprinkler system must be installed in new one- and two-family dwellings and townhouses, designed to the residential standard NFPA 13D or the IRC's own Section P2904. The goal is to control a fire long enough for everyone to get out. Here is the core requirement:

An automatic residential fire sprinkler system shall be installed in one- and two-family dwellings.

This has been in the IRC since the 2009 edition. On paper, every new custom home in Arizona would need a sprinkler system. In reality, almost none do, because of state law.

The Arizona override (A.R.S. 9-807)

Arizona Revised Statute 9-807 bars cities and counties from enforcing a code that requires fire sprinklers in new single-family or two-family homes, unless the local mandate was already on the books before a 2009 cutoff. So when an Arizona city adopts the IRC, it amends out R313 (or makes it optional). That is why Phoenix, Scottsdale, Cave Creek, Carefree, and the counties do not force sprinklers on a standard new house. The state decided the cost should be the owner's choice, not a mandate.

There is a real exception: Fountain Hills adopted its residential sprinkler requirement in 2008, before the cutoff, so it is grandfathered and still enforced. A new home in Fountain Hills needs sprinklers reaching every level plus attached garages and carports. See the Fountain Hills fire sprinkler requirements page for the full local rule and what it costs.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For most Valley builds, R313 is a code section you can read about but will not have to follow, because your city deleted it. You can still choose to install sprinklers, and some owners do for fire safety or insurance, but it is voluntary. Budget impact is the headline: a 13D system typically adds a few dollars per covered square foot, so a Fountain Hills home where it is mandatory carries a cost a comparable Scottsdale home skips.

For example, two identical floor plans, one in Fountain Hills and one in nearby Scottsdale, will price differently because only the Fountain Hills home must plumb and pressure-test a sprinkler system before its final inspection. This is one of the clearer cost gaps between neighboring jurisdictions, covered in what drives the cost of building a custom home in Arizona .

Edition note

The R313 requirement itself is consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. What varies in Arizona is not the edition but the local amendment: nearly every AHJ here strikes R313 under A.R.S. 9-807, while Fountain Hills keeps it. Always check the local amendment, not just the base edition, on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R313 is the active-suppression side of fire safety. It backs up the warning systems in R314 smoke alarms and the fire-separation rules in R302 . On wildland-edge lots it pairs with defensible space planning. For the local sprinkler question most often asked, see do I need fire sprinklers in a new home in Fountain Hills, Arizona .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R313 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause is excerpted; the explanation, and the Arizona override, are ours.

title: IRC R314: Smoke Alarms
shortSummary: IRC R314 says where smoke alarms go in a house: inside every bedroom, outside each sleeping area near the bedrooms, and on every story including the basement. In a new home the alarms must be hardwired with battery backup and interconnected so when one sounds, they all sound.
bodyHtml:
Smoke alarms are the cheapest life-safety system in a house and the one that saves the most lives. IRC Section R314 spells out exactly where they go and how they have to work, so a fire that starts while you sleep wakes you in time to use the exits. The rule is not "put one in the hallway." It is a specific set of locations plus an interconnection requirement that most homeowners do not realize their house needs.

Where R314 requires alarms

The code requires a smoke alarm in three kinds of locations: inside each sleeping room (every bedroom), outside each separate sleeping area in the immediate vicinity of the bedrooms (the hall outside the bedrooms), and on each additional story of the home, including basements and habitable attics. Here is the location language:

Smoke alarms shall be installed in each of the following locations: In each sleeping room. Outside each separate sleeping area in the immediate vicinity of the bedrooms. On each additional story of the dwelling, including basements.

How the alarms must work

Location is only half of R314. In new construction the alarms must be interconnected, so that when any one alarm detects smoke, every alarm in the house sounds. A fire that starts in a far bedroom has to wake a sleeper at the other end of the house, and a single beeping unit cannot do that. The alarms also draw primary power from the building wiring (hardwired) with a battery backup so they keep working in a power outage. Interconnection can be a hard wire between units or a listed wireless link.

There is one placement carve-out worth knowing: an alarm has to sit at least 3 feet from a bathroom with a tub or shower, because steam triggers nuisance alarms that get people to disable them.

What it means in practice

For example, a four-bedroom single-story custom home in Cave Creek needs a smoke alarm in all four bedrooms, one in the hallway serving them, and one in any additional living area that reads as a separate story or level, all wired together. Add a second story and you add at least one alarm for that level. The cost is small, but the interconnection wiring has to be planned during rough electrical, not added later.

How it connects

R314 is the early warning that makes the rest of the life-safety chain work. It pairs with IRC R315 carbon monoxide alarms , which often share a combination device, and with IRC R311 means of egress , the safe path the alarm sends you to. The fire separation in IRC R302 fire-resistant construction slows the fire while the alarm gets everyone moving.

Edition note

The required locations (each bedroom, outside each sleeping area, each story) and the interconnection rule are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition adds provisions around alarm replacement age and certain listing standards, but the placement and interconnection numbers above do not change. Confirm your city's adopted edition on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

Source

title: IRC R315: Carbon Monoxide Alarms
shortSummary: IRC R315 requires carbon monoxide alarms in a home that has a fuel-fired appliance or fireplace, or an attached garage that opens into the house. The alarms go outside each sleeping area near the bedrooms, and inside any bedroom that contains a fuel-burning appliance.
bodyHtml:
Carbon monoxide is a colorless, odorless gas that comes from anything that burns fuel: a furnace, a gas water heater, a fireplace, or a car idling in the garage. You cannot smell it, and at high levels it kills before you wake up. IRC Section R315 decides which homes must have CO alarms and where those alarms go. The rule is narrower than the smoke-alarm rule, because not every house produces carbon monoxide.

When R315 requires CO alarms

A home needs carbon monoxide alarms if either of two conditions is true. Here is the trigger:

For new construction, an approved carbon monoxide alarm shall be installed outside of each separate sleeping area in the immediate vicinity of the bedrooms in dwelling units within which fuel-fired appliances are installed and in dwelling units that have attached garages.

In plain terms: if the house has a fuel-fired appliance or fireplace, or an attached garage that opens into the living space, CO alarms are required. An all-electric house with no gas appliances and no attached garage is the rare case that may not need them.

Where the alarms go

The placement rule mirrors the smoke alarm logic. CO alarms go outside each separate sleeping area, near the bedrooms, so the alarm sits between a sleeping person and the source of the gas. There is one addition: if a fuel-burning appliance sits inside a bedroom or its attached bathroom, a CO alarm has to be installed in that bedroom. The alarms must be listed to UL 2034, and combination smoke/CO units must meet both UL 2034 and UL 217.

What it means in practice

For example, a custom home in Apache Junction with a gas furnace, a gas tankless water heater, and an attached three-car garage clearly triggers R315. The builder places a CO alarm in the hallway outside each bedroom cluster. Many builders use combination smoke-and-CO alarms to satisfy both IRC R314 smoke alarms and R315 with one hardwired, interconnected device, which keeps the ceilings cleaner and the wiring simpler.

The Arizona angle is the attached garage. The Valley's hot months mean cars sit in closed garages, and an attached garage that communicates with the house is enough on its own to require CO alarms even in an otherwise all-electric home.

How it connects

R315 is the second half of the alarm pair. It sits right next to IRC R314 smoke alarms in the code and is usually wired together with it, and it complements the garage fire separation in IRC R302 fire-resistant construction .

Edition note

The trigger conditions (fuel-fired appliance or attached garage) and the placement near sleeping areas are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition tightens some power-source and listing details, but the "when required" and "where" rules above hold. Confirm your city's adopted edition on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

title: IRC R324: Rooftop Solar Photovoltaic Systems
shortSummary: IRC R324 governs how rooftop solar panels are installed. Panels must meet the same wind and fire ratings as the roof, and R324.6 keeps clear access pathways for firefighters: two 36-inch paths to the ridge and an 18-inch clear setback below the ridge on most homes.
bodyHtml:
Solar makes obvious sense in Arizona, and Section R324 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the part of the code that says how panels attach to your roof and how they share that roof with firefighters. It covers two things people rarely think about until plan check: the panels have to meet the roof's own wind and fire ratings, and they have to leave clear lanes a crew can walk and cut for ventilation. This page explains both, with the access numbers that decide how much of your roof can actually hold panels.

What R324 requires

R324 has two halves. The first is structural and fire. Rooftop photovoltaic (PV) panels and their mounts have to be rated for the same wind loads as the building and carry a fire classification that does not downgrade the roof. The mounting can't compromise the roof's water shedding or its rated covering.

The second half is roof access and pathways under R324.6, and that is what shapes the array layout. The code keeps lanes open so firefighters can reach the roof, move across it, and cut ventilation holes. Here is the access intent:

Roof access, pathways and setbacks ... shall provide emergency access to the roof, provide pathways to specific areas of the roof, provide for smoke ventilation opportunity areas and provide emergency egress from the roof.

The access numbers

For a typical pitched roof, R324.6 calls for:

  • At least two 36-inch-wide pathways from the lowest roof edge up to the ridge, on separate roof planes.
  • A 36-inch-wide pathway on the same or an adjacent plane for each roof plane that carries an array.
  • A clear setback at the ridge: at least 18 inches on both sides of a horizontal ridge where the array covers no more than a third of the roof, growing to 36 inches on both sides where the array covers more than a third.

These setbacks and lanes cut into usable panel area, which is why a system designer maps them before counting how many modules fit.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

Arizona has the sun, so the limiting factors are roof geometry and these access rules, not whether solar pays off. On a low-slope tile or foam roof common in Scottsdale and Phoenix, the array has to leave its ridge setbacks and edge pathways, so a roof that looks fully usable from the street may only hold panels on part of its planes. Tile roofs add a flashing-and-mount detail so the penetrations stay watertight through monsoon storms.

For example, on a single-story Cave Creek home with a hip roof, the two required access paths plus the ridge setbacks can shrink the panel zone enough that a builder reorients the array to a larger plane or plans a ground mount. Designing the roof and structure for solar from the start, including conduit and a cool roof surface that also cuts attic heat, beats retrofitting later. If you are weighing a system, see is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona and should I prewire a new home for solar and automation .

Edition note

The structural and fire provisions are stable, but the access-pathway and setback details have evolved. The 2021 IRC carries the two-pathway and 18-inch/36-inch ridge-setback scheme; the 2024 edition refines pathway and ventilation language further, and your local fire code amendments can tighten it. Because solar access is partly a fire-code item, the adopted edition and any fire amendments both matter. Check them on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R324 sits on top of the roof that R905 roof covering and R802 roof framing build, and added panel weight has to land within those structural limits. The wiring ties back to the home's electrical service under E3601/E3602 . Local enforcement runs through your AHJ and its fire reviewer.

title: IRC R401-R405: Foundations, Soils & Drainage
shortSummary: IRC R401 through R405 cover the foundation chapter: how the soil is classified, how the ground must drain, and when a foundation has to be engineered. The headline rule is that the lot must slope away from the house at least 6 inches over the first 10 feet.
bodyHtml:
Chapter 4 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the foundation chapter, and Sections R401 through R405 set the rules before a single footing is poured. They decide how the lot drains, how the soil under your house is judged, and when a plain slab is not allowed. In the Phoenix metro, this chapter is where soil that swells, called expansive soil, forces an engineered foundation. Here is what each piece requires and why it matters on Arizona ground.

R401: general rules and drainage

R401 sets the baseline. The foundation has to carry the building's weight down to soil that can hold it, and the lot has to shed water away from the house. The grading number is the one to remember:

Lots shall be graded to drain surface water away from foundation walls. The grade shall fall a minimum of 6 inches within the first 10 feet.

That 6-inch fall over the first 10 feet keeps monsoon runoff and irrigation from pooling against the slab. R401.4 is the soil-characteristics rule. When the building official has reason to question the soil, a soil report is required to classify it, and that report drives everything downstream.

R403: footings and the expansive-soil trigger

R403 sizes the footing and contains the rule that reshapes most Valley foundations. Soil that tests as expansive soil (clay that swells when wet and shrinks when dry) cannot sit under a conventional footing and slab. The code sends that design to the International Building Code for an engineered solution, which in Arizona usually means a stiffened post-tensioned slab. We cover the footing numbers in depth on the R403 footings page.

R405: foundation drainage

R405 covers drains around foundations that retain earth and enclose space below grade, like a basement. It calls for a perimeter drain of gravel, drain tile, or perforated pipe that carries groundwater away by gravity or a pump. The exception is the key Arizona detail: no drain is required when the foundation sits on well-drained ground or clean sand-gravel soil (Group I under the Unified Soil Classification System). Because most Valley homes are slab-on-grade with no basement, R405 rarely triggers here, but it governs the occasional hillside or daylight-basement build in Cave Creek or Fountain Hills.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, on a build-on-your-lot project in Apache Junction, the order of operations is set by this chapter: the geotechnical engineer drills and tests the soil, the report classifies it, and the foundation gets designed to that report. Clean granular soil may allow a conventional footing. An expansive result means an engineered slab and a thicker stem wall, which costs more but stops the cracking that plagues older homes built before this was enforced. The 6-inch grading rule then has to survive landscaping, so confirm final grade slopes away after the yard goes in.

Edition note

The grading rule (6 inches in 10 feet), the soil-classification framework, and the foundation-drainage exception are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition tightened a few detailing items, but the homeowner-level numbers did not change. What changes is which edition your city enforces, listed on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

This chapter is the front end of the foundation. It hands off to R403 footings for sizing and to R506 concrete slab-on-ground floors for the slab itself. For the homeowner questions it answers, see do I need a soil test to build in Arizona and foundation design for expansive soil .

title: IRC R403: Footings
shortSummary: IRC R403 sets the minimum size and depth for the footings under your house. A standard footing must be at least 12 inches wide and 6 inches thick, placed at least 12 inches below undisturbed grade, and footings on expansive soil must be engineered to handle the soil movement.
bodyHtml:
A footing is the wide concrete base that spreads your house's weight onto the soil so the structure does not sink or tip. Section R403 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets the floor for how big and how deep that base has to be. Get it wrong and you get cracked walls, sticking doors, and an uneven slab. This page explains the real numbers, then the part that matters most in the Valley: what R403 demands when the soil underneath swells.

What R403 requires

A conventional spread footing has three baseline numbers. It must be at least 12 inches wide, at least 6 inches thick, and set at least 12 inches below the undisturbed ground surface. "Undisturbed" means original, compacted soil, not loose fill someone dumped on the lot. Beyond those minimums, R403 sends you to Table R403.1 to size the footing to the load above it and the bearing strength of the soil below it. Heavier houses and weaker soils mean wider footings.

The depth rule normally exists to get the footing below the frost line, but most of Arizona has no meaningful frost depth, so the 12-inch minimum governs here instead. Many Maricopa and Pinal County jurisdictions still require deeper footings (18 inches is common) to reach stable soil below the zone where moisture moves up and down.

Here is the requirement the code points to for problem soils:

Foundation and floor slabs for buildings located on expansive soils shall be designed in accordance with Section 1808.6 of the International Building Code.

The expansive-soil rule (R403.1.8)

Section R403.1.8 is the provision that drives foundation design across the Phoenix metro. Soil is classified as expansive soil when it meets a set of lab thresholds, including a plasticity index of 15 or greater and an expansion index above 20. Expansive clay absorbs water and swells, then dries and shrinks. That movement lifts and drops parts of a footing at different rates, which is what cracks foundations.

When a site tests expansive, R403.1.8 stops you from using a plain footing and slab. The foundation has to be engineered, usually as a stiffened post-tensioned slab or a pier-and-beam system designed by a licensed engineer to ride out the soil movement. This is why a soils report comes before the foundation design on almost every custom home east of the I-17 corridor.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, on a build-on-your-lot project in Cave Creek or Apache Junction, the sequence is: geotechnical engineer drills and tests the soil, the report classifies it, then the foundation is designed to that report. If the lot is clean granular soil, a conventional R403 footing at 12 to 18 inches deep may be all you need. If it tests expansive, expect an engineered slab and a thicker stem wall, which adds cost but prevents the cracking that haunts older Valley homes built before this was enforced. For the slab side of that decision, see why post-tension slabs are common in Arizona .

Edition note

The core footing dimensions in R403.1 (12-inch width, 6-inch thickness, 12-inch depth) are unchanged across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The expansive-soil pointer to IBC 1808.6 is also stable. What changes is which edition your city enforces and what local amendments add on top. Scottsdale, Phoenix, and the counties each adopt and amend separately, so confirm the edition on the Arizona building code editions by city page before you design to a specific table.

title: IRC R506: Concrete Slab-on-Ground Floors
shortSummary: IRC R506 sets the rules for a concrete slab poured directly on the ground. The slab must be at least 3.5 inches thick, and in the 2021 IRC a 10-mil vapor retarder goes under any heated slab to keep ground moisture from wicking up into the finished floor.
bodyHtml:
Almost every house in the Phoenix metro sits on a concrete slab poured straight onto the prepared ground, not a raised floor over a crawl space. IRC Section R506 is the part of the code that governs that slab: how thick it has to be, what goes under it, and how to keep ground moisture out of your finished floors. The two numbers that matter most are the slab thickness and, since the 2021 edition, the thickness of the plastic sheet beneath it.

What R506 requires

The headline number is thickness. Under R506.1, a slab-on-ground floor must be at least 3.5 inches thick. That is the prescriptive minimum for a plain residential slab. The section also points to the soil rules: if the lot is expansive, you leave the prescriptive path and design the slab as an engineered foundation instead.

The second requirement is moisture control. Bare ground constantly gives off water vapor, and concrete is porous, so without a barrier that vapor migrates up through the slab and ruins wood flooring, lifts tile, and feeds mold under carpet. R506.2.3 requires a vapor barrier (the code calls it a vapor retarder) between the slab and the ground. Here is the current requirement:

A minimum 10-mil vapor retarder conforming to ASTM E1745 Class A requirements with joints lapped not less than 6 inches shall be placed between the concrete floor slab and the base course or the prepared subgrade.

What it means in practice

A 10-mil sheet is thicker and tougher than the flimsy 6-mil poly that was standard for years, so it resists puncture from rebar chairs and foot traffic during the pour. The 6-inch lap at the seams matters too: a gap at a joint is a direct path for vapor. The retarder is not required under unheated space like a garage slab, a detached utility building, or a patio, since there is no finished floor to protect.

For example, on a custom home in Fountain Hills with hardwood in the great room, the crew rolls out the 10-mil sheet over the compacted subgrade, laps and tapes the seams, sets the rebar or post-tension cables on chairs above it, then pours the slab. Skip or tear that sheet and the homeowner may see cupped boards within a year, with no easy fix once the floor is down.

How it ties to slab type

R506 sets the minimum, but on expansive Valley soil the slab is usually upgraded to an engineered post-tension slab , where steel cables are tensioned after the concrete cures to hold the slab stiff against soil movement. That design is driven by the foundation rules in IRC R403 footings , while R506 still governs the slab's thickness and the vapor retarder under it. To compare the two common slab designs, see post-tension slab vs rebar slab and why post-tension slabs are common in Arizona .

Edition note

The vapor retarder thickness is the headline change. The 2018 IRC required only a 6-mil retarder; the 2021 IRC raised it to 10-mil Class A under heated slabs, and the 2024 IRC keeps the 10-mil rule. The 3.5-inch minimum slab thickness is unchanged across all three editions. Because the 6-mil-versus-10-mil difference depends entirely on which edition your city adopted, confirm it on the Arizona building code editions by city page before a crew shows up with the wrong roll of plastic.

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R506.2.3 on codes.iccsafe.org . The I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the one clause above is quoted; everything else here is our own explanation.

title: IRC R602: Wood Wall Framing & Bracing
shortSummary: IRC R602 sets the rules for wood-framed walls: what size studs to use, how far apart to space them, and how much braced wall panel a house needs to resist wind and earthquakes. A standard braced wall panel must be at least 48 inches long.
bodyHtml:
Section R602 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the rulebook for wood-framed walls, the most common way custom homes in Arizona are built. It does two jobs: it sizes the studs that hold up the roof and floors, and it sets how much wall bracing a house needs so it does not rack and lean over in a windstorm. Both halves get checked at the framing inspection, so getting them right keeps the build on schedule.

Stud size and spacing

R602.3 sizes the vertical studs in a wall and sends you to Table R602.3(5) for the limits. The two numbers a homeowner hears most are 16 inches and 24 inches on center, meaning the spacing from the center of one stud to the next. A typical exterior bearing wall uses 2x6 studs at 16 inches on center, which gives room for more insulation and carries more load. Interior nonbearing walls can use lighter framing: the code allows 2x3 studs at 24 inches on center, or flat 2x4s at 16 inches on center, as long as they are not part of a braced wall line.

The header over a window or door is part of this chapter too. A header is the beam that carries the load above an opening down to the studs on each side, and R602 sets how big it has to be for a given span.

Braced wall panels

R602.10 is the bracing rule, and it is the part inspectors scrutinize. A braced wall panel is a length of wall built to resist sideways force from wind or an earthquake. The minimum length is the number to remember:

Each braced wall panel shall be not less than 48 inches in length.

A 48-inch panel covers three stud bays at 16-inch spacing or two bays at 24-inch spacing. These panels line up along braced wall lines spaced no more than 25 feet apart in most layouts. The code lists several approved bracing methods, but the common one is wood structural panel sheathing (plywood or OSB) nailed to the studs in a set pattern.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, a one-story home in Scottsdale with lots of glass on the rear elevation has to make up the bracing it loses to those big windows somewhere else, usually with longer or continuously sheathed panels on the side walls. This is why a wall of windows facing the pool often pairs with a structural engineer's bracing plan rather than the prescriptive tables. Arizona sits in a low-seismic, moderate-wind zone, so wind governs most bracing here, not earthquakes. Two-story homes and tall great-room walls push past the simple tables fastest, and that is where an engineered design earns its cost.

Edition note

The stud-sizing tables and the 48-inch braced-panel minimum are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2024 edition reorganized and clarified the bracing tables and added detailing for tall walls, but the core panel length and spacing logic held. Which edition applies is set by your city, listed on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R602 walls sit on the foundation from R403 footings and carry the roof framed under R802 roof-ceiling framing . The wall system also matters for the stucco and insulation decisions in are stucco homes good for Arizona . For a stronger alternative to wood framing, compare with are ICF homes worth it in Arizona .

Source

Read the section text on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R602.10 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours.

title: IRC R703: Exterior Wall Covering
shortSummary: IRC R703 sets the rules for siding and stucco on an exterior wall. For traditional stucco it requires three coats over lath, a water-resistive barrier behind it, and a weep screed at the base that drains trapped water out, kept at least 4 inches above the soil.
bodyHtml:
Stucco covers most of the homes in the Valley, and Section R703 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the part of the code that says how it goes on. The rules cover any exterior wall covering, from fiber-cement siding to brick, but the stucco provisions in R703.7 are the ones that matter most here. Done right, the system sheds water and lasts decades. Done wrong, water gets trapped behind the wall and the problems hide until they are expensive.

What R703 requires

R703 has two big jobs: keep water out, and hold the covering on. Behind any covering, R703.2 requires a water-resistive barrier (a vapor barrier cousin, usually building paper or a house wrap) that acts as the drainage plane. For traditional stucco, R703.7 requires the plaster to be applied in three coats over metal or wire lath:

Plaster shall be not less than three coats where applied over metal lath or wire lath.

The three coats are the scratch coat, the brown coat, and the finish coat, applied over lath that is fastened to the framing per ASTM C1063. At the bottom of the wall, R703.7.2.1 requires a weep screed, a metal flashing that lets any water that gets behind the stucco drain back out. The code places it at least 4 inches above the earth or 2 inches above a paved surface, and the water-resistive barrier and lath have to lap over its flange so water rides down and out, not behind it.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, on a custom home in Phoenix or Fountain Hills, the wall is built up in order: sheathing, then two layers of water-resistive barrier (a common detail over wood sheathing in a dry climate), then the weep screed at the base, then lath, then the three stucco coats. The three coats together run about 7/8 inch thick over the lath. The weep screed sitting 4 inches above the dirt is the detail inspectors check closely, because builders who bury it lose the drainage path, and in monsoon season that traps water against the framing.

Arizona's dry heat is easy on stucco most of the year, but the monsoon and irrigation are where the weep screed earns its place. Sprinkler spray and splashback at the base of a wall are the real moisture source here, not driving rain. Keeping the screed clear of soil and mulch is what lets that water escape. For how stucco performs in the Valley overall, see are stucco homes good for Arizona .

Edition note

The three-coat rule, the weep screed placement, and the water-resistive barrier requirement are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition tightened the water-resistive barrier language and the wood-sheathing provisions in R703.7.3, clarifying when a second layer or a drainage gap is needed, but the core stucco assembly homeowners ask about has not changed. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

The exterior covering set here has to stay attached against the wind load from R301 design criteria and is fastened to the wall framing built under R602 . The water-resistive barrier works with the wall insulation that drives the R-value of the assembly.

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R703.7 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours. The lath and plaster details point to the ASTM C926 and C1063 standards.

title: IRC R802: Roof-Ceiling Framing
shortSummary: IRC R802 sizes the wood that holds up your roof: rafters and trusses. It sets the maximum span a given rafter can cover based on its size, the lumber, and the roof load, and it requires rafter or ceiling ties so the roof does not push the walls outward.
bodyHtml:
Section R802 of the International Residential Code (IRC) is the roof-framing rulebook. It sizes the rafters or trusses that span your house, sets how far each member can reach, and makes sure the roof does not spread the exterior walls apart. In Arizona the roof carries less snow than almost anywhere, but it bakes in heat and takes hard monsoon wind uplift, so the connections matter as much as the spans.

What R802 requires

R802 covers two ways to frame a roof: stick-framed rafters cut on site, or factory-built trusses. For rafters, the code sizes each one off span tables rather than a single number, because the right size depends on the lumber, the spacing, and the load. The rule points you straight to the tables:

Rafters shall be sized based on the rafter spans specified in Tables R802.4.1(1) through R802.4.1(8).

A heavier roof or a longer reach means a deeper rafter (a 2x10 instead of a 2x8) or tighter spacing. The second R802 job is resisting rafter thrust. A sloped rafter wants to push the top of the wall outward, so the code requires ceiling joists or rafter ties low in the attic to tie the two sides together. If those ties sit higher in the attic for a vaulted ceiling, the allowable rafter span gets cut by an adjustment factor.

Most production and custom homes in the Valley use engineered roof trusses instead of cut rafters. A truss is a pre-engineered triangle of smaller members designed by the manufacturer to a stamped layout, which speeds framing and clears long spans without interior bearing walls.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, a single-story home in Rio Verde with a low-slope roof and wide great room is a natural truss job: the trusses span wall to wall, so the interior can stay open. Low slope is common here because there is no snow to shed, but a shallow pitch leaves less attic depth, which squeezes both the framing and the room for insulation. That is the heat angle. A hot, shallow attic needs the truss layout coordinated with the insulation plan so you can still hit the required ceiling R-value without crushing the insulation at the eaves.

Monsoon wind uplift is the other Arizona driver. Roof-to-wall connections get hurricane-style metal clips so a gust does not peel the roof off. Inspectors check those clips at framing before the roof gets sheathed.

Edition note

The rafter-span framework and the rafter-tie rules are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition clarified how ceiling joists resist thrust depending on their height in the attic, which mostly affects vaulted ceilings. The span tables themselves were refined but follow the same logic. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R802 roofs land on the walls framed under R602 wall construction , and the attic it creates is vented under R806 roof ventilation . Roof framing also shapes the choice between single-story and two-story homes . For how the roof and walls handle Arizona weather together, see are stucco homes good for Arizona .

Source

title: IRC R806: Roof/Attic Ventilation
shortSummary: IRC R806 requires a vented attic to have ventilation equal to at least 1/150 of the attic floor area. That ratio can drop to 1/300 when the vents are split evenly high and low and a vapor retarder is used, which keeps hot air moving out of the attic.
bodyHtml:
Section R806 of the International Residential Code (IRC) tells you how much airflow a vented attic needs. The rule keeps hot, moist air from sitting trapped under the roof, where in Arizona it would cook the shingles from below and drive your cooling bill up. The math is a simple ratio of vent opening to attic size, and getting it right is one of the cheapest ways to make a Valley roof last.

What R806 requires

A vented attic needs net free ventilating area (the actual open area of the vents, after subtracting the screen and louver blockage) equal to a fraction of the space below it. The baseline rule is one part vent for every 150 parts of attic floor:

The minimum net free ventilating area shall be 1/150 of the area of the vented space.

The code lets you cut that in half, to 1/300, but only if you do two things. First, split the vents so that 40 to 50 percent sit high in the attic (near the ridge) and the rest sit low (at the eaves). That sets up a chimney effect that pulls air through. Second, install a vapor retarder on the warm side of the ceiling in cold climates. In Arizona's warm zones the vapor-retarder half of that exception usually does not apply, so most Valley attics are designed to the balanced high-low vent layout to earn the 1/300 ratio.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, a 2,400-square-foot home in Cave Creek with the attic following the same footprint needs roughly 16 square feet of net free vent area at 1/150, or about 8 square feet at 1/300 with balanced ridge and soffit vents. That is the difference between a roof full of box vents and a clean ridge-vent-plus-soffit setup. The heat angle is the whole point here. A poorly vented Arizona attic can run 150 degrees or hotter. That bakes the underside of the roof deck, shortens shingle life, and dumps heat into the rooms below. Moving that air out protects the roof and takes load off the air conditioner.

Ventilation works with insulation, not instead of it. The vents handle the air; the ceiling R-value handles the heat that would otherwise radiate into the house. Baffles at the eaves keep the insulation from blocking the soffit vents, which is a common framing-stage miss on low-slope roofs where attic depth is tight.

The unvented alternative

R806 also allows an unvented attic (R806.5) where the insulation is sprayed against the underside of the roof deck and the attic becomes conditioned space. This is popular in Arizona when the HVAC equipment and ducts live in the attic, because it pulls them into the cooled envelope. An unvented assembly has its own detailing rules and usually needs spray foam plus a careful vapor strategy, so it is an engineered choice, not a default.

Edition note

The 1/150 baseline and the 1/300 balanced-vent exception are stable across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The unvented-attic provisions have been refined edition to edition, mostly around insulation and vapor control, but the vented-attic ratios homeowners ask about have not changed. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

R806 ventilates the attic framed under R802 roof-ceiling framing and works alongside the insulation rules in N1102 building thermal envelope . The vapor side ties to a vapor barrier . For how a hot roof affects panel output, see is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona .

Source

title: IRC R807: Attic Access
shortSummary: IRC R807 requires an attic access opening of at least 22 by 30 inches whenever the attic is big enough to enter, meaning 30 inches of height over at least 30 square feet. The opening also needs 30 inches of headroom above it so the space can be reached and serviced.
bodyHtml:
If an attic is big enough to crawl into, the code wants a way to get into it. Section R807 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets when an attic access opening is required and how big it has to be. The reason is simple: someone has to be able to reach the attic to service the HVAC equipment, inspect the framing, run wiring, and check the insulation that does so much work in an Arizona home.

What R807 requires

The trigger is the size of the attic. Access is required when there is a combustible (wood-framed) roof or ceiling and the attic has at least 30 inches of vertical height over an area of at least 30 square feet. Below that, the space is too small to enter and no opening is required. When access is required, the opening has a minimum size:

The rough-framed opening shall be not less than 22 inches by 30 inches.

That opening has to sit in a hallway or another spot with ready access, not buried in a closet behind shelving. R807 also requires at least 30 inches of unobstructed headroom in the attic at some point above the opening, measured from the bottom of the ceiling framing, so a person can actually climb up and stand. If the access is a panel in a wall instead of the ceiling, it is 22 inches wide by 30 inches high.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, in a single-story home in Phoenix or Apache Junction with the air handler in the attic, the access panel is not a formality, it is how the HVAC tech gets to the equipment for every service call. Builders here often upsize the opening beyond the 22-by-30 minimum, sometimes to a pull-down stair, specifically because Arizona attics hold the air conditioner, the ducts, and a lot of insulation that all need to be reachable. A cramped 22-by-30 hole over a 150-degree attic makes service miserable and gets skipped.

The access opening is also a weak spot in the insulated ceiling. The hatch itself should be insulated and weather-stripped, because an uninsulated panel is a direct hole in the ceiling R-value that lets attic heat pour into the hallway below. In our climate that detail is worth getting right.

Edition note

The 22-by-30 opening size, the 30-inch height over 30 square feet trigger, and the 30-inch headroom rule are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. This section has been stable, with only minor wording cleanup between editions. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

The attic reached through this opening is framed under R802 and ventilated under R806 roof ventilation , and it usually houses the ducts governed by M1601 . For how the attic and shell affect comfort, see are stucco homes good for Arizona .

title: IRC R905: Roof Covering
shortSummary: IRC R905 sets the rules for roof coverings by type and slope. Asphalt shingles need a slope of at least 2:12, with doubled underlayment below 4:12, while clay and concrete tile need at least 2.5:12, which shapes how a low-slope Arizona roof gets covered.
bodyHtml:
A roof covering does one job: keep water out while taking the full force of the sun. Section R905 of the International Residential Code (IRC) sets the rules for each covering type, and the key gate is slope. Use a covering on a roof flatter than the code allows and water can work back under it, so R905 ties every material to a minimum pitch and to the underlayment that backs it up.

What R905 requires

R905 has a subsection for each common covering: asphalt shingles, clay and concrete tile, metal panels, and more. Each sets a minimum slope and the underlayment beneath it. For asphalt shingles, R905.2.2 draws the line:

Asphalt shingles shall be used only on roof slopes of two units vertical in 12 units horizontal (2:12) or greater.

Slope is written as rise over run, so 2:12 means the roof climbs 2 inches for every 12 inches it runs out. Below 4:12, R905.1.1 requires a doubled layer of underlayment so the shallower pitch has more backup against water. Clay and concrete tile need a steeper minimum, 2.5:12. The underlayment itself, the felt or synthetic sheet under the covering, has to meet standards like ASTM D226, D1970, or D4869 and carry a label proving it.

What it means for an Arizona custom home

For example, a custom home in Cave Creek with a low 3:12 roof and concrete tile clears the 2.5:12 tile minimum, but because it sits below 4:12 it needs doubled underlayment under the tile. That underlayment is doing more in Arizona than almost anywhere, because tile roofs here are a rain screen, not a seal. Wind-driven monsoon rain blows under the tile, and the underlayment is the real waterproof layer. A common upgrade in the Valley is a high-temperature self-adhered membrane that survives the heat under dark tile.

The sun is the other half of the story. Roof covering color and the air space under tile affect how much heat reaches the attic, which feeds back into the ceiling R-value and the cooling load. Concrete and clay tile hold up well to decades of Arizona ultraviolet, which is one reason they are so common here, while asphalt shingles in this climate wear faster and are usually chosen for budget or a specific look. For how the roof and shell affect comfort, see are stucco homes good for Arizona .

Edition note

The slope minimums and underlayment standards in R905 are consistent across the 2018, 2021, and 2024 IRC. The 2021 edition reorganized the underlayment provisions into the shared R905.1.1 and updated the referenced ASTM standards, but the 2:12 asphalt and 2.5:12 tile thresholds and the doubled-underlayment-below-4:12 rule have held. Which edition your city enforces is on the Arizona building code editions by city page.

How it connects

The covering set here sits on the roof framing built under R802 and works with the attic airflow from R806 roof ventilation , which carries off the heat the covering soaks up. For how a hot roof affects panel output, see is solar worth it on a new construction home in Arizona .

Source

Read the section on the official ICC site: 2021 IRC R905.2.2 on codes.iccsafe.org . The ICC I-Codes are copyrighted, so only the short clause above is quoted; the explanation is ours. The underlayment requirements point to the ASTM D-series standards.

title: Carefree Ranch Design Guidelines (Village of Lone Mountain): Height, Setback, and Fee Rules for a Custom Home
shortSummary: Carefree Ranch in the Village of Lone Mountain caps a custom home at 24 feet above natural grade at any point, breaks it into building masses of 16 to 17.5 feet, and sets 60-foot street and 35-foot side setbacks. You must finish the exterior within 549 days and pay $1,500 for plans plus $500 for the building envelope.
bodyHtml:
If you are building a custom home in Carefree Ranch, in the Village of Lone Mountain near Carefree, the Carefree Ranch Owners Association reviews and approves your design before you build, and its rules are detailed. The community Design Review Committee caps your home at 24 feet above natural grade, breaks it into smaller building masses, sets deep setbacks, and charges published fees. This page explains what those guidelines require, why they exist, and what they mean for a build, so you design to them up front.

This page is part of the Jematell community design guidelines library, our plain-language reading of the design rules that guard-gated and master-planned communities in our service area enforce on custom homes. These community rules sit on top of the city or county building codes, and on a hillside desert lot they are usually the tighter constraint.

What the Carefree Ranch guidelines require

The Carefree Ranch Design Guidelines control your home's height, massing, setbacks, schedule, and fees, and a Design Review Committee approves all of it first. Carefree Ranch is a custom-home community in the Village of Lone Mountain, and its design guidelines are the binding rules the committee uses. The signature rule is the sloping-height cap, which limits how tall any point of the home can stand above the original ground:

no portion of the Residence or other Improvements, except for chimneys, may exceed a height of 24 '- 0" above the existing natural grade.

Natural grade is the original ground surface before any cut or fill. So the 24-foot limit is measured straight down from each point of the house to the dirt as you found it, point by point. Here are more of the hard standards:

  • Building masses. No single building mass may exceed 16 feet to the top of a flat-roof parapet, or 17 feet 6 inches to a sloping-roof ridge. Up to one-third of the enclosed footprint may go higher, to a maximum of 19 feet flat or 20 feet 6 inches to a ridge. Each home must be broken into at least three visual masses of different heights, or four masses if it is larger than 5,000 square feet.
  • Setbacks. The general setback requirements are 60 feet from the street and 35 feet from adjacent lots. These setbacks are administered by the City of Scottsdale, so the City, not the community alone, enforces them.
  • Completion deadline. You must finish the exterior of the home (including stucco, paint, windows, doors, and all landscaping) within 549 days of starting construction, unless the committee grants an extension in writing.
  • Design review fees. The standard fees are $1,500 for plans plus $500 for the building envelope, payable to the association. Non-standard requests are billed by the association architect at an hourly rate.

When these guidelines come into play

These rules shape both the design and the schedule, so they come into play from the first site plan through the final landscaping. A few common situations:

  • You want a tall, prominent home on a knoll. Carefree Ranch is built to discourage that. The 24-foot point-by-point cap, the 16-to-17.5-foot mass limits, and the required three or four separate masses all push the house to step down and blend into the desert rather than dominate a ridge.
  • You are buying a lot and pricing the build. The 60-foot street and 35-foot side setbacks shrink the buildable area a lot, and the $1,500 plan fee plus $500 building-envelope fee are real line items on top of the City permit fees.
  • Your schedule is tight. The 549-day exterior completion clock starts when you begin construction. A long custom build with delayed landscaping can run up against it, and the association can step in to finish the exterior at your cost if you miss it.

The Design Review Committee is the community's design review committee (DRC) , and it reviews against the CC&Rs (the recorded covenants you agreed to at purchase) and these guidelines. Because the City of Scottsdale administers the setbacks and the building permit, the City is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) for the permit while the committee judges design fit.

What this means for your build

Design the home in stepped masses from the start, because the 24-foot cap and the 16-to-17.5-foot mass limits will not bend for a tall, single-volume house. On Carefree Ranch's varied terrain, the point-by-point natural-grade measurement matters. For example, on a lot that slopes 6 feet across the building pad, a roofline that reads 20 feet above grade at the high side can measure 26 feet above the natural grade at the low side, which breaks the 24-foot cap even though the house looks one consistent height. Check the grade at each point, not just at the entry. Budget the 60-foot and 35-foot setbacks into your buildable-area math before you fall for a floor plan, and put the $1,500 and $500 fees in your project budget. For how those fees compare across Arizona communities, see how much are HOA design review fees for a custom home in Arizona , and for how the 24-foot cap sits against other gated communities, how tall can a custom home be in a gated or hillside community in Arizona . Build the 549-day exterior deadline into your construction schedule, landscaping included, so a slow finish does not trigger the association's completion remedy.

This is a second approval on top of the City permit. For how that HOA review works in Arizona, including the law that governs it and the on-site inspections, see how HOA design review affects building a custom home . For the City zoning and setbacks that apply underneath these guidelines, see Carefree Residential Zoning and Setbacks , and for the codes your structure is built to, Carefree Adopted Building Codes . For the Scottsdale-area design-review system these communities share, see how HOA and community design review works in Scottsdale .

Source

HOAs revise and re-post these documents, so the version and the file location can change. This page reflects the July 2019 revision. title: FireRock Ridge Design Guidelines (Fountain Hills): Height, Roof, and Color Rules for a Custom Home
shortSummary: FireRock Ridge in Fountain Hills holds custom homes to single-story or split-level plans, no wall or roofline over 20 feet above natural grade, a 5/12 maximum roof slope, paint colors at 50% LRV or lower, and a 2,500 square foot minimum livable area. A community committee approves your plans before you build.
bodyHtml:
If you are building a custom home in FireRock Ridge in Fountain Hills, a community committee approves your exterior design before the town will let you start, and its rules are stricter than the town code. FireRock Ridge publishes Architectural Guidelines and Standards that cap your home at 20 feet tall, limit your roof slope and color, control your paint, and set a minimum house size. This page explains what those guidelines require, why they exist, and what they mean for a build, so you design to them from the first sketch instead of reworking plans later.

This page is part of the Jematell community design guidelines library, our plain-language reading of the design rules that guard-gated and master-planned communities in our service area enforce on custom homes. These community rules sit on top of the city building codes, and they are usually the tighter constraint.

What the FireRock Ridge guidelines require

The FireRock Ridge guidelines control the height, shape, roof, and color of every new home, and a design committee must approve your plans first. FireRock Ridge is a community in Fountain Hills, and its design guidelines are the binding rules its committee uses to review custom homes. The biggest single rule is height. The document limits homes to single-story or split-level plans and caps how tall any part of the house can stand:

Homes within FireRock Ridge are limited to single story or split level plans that follow natural topography... with no single wall plane or ridgeline to exceed 20 feet above natural grade.

Natural grade is the original ground level before any soil, paving, or decking is added. So the 20-foot limit is measured from the dirt as you found it, not from a raised pad. A few more of the hard rules from the guidelines:

  • Roofs. The maximum pitched-roof slope is 5/12 (5 inches of rise for every 12 inches of run), and the roof material reflectance cannot exceed 50 percent. Red "Mexican tile" or "barrel tile" roofs are not permitted. White roofs are to be avoided.
  • Paint color. House paint must have a Light Reflective Value, or LRV, of 50 percent or less, in subdued desert colors. LRV is a 0-to-100 scale for how much light a color bounces back, so a 50 percent cap rules out bright or pale walls. Each color must be submitted with the manufacturer, color name, LRV, and two paint chips for approval.
  • Garages. Carports are not allowed, and a fully enclosed two-bay garage is required. Garage doors must be sectional metal roll-up doors, recessed at least 16 inches from the face of the wall. Wood overhead garage doors are not permitted.
  • Minimum house size. Livable area, not counting garages, patios, or porches, must be at least 2,500 square feet.
  • Setbacks. Building setbacks follow the approved zoning district, so the Town of Fountain Hills yard rules still apply underneath the community guidelines.

When these guidelines come into play

These rules shape the home before the floor plan is final, so they come into play the moment you start designing on a FireRock Ridge lot. A few common situations:

  • You want a two-story home. FireRock Ridge does not allow it. The single-story or split-level rule and the 20-foot cap mean a tall, stacked design is off the table, so the square footage has to spread across the lot instead of up.
  • You picked a roof tile you love. If it is red barrel tile, or a glossy material over 50 percent reflectance, the committee will reject it. Roof color and material both get reviewed against the approved list.
  • You chose a bold exterior color. A bright white, deep black, or saturated accent wall will likely fail the 50 percent LRV limit. The palette runs to subdued desert browns, beiges, and grays.

The committee reviews your plans against the CC&Rs (the recorded covenants, conditions, and restrictions you agreed to when you bought the lot) and these guidelines. That committee is a design review committee (DRC) , and it, not the Town, is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) for design fit, while the Town still issues the building permit.

What this means for your build

Design to the 20-foot height cap and the single-story rule from day one, because they decide the whole massing of the house. On a Fountain Hills lot with slope, the natural-grade measurement matters. For example, say your lot drops 4 feet from the street side to the back. A roofline that sits 18 feet above grade at the front can measure 22 feet above the natural grade at the downhill wall, which fails the 20-foot rule even though the house looks the same height throughout. The grade has to be checked at each wall, not just at the front door. Pick your roof slope, roof material, and paint colors against the guidelines before you fall in love with a look, since the committee can send any of them back. For how these numbers compare to other luxury communities, see how tall can a custom home be in a gated or hillside community in Arizona , what roof types and slopes do luxury Arizona communities allow , and what are LRV paint color rules in North Scottsdale luxury communities . Plan for the 2,500 square foot minimum and the recessed two-bay garage as fixed inputs, not options.

Remember this is a second approval on top of the town permit. For how that HOA review works in Arizona, including the law that governs it and the on-site inspections, see how HOA design review affects building a custom home . For the town zoning that sets your setbacks underneath these guidelines, see Fountain Hills Residential Zoning and Setbacks , and for the codes your structure is built to, Fountain Hills Adopted Building Codes . For the Scottsdale-area version of this design-review system, see how HOA and community design review works in Scottsdale .

Source

The height, roof, color, garage, and house-size rules above come from the FireRock Ridge Architectural Guidelines and Standards, Revised 7/2022, published through the community's managing agent: View the FireRock Ridge Architectural Guidelines (PDF) . The FireRock Ridge guidelines are a private, copyrighted association document, quoted here only briefly for accuracy; read the full guidelines at the source.

HOAs revise and re-post these documents, so the version and the file location can change. This page reflects the 7/2022 revision. Confirm the current guideline version with the FireRock Ridge design committee or its managing agent before you rely on any number for your plans.

title: How HOA and Community Design Review Works for a Scottsdale-Area Custom Home: ESL, Native Plant Ordinance, and Building Envelopes
shortSummary: Building a custom home in a Scottsdale-area gated community means clearing two reviews: the City of Scottsdale's Environmentally Sensitive Lands (ESL) ordinance, which forces 15% to 80% of your lot into permanent Natural Area Open Space, plus its Native Plant Ordinance, and your community's own Design Review Committee. This page explains how both layers work together.
bodyHtml:
Two separate design reviews stand between a Scottsdale-area lot and a broken-ground permit, and clearing one does not clear the other. The City of Scottsdale reviews your lot against its Environmentally Sensitive Lands (ESL) ordinance and its Native Plant Ordinance, which together decide how much of the lot you can touch and what desert you must keep. Then your community's own Design Review Committee (DRC) reviews the look of the home against its private guidelines. This page explains how both layers work, so you know which rule comes from the City and which comes from your HOA before you design.

This page is the hub for the Jematell community design guidelines library. The library covers the design rules that guard-gated and master-planned communities in our service area enforce on custom homes. Examples include FireRock Ridge in Fountain Hills , Troon North in north Scottsdale , and Carefree Ranch in the Village of Lone Mountain . Those community rules sit on top of the City framework explained here. On a north Scottsdale desert lot, the two layers together define the home before you draw a wall.

The City layer: ESL, NAOS, and native plants

The City of Scottsdale's first design control is the Environmentally Sensitive Lands (ESL) ordinance, a zoning overlay that protects desert and mountain land. The City states its purpose plainly:

The intent and purpose of the ESL is to identify and protect environmentally sensitive lands in the City and to promote public health and safety by managing development on these lands.

The ESL covers 134 square miles of desert and mountain land north and east of the Central Arizona Project canal. That is exactly where most north Scottsdale custom homes get built. Its core tool is Natural Area Open Space (NAOS). NAOS is land that must stay in its natural state for good. The required NAOS rises with the landform and the slope. It runs from about 15 percent on flat lower desert, to 20 percent on upper desert, up to as much as 80 percent on steep hillside. The steeper and more sensitive the ground, the more of it you must leave alone. That preserved area is usually recorded as a Natural Area Easement over your lot. The easement is a legal interest that locks the desert in place. The part of the lot you can still build on is your building envelope.

On top of the ESL, Scottsdale runs a Native Plant Ordinance (City Code, Chapter 46, Article V). It protects large native cacti and trees, including saguaros, across the whole city. You need a native plant permit to remove, relocate, or destroy a protected plant. The City will only issue that permit as part of an approved development project. So on a typical lot you inventory the protected plants first. Then you preserve or transplant them under the permit instead of clearing them.

The community layer: each gated community's own DRC

The second design control is your community's own committee. The City does not run it. Most guard-gated Scottsdale-area communities operate an independent Design Review Committee (DRC) , also called an Architectural Review Committee. The DRC reviews the style of your home against private guidelines. It looks at height, roof form and color, wall and stucco materials, paint colors, walls and fences, and where the home sits in its envelope. Arizona law, A.R.S. 33-1817, sets out how this HOA review must run in a planned community. It requires a final design approval meeting, a security deposit held in trust, and at least two on-site reviews during construction.

Several of north Scottsdale's most exclusive communities run their own DRC this way, each with its own guidelines:

  • Desert Mountain reviews every new home through its Architectural Review Committee, under an "organic architecture" philosophy in the Frank Lloyd Wright tradition.
  • Silverleaf and DC Ranch run one of the strictest reviews in Arizona, examining paver lines, plant species, hardscape proportions, and lighting fixtures against a native Sonoran palette.
  • Estancia requires dual approval: its own DRC plus the City of Scottsdale.
  • Mirabel reviews custom homes through its design committee, with saguaro-protection and natural-drainage rules.
  • Whisper Rock Estates reviews custom homes in its golf community through its own committee.

Each of these communities controls its own numbers through its private guidelines, which it gives only to members. You do not need their documents to understand the structure. The City handles the land and the desert through ESL and the Native Plant Ordinance. The community handles the look of the home through its DRC.

What this means for your build

Treat the two reviews as two separate hurdles. Clearing one does not clear the other. First, before you buy, check the lot's ESL landform and slope. That lets you estimate the NAOS percentage and the real building envelope. It matters because two same-size lots can have very different buildable areas once NAOS is applied. For example, take a one-acre upper-desert lot at a 45 percent NAOS requirement. It leaves under half an acre to hold the house, garage, driveway, pool, and graded yard. Second, inventory the protected native plants early. That way the native plant permit and any transplanting are planned, not a surprise. Third, get your community's current DRC guidelines and design to them. The community height, color, and material rules are usually tighter than the City code.

The City is the authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) for the permit and the ESL. Your community DRC judges design fit. The preserved desert is held by a Natural Area Easement , and it stacks with your normal setbacks . For how the recorded envelope and NAOS easement play out on a single lot, see what is a building envelope or NAOS easement on an Arizona custom lot , for the construction fence that protects the NAOS during the build, why do gated communities require a construction disturbance or NAOS fence in Arizona , and for what the DRC charges to review your plans, how much are HOA design review fees for a custom home in Arizona . For how the HOA review works under Arizona law, see how HOA design review affects building a custom home . For the City code details, see the Scottsdale Environmentally Sensitive Lands Ordinance and Scottsdale Residential Zoning and Setbacks .

Source

The ESL purpose, the NAOS requirement, and the covered area are published by the City of Scottsdale at Environmentally Sensitive Lands Overlay on scottsdaleaz.gov . The Native Plant Ordinance is at Native Plant Ordinance Information , and the full code index is at Codes and Ordinances . The Arizona law governing HOA design review is A.R.S. 33-1817 . These City ordinances are public records.

This page is general information, not a zoning determination. Overlay boundaries, NAOS percentages, and each community's private guidelines depend on the specific parcel and HOA, so confirm your lot's ESL requirement with Scottsdale Planning and Development and your community's current guidelines with its Design Review Committee before you rely on a number.

  • City of Scottsdale Native Plant Ordinance Information
  • City of Scottsdale Codes and Ordinances
  • A.R.S. 33-1817 (Design, architectural committees; review)
